topics.title: Overview of Machine Learning
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Machine Learning

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Learning (ML)** is a sub-field of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** that enables computational systems to learn patterns and relationships from data and subsequently improve their performance on a specific task **without being explicitly programmed** for every rule.

The two canonical textbook definitions used in KTU valuation key answers are:

**Arthur Samuel (1959):**
> *"Machine Learning is the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."*

**Tom Mitchell (1997) — The Formal Definition:**
> *"A computer program is said to learn from experience **E** with respect to some class of tasks **T** and performance measure **P**, if its performance at tasks in **T**, as measured by **P**, improves with experience **E**."*

This triplet **(T, P, E)** is known as the **Learning Problem Formulation** and is a high-yield KTU short-answer point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Teaching a Child vs Programming

| Aspect | Traditional Programming | Machine Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Data + Rules (Program) | Data + Expected Outputs |
| **Process** | Rule Engine executes logic | Algorithm discovers the rules |
| **Output** | Answers | Rules (Model) + Answers |

**Analogy:** Imagine teaching a toddler to recognize a *cat*.
- **Traditional Programming:** You would write thousands of `if-else` rules (four legs, whiskers, meows...). This is brittle and incomplete.
- **Machine Learning:** You simply *show* the toddler 1,000 pictures labeled "Cat" and 1,000 pictures labeled "Not-Cat". The child (model) figures out the distinguishing features automatically.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** ML *replaces the programmer-written rules* with *statistical patterns inferred from data*. The "learning" is essentially **parameter optimization** that minimizes prediction error.

## 1.3 Core Terminology You MUST Memorize

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Glossary for KTU ESE (End Semester Exam):**
> - **Feature (x):** An individual measurable property of the observed phenomenon (a column in the dataset).
> - **Label / Target (y):** The output variable we are trying to predict — the "ground truth".
> - **Training Set:** The subset of data used to *fit* (teach) the model.
> - **Testing Set:** The held-out subset used to *evaluate* generalization.
> - **Hypothesis (h):** The model's learned mapping function, $h: X \rightarrow Y$.
> - **Model Parameters ($\theta$):** The internal weights/coefficients that the learning algorithm adjusts.
> - **Loss Function (L):** A scalar metric that quantifies how wrong the model's predictions are.
> - **Epoch:** One complete pass of the entire training dataset through the learner.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Paradigms of Machine Learning

Machine Learning is broadly classified into **four paradigms**, each answering a different kind of business or scientific question.

### 2.1.1 Supervised Learning
The dataset contains **labeled examples** $(x_i, y_i)$. The algorithm learns the input-output mapping.
- **Classification:** Predicting a discrete category (Spam vs Ham, Disease vs Healthy).
- **Regression:** Predicting a continuous value (House price, Temperature).

### 2.1.2 Unsupervised Learning
The dataset contains **only inputs** $x_i$, no labels. The algorithm discovers intrinsic structure.
- **Clustering:** Grouping similar customers (K-Means, DBSCAN).
- **Dimensionality Reduction:** Compressing features while preserving variance (PCA).
- **Association:** Market-basket analysis (Apriori).

### 2.1.3 Semi-Supervised Learning
A **hybrid** approach where the dataset has a small amount of labeled data and a large amount of unlabeled data. Common in medical imaging where expert labeling is expensive.

### 2.1.4 Reinforcement Learning (RL)
An **agent** learns by interacting with an **environment**, receiving **rewards** or **penalties** for actions. Goal: maximize cumulative reward.
- Examples: Game-playing AI (AlphaGo), Robotics, Self-driving cars.

## 2.2 The Generic ML Pipeline

The end-to-end engineering workflow consists of **seven sequential stages**:

1. **Data Collection** — Sourcing raw data from databases, sensors, or the web.
2. **Data Preprocessing** — Cleaning missing values, handling outliers, normalizing scales.
3. **Feature Engineering** — Selecting, extracting, or transforming informative variables.
4. **Model Selection** — Choosing an algorithm family (e.g., Decision Tree, SVM, Neural Net).
5. **Training** — Optimizing parameters $\theta$ to minimize the loss on training data.
6. **Evaluation** — Testing on unseen data using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall.
7. **Deployment & Monitoring** — Integrating into production (MLOps) and tracking drift.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real KTU-aligned industry projects, roughly **80% of an ML engineer's time** is spent on Steps 1–3 (data preparation). The "fancy algorithm" is often the smallest component.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Overview Module

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Mitchell's Learning Task** | $(T, P, E)$ | Task, Performance metric, Experience tuple |
| **Hypothesis Mapping** | $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$ | Linear model prediction |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Regression loss |
| **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Robust regression loss |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L = -\sum_{i} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Classification loss |
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Classification metric |
| **Precision** | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| **Recall** | $\text{Rec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| **Empirical Risk** | $R_{emp}(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} L(h_\theta(x_i), y_i)$ | Average training loss |
| **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** | $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Decomposition of test error |
| **Train-Test Split Ratio** | $70:30$ or $80:20$ | Standard empirical convention |

## 2.4 Why ML Matters in Modern Engineering

- **Automation of High-Dimensional Decision Making:** Tasks like fraud detection involve thousands of features that no human can manually encode.
- **Adaptability:** Models retrain on fresh data to handle concept drift.
- **Scalability:** A single trained model serves millions of requests per second (e.g., recommendation engines at Netflix/Amazon).
- **Scientific Discovery:** Protein folding (AlphaFold), genomics, and climate modeling rely on ML to find patterns humans cannot see.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic is an *Overview*, the derivations below focus on **the formal mathematical formulation of the learning problem**, the **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) principle**, and a **worked numerical example** demonstrating the hypothesis-loss-evaluation cycle that defines all supervised ML algorithms.

## 3.1 Derivation: The Formal Learning Problem

**Step 1 — Define the Data Universe.**
We are given a dataset $D$ consisting of $n$ independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) samples drawn from some unknown joint probability distribution $P(X, Y)$.

$$D = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}$$

where each $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is a $d$-dimensional feature vector and $y_i \in \mathbb{R}$ is the corresponding target label.

**Step 2 — Define the Hypothesis Class.**
We restrict the search to a family of functions parametrized by $\theta \in \Theta$, called the **hypothesis class** $\mathcal{H}$.

$$\mathcal{H} = \{h_\theta : X \rightarrow Y \mid \theta \in \Theta\}$$

For example, the linear hypothesis class is:

$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \theta_d x_d = \theta^T x$$

**Step 3 — Define the Loss Function.**
The loss $L(\hat{y}, y)$ quantifies the penalty for predicting $\hat{y}$ when the truth is $y$.

$$\text{Squared Loss: } L(\hat{y}, y) = (y - \hat{y})^2$$

**Step 4 — Define the Expected Risk.**
The true performance of a hypothesis is the **expected loss** over the entire data distribution:

$$R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim P} [L(h_\theta(x), y)]$$

Since the true distribution $P(X, Y)$ is **unknown**, we cannot compute $R(\theta)$ directly. This is the central challenge of ML.

**Step 5 — Approximate via Empirical Risk.**
Because we only have a finite sample $D$, we replace the expectation with a sample average — this is the **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** principle:

$$R_{emp}(\theta) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(h_\theta(x_i), y_i)$$

**Step 6 — The Learning Objective.**
The learning algorithm solves the optimization problem:

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} R_{emp}(\theta)$$

The optimal $\hat{\theta}$ defines the best hypothesis $h_{\hat{\theta}}$ that the algorithm has learned from the data.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A Linear Regression Overview

Consider a **single-feature** toy dataset for predicting a student's exam score $y$ from study hours $x$:

| $i$ | Hours ($x_i$) | Score ($y_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 3 | 6 |

**Step 1 — Choose Hypothesis Class.**
We use a simple linear model:

$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$

**Step 2 — Pick Initial Parameters.**
Let $\theta_0 = 0$ and $\theta_1 = 1$. The initial hypothesis is $h(x) = x$.

**Step 3 — Compute Predictions.**
$\hat{y}_1 = 0 + 1(1) = 1$
$\hat{y}_2 = 0 + 1(2) = 2$
$\hat{y}_3 = 0 + 1(3) = 3$

**Step 4 — Compute Squared Losses.**
$L_1 = (2 - 1)^2 = 1$
$L_2 = (4 - 2)^2 = 4$
$L_3 = (6 - 3)^2 = 9$

**Step 5 — Compute Empirical Risk (MSE).**
Summing the individual losses:

$$R_{emp}(\theta) = \frac{1}{3}(1 + 4 + 9) = \frac{14}{3} \approx 4.67$$

**Step 6 — Update Parameters (Conceptual Gradient Descent Step).**
The gradient of MSE with respect to $\theta_1$ is:

$$\frac{\partial R_{emp}}{\partial \theta_1} = -\frac{2}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)$$

Plugging values:

$$\frac{\partial R_{emp}}{\partial \theta_1} = -\frac{2}{3}[(1)(2-1) + (2)(4-2) + (3)(6-3)]$$

$$= -\frac{2}{3}[1 + 4 + 9] = -\frac{28}{3}$$

Updating with a small learning rate $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$\theta_1^{new} = 1 - (0.05)\left(-\frac{28}{3}\right) = 1 + 0.467 \approx 1.467$$

**Step 7 — Verify Improvement.**
With $\theta_1 = 1.467$, the new predictions are $1.467, 2.933, 4.400$ and the new MSE drops to $\approx 1.42$. The model has **learned** — the loss decreased from 4.67 to 1.42.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Conclusion:** This numerical example demonstrates the canonical ML loop — *predict → measure loss → update parameters → repeat*. Every supervised algorithm (linear regression, logistic regression, neural networks) follows this exact ERM blueprint.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level ML Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Data Sources] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering]
    C --> D[Train Test Split 80 20]
    D --> E[Model Training]
    E --> F[Model Evaluation]
    F --> G{Performance Acceptable?}
    G -- No --> H[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    H --> E
    G -- Yes --> I[Deployment]
    I --> J[Monitoring and Maintenance]
    J --> B
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Machine Learning Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ML[Machine Learning] --> SL[Supervised Learning]
    ML --> UL[Unsupervised Learning]
    ML --> SSL[Semi Supervised Learning]
    ML --> RL[Reinforcement Learning]

    SL --> CLS[Classification]
    SL --> REG[Regression]
    CLS --> CLSex[SVM, Decision Tree, KNN, Neural Nets]
    REG --> REGex[Linear Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost]

    UL --> CLU[Clustering]
    UL --> DR[Dim Reduction]
    UL --> ASR[Association Rules]
    CLU --> CLUex[K Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical]
    DR --> DRex[PCA, t SNE, LDA]

    SSL --> SSLex[Self Training, Co Training]

    RL --> RLex[Q Learning, Deep RL, Policy Gradient]
```

## 4.3 Supervised Learning Pipeline — Detailed View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ID[Input Features x] --> HF[Hypothesis Function h theta x]
    TH[Model Parameters theta] --> HF
    HF --> PR[Predictions y hat]
    PR --> LF[Loss Function L]
    TL[True Labels y] --> LF
    LF --> ER[Empirical Risk R emp]
    ER --> OPT[Optimizer Gradient Descent]
    OPT --> UP[Update Parameters theta]
    UP --> TH
```

## 4.4 Bias-Variance Tradeoff — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[Underfitting] -->|Increase Model Complexity| M[Good Fit]
    M -->|Excessive Complexity| O[Overfitting]
    U --> HL[High Bias Low Variance]
    O --> HV[High Variance Low Bias]
    M --> LB[Low Bias Low Variance]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Machine Learning. State and explain Tom Mitchell's formal definition with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Machine Learning is a branch of Artificial Intelligence that enables systems to learn from data and improve their performance over time without being explicitly programmed.
**Mitchell's Definition:** A computer program is said to learn from experience **E** with respect to some class of tasks **T** and performance measure **P**, if its performance at tasks in **T**, as measured by **P**, improves with experience **E**.
**Example:** In a spam-email classifier, the task **T** is to mark incoming mail as spam or not-spam; the experience **E** is the training dataset of previously labeled emails; and the performance measure **P** is the classification accuracy on a held-out test set. As more labeled emails are observed, the accuracy **P** improves.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning with one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Paradigm | Data Type | Goal | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Supervised** | Labeled $(x, y)$ | Learn input-output mapping | Email spam classification using pre-labeled inbox data |
| **Unsupervised** | Unlabeled $x$ | Discover hidden structure / groupings | Customer segmentation using K-Means clustering on purchase data |
| **Reinforcement** | No fixed dataset; agent-environment interaction | Maximize cumulative reward | A chess engine learning by playing millions of self-games and receiving +1/-1 rewards |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**a) Explain in detail the different types of Machine Learning paradigms with examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
The four major paradigms of ML are:

**1. Supervised Learning:** The algorithm is trained on a dataset where each example is paired with a correct label. The goal is to learn a mapping function $h: X \rightarrow Y$ such that $h(x) \approx y$ for unseen data. It is divided into:
- **Classification** — discrete output. Examples: image recognition, disease diagnosis. Algorithms: SVM, Decision Trees, KNN.
- **Regression** — continuous output. Examples: predicting stock prices, temperature forecasting. Algorithms: Linear Regression, Random Forest.

**2. Unsupervised Learning:** The algorithm receives only inputs with no labels and must discover inherent structure. Sub-types:
- **Clustering** (K-Means, DBSCAN) — grouping similar customers for targeted marketing.
- **Dimensionality Reduction** (PCA, t-SNE) — compressing high-dimensional data for visualization.
- **Association Rule Mining** (Apriori) — discovering "customers who buy bread also buy butter".

**3. Semi-Supervised Learning:** Combines a small labeled set with a large unlabeled set. Used when labeling is expensive (e.g., medical imaging where expert radiologists must annotate scans). Self-training algorithms iteratively pseudo-label the unlabeled data.

**4. Reinforcement Learning:** An agent interacts with an environment, taking actions $a_t$ and receiving rewards $r_t$. The goal is to learn a **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ that maximizes cumulative discounted reward $G = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r_t$. Examples include game-playing agents (AlphaGo) and autonomous vehicle navigation.

---

**b) Describe the steps involved in a typical Machine Learning project pipeline with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
The end-to-end ML pipeline consists of seven iterative stages:

1. **Problem Definition:** Identify whether the problem is classification, regression, or clustering. Define the success metric (accuracy, RMSE, F1-score).

2. **Data Collection:** Gather data from databases, APIs, IoT sensors, or web scraping. Quantity and quality of data directly determine model performance.

3. **Data Preprocessing:** Handle missing values (imputation or removal), remove duplicates, treat outliers using IQR or Z-score methods, and normalize/standardize features (Min-Max scaling or Z-score normalization).

4. **Feature Engineering:** Select relevant features, encode categorical variables (one-hot or label encoding), and create derived features (polynomial features, interaction terms).

5. **Train-Test Split:** Partition the dataset, typically in an 80:20 or 70:30 ratio. Optionally include a validation set for hyperparameter tuning.

6. **Model Training & Evaluation:** Fit multiple candidate models, evaluate using appropriate metrics, and tune hyperparameters via Grid Search or Random Search.

7. **Deployment & Monitoring:** Serialize the model (e.g., using PMML or ONNX), deploy via REST API or containerized microservices, and monitor for data drift and performance degradation over time.

**Block Diagram:** *(refer to Section 4.1 above for the Mermaid flowchart)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**a) Explain the concept of Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM). Derive the expression for the empirical risk under squared loss. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
In supervised learning, the true underlying distribution $P(X, Y)$ is unknown. The goal is to find a hypothesis $h_\theta \in \mathcal{H}$ that minimizes the **expected risk**:

$$R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P}[L(h_\theta(x), y)]$$

Since $P(X, Y)$ is inaccessible, we approximate the expectation using the empirical average over the finite training dataset $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$. This approximation is called the **Empirical Risk**:

$$R_{emp}(\theta) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(h_\theta(x_i), y_i)$$

The **ERM principle** states that the optimal parameters are those that minimize this empirical average:

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} R_{emp}(\theta)$$

**Derivation for Squared Loss:**
Using the squared loss $L(\hat{y}, y) = (y - \hat{y})^2$ and a linear hypothesis $h_\theta(x_i) = \theta^T x_i$, we substitute:

$$R_{emp}(\theta) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \theta^T x_i)^2$$

Expanding the square:

$$= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i^2 - 2 y_i \theta^T x_i + (\theta^T x_i)^2)$$

Separating into scalar components:

$$= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2 - \frac{2}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \theta^T x_i + \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\theta^T x_i)^2$$

Setting the gradient with respect to $\theta$ to zero gives the **Normal Equation**:

$$\theta^* = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$$

which is the closed-form ERM solution for linear regression.

---

**b) With a suitable example, explain the concepts of features, labels, training set, test set, hypothesis, and loss function. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
Consider the problem of **predicting house prices** based on area, number of bedrooms, and location.

- **Features ($x$):** The measurable input attributes. Here, $x = [\text{area}, \text{bedrooms}, \text{location\_score}]$. Each house is a feature vector $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^3$.

- **Label ($y$):** The target variable to be predicted — in this case, the house price in lakhs of rupees.

- **Training Set:** A subset (say 80%) of labeled houses used to *fit* the model parameters, e.g., 800 of 1000 houses.

- **Test Set:** The remaining 200 houses, kept aside and not seen during training, used to *evaluate* the model's generalization ability.

- **Hypothesis ($h_\theta$):** The model's learned predictive function. For linear regression, $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \theta_3 x_3$.

- **Loss Function ($L$):** Measures the prediction error on a single example. The squared loss is $L = (y_i - h_\theta(x_i))^2$.

**Training Loop:** The algorithm iteratively adjusts $\theta$ to minimize the average loss on the training set. After convergence, the hypothesis is evaluated on the test set. If the test loss is acceptably low, the model is deployed; if it is much higher than the training loss, the model has **overfit** and regularization techniques (L1, L2) are required.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "T, P, E" definitions** in Mitchell's formulation — many students write the *performance metric* as the *task*. Memorize: **T = Task, P = Performance measure, E = Experience**.
> 2. **Swapping examples** — writing K-Means under supervised learning is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. Always state the algorithm AND its category.
> 3. **Forgetting the ERM formula** — in derivation questions, students often write $R(\theta)$ instead of $R_{emp}(\theta)$. The examiner strictly checks the **empirical** approximation step.
> 4. **No diagram in pipeline questions** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates a neat block diagram for 7-mark pipeline questions. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Bias and Variance** — High bias implies underfitting (model too simple), high variance implies overfitting (model too complex). Reversing these definitions is a common pitfall.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1 Overview:**
> - **ML Definition:** Arthur Samuel (informal) + Tom Mitchell's $(T, P, E)$ formal triplet.
> - **Four Paradigms:** Supervised, Unsupervised, Semi-Supervised, Reinforcement — with one example each.
> - **Supervised Sub-types:** Classification (discrete) vs Regression (continuous).
> - **Unsupervised Sub-types:** Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction, Association.
> - **Core Terminology:** Feature $x$, Label $y$, Hypothesis $h_\theta$, Loss $L$, Empirical Risk $R_{emp}$.
> - **ERM Principle:** $\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_\theta \frac{1}{n}\sum L(h_\theta(x_i), y_i)$.
> - **Pipeline Stages:** 7 steps from problem definition to deployment monitoring.
> - **Bias-Variance:** High bias = underfit, High variance = overfit.
> - **Evaluation Metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, MSE, MAE, RMSE.
> - **Train-Test Split:** Standard 80:20 or 70:30 convention.
> - **Closed-form Linear Regression:** $\theta^* = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$.
> - **Key Insight:** ML replaces hand-coded rules with statistically learned patterns from data.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning Paradigms - Supervised, Semi-Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning Paradigms - Supervised, Semi-Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Machine Learning Paradigm?

A **Machine Learning (ML) Paradigm** is a fundamental framework that defines how an algorithm learns from data. It specifies the type of data exposure (labeled, unlabeled, or interactive feedback), the nature of the inference task (prediction, grouping, or sequential decision-making), and the mathematical optimization principle guiding the model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Machine learning paradigms are classified based on the **nature of the supervisory signal** available during training. The four canonical paradigms are: **Supervised**, **Unsupervised**, **Semi-Supervised**, and **Reinforcement Learning**.

---

### 1.2 Supervised Learning

**Formal Definition:**
Supervised Learning is the paradigm in which the model is trained on a dataset consisting of input-output pairs $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where each input $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is mapped to a known target label $y_i$. The objective is to learn a function $f_\theta: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that minimizes a predefined loss function over the training distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Canonical Sub-Tasks:**
> - **Classification:** Discrete target labels $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ (e.g., spam vs. ham email).
> - **Regression:** Continuous target labels $y \in \mathbb{R}$ (e.g., predicting house prices).

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):**
Imagine a student preparing for an exam using a **solved question paper**. Every question comes with a verified answer key. The student learns the mapping from "question" $\rightarrow$ "answer". Once trained, the student can solve new, unseen questions. This is exactly supervised learning: the **teacher (supervisor)** provides the correct answers for every training example.

---

### 1.3 Unsupervised Learning

**Formal Definition:**
Unsupervised Learning operates on datasets containing only inputs $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with no corresponding output labels. The model must discover latent structure, patterns, or compact representations without any external supervisory signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Sub-Tasks:**
> - **Clustering:** Grouping similar instances (e.g., K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical).
> - **Dimensionality Reduction:** Compressing high-dimensional data (e.g., PCA, t-SNE, Autoencoders).
> - **Density Estimation:** Modeling the data distribution $P(X)$.

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):**
Picture a toddler given a **bucket of mixed LEGO bricks** without instructions. Without anyone telling them "this is a wheel" or "this is a brick", they naturally sort pieces by shape, color, or size based on inherent similarities. Unsupervised learning mimics this **self-discovery of structure**.

---

### 1.4 Semi-Supervised Learning

**Formal Definition:**
Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL) is a hybrid paradigm where the training set contains a **small amount of labeled data** $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{\ell}$ combined with a **large amount of unlabeled data** $\{x_j\}_{j=\ell+1}^{\ell+u}$. The model exploits the underlying data distribution of the unlabeled set to improve learning accuracy beyond what could be achieved from the labeled subset alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Assumption:** Data points that are **close to each other** in feature space are likely to share the same label (Cluster Assumption), or the data lies approximately on a **low-dimensional manifold** (Manifold Assumption).

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):**
Consider a medical diagnosis scenario where a radiologist labels only **10% of X-ray images** due to time constraints. The remaining 90% remain unlabeled, but they still contain rich structural information (edges, textures, organ shapes). The model learns from the few labeled examples and leverages the visual coherence of the unlabeled pile. SSL is **cost-effective** when labeling is expensive.

---

### 1.5 Reinforcement Learning

**Formal Definition:**
Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a paradigm where an **agent** learns an optimal **policy** $\pi(a|s)$ by interacting with a dynamic **environment**. At each time step $t$, the agent observes state $s_t$, selects action $a_t$, receives a reward $r_t$, and transitions to a new state $s_{t+1}$. The objective is to maximize the expected cumulative discounted reward.

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):**
Training a **pet dog** is the classic RL analogy. The dog (agent) performs tricks (actions) in a park (environment). When it sits on command, you give it a treat (reward). Over time, the dog learns the optimal sequence of actions (policy) to maximize treats. There is no labeled "correct action" provided beforehand — only delayed rewards.

---

### 1.6 Comparative Snapshot

| Feature | Supervised | Unsupervised | Semi-Supervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Type** | Labeled | Unlabeled | Mixed (mostly unlabeled) | Interactive Trajectories |
| **Feedback Signal** | Ground-truth labels | None | Partial labels + structure | Scalar reward |
| **Main Goal** | Predict $y$ from $x$ | Discover hidden patterns | Improve with cheap unlabeled data | Maximize cumulative reward |
| **Examples** | SVM, CNN, Decision Trees | K-Means, PCA, Autoencoders | Self-Training, Co-Training | Q-Learning, DQN, PPO |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Mathematical Foundation

Every ML paradigm can be cast as an optimization problem:

$$\min_{\theta} \; \mathcal{L}(\theta)$$

where $\theta$ represents the model parameters and $\mathcal{L}$ is a measure of model quality. What differs across paradigms is **how $\mathcal{L}$ is constructed** and **what signal drives the gradient updates**.

---

### 2.2 Supervised Learning — Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)

The foundational principle is **Empirical Risk Minimization**, formalized by Vapnik & Chervonenkis. Instead of minimizing the true (unknown) risk $R(f) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim P}[\ell(f(x), y)]$, we minimize its empirical estimate over the training set:

$$\hat{R}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1:** Collect labeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ sampled i.i.d. from joint distribution $P(X, Y)$.
- **Step 2:** Choose a hypothesis class $\mathcal{F}$ (e.g., linear functions, neural networks).
- **Step 3:** Define a loss function $\mathcal{L}$ that quantifies prediction error.
- **Step 4:** Solve $\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \hat{R}(f_\theta)$ using gradient descent.
- **Step 5:** Apply regularization to control model complexity and prevent overfitting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works:** By the **Law of Large Numbers**, as $N \to \infty$, the empirical risk $\hat{R}$ converges to the true risk $R$. The model thus approximates the optimal Bayes classifier.

---

### 2.3 Unsupervised Learning — Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

For unsupervised tasks like density estimation, the objective is to find parameters $\theta$ that make the observed data most probable:

$$\theta^* = \arg\max_{\theta} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_\theta(x_i)$$

Equivalently, we **minimize the negative log-likelihood**:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \left[ -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P_\theta(x_i) \right]$$

For clustering algorithms like **K-Means**, the objective is:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \vert\vert x - \mu_k \vert\vert_2^2$$

where $\mu_k$ is the centroid of cluster $C_k$. The algorithm alternates between (a) assigning points to nearest centroid and (b) recomputing centroids — an instance of the **Expectation-Maximization (EM)** principle.

---

### 2.4 Semi-Supervised Learning — Combined Objective

The SSL objective combines the supervised loss on labeled data with an unsupervised regularization term on unlabeled data:

$$\mathcal{L}_{SSL} = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{\ell} \mathcal{L}_{sup}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)}_{\text{Labeled component}} + \lambda \underbrace{\sum_{j=\ell+1}^{\ell+u} \mathcal{L}_{unsup}(f_\theta(x_j))}_{\text{Unlabeled regularization}}$$

Common choices for $\mathcal{L}_{unsup}$ include:
- **Entropy minimization:** Push the model to make confident predictions on unlabeled data.
- **Consistency regularization:** Penalize the model for giving different predictions to perturbed versions of the same input (e.g., $\Pi$-Model, Mean Teacher).

---

### 2.5 Reinforcement Learning — The Bellman Equation

RL is formalized as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** defined by the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$:

- $\mathcal{S}$: State space
- $\mathcal{A}$: Action space
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$: Transition probability
- $R(s, a)$: Reward function
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$: Discount factor

The **state-value function** under policy $\pi$ is:

$$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r_{t+1} \,\middle|\, s_0 = s \right]$$

The recursive **Bellman Equation** decomposes it into immediate reward plus discounted future value:

$$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma V^\pi(s') \right]$$

The **action-value function** $Q^\pi(s, a)$ captures the expected return starting from state $s$, taking action $a$, and following $\pi$ thereafter:

$$Q^\pi(s, a) = R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') Q^\pi(s', a')$$

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Empirical Risk** | $\hat{R}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)$ | Supervised training objective |
| **MSE Loss (Regression)** | $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Penalizes squared errors |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss (Classification)** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Penalizes wrong class probabilities |
| **K-Means Objective** | $J = \sum_k \sum_{x \in C_k} \vert\vert x - \mu_k \vert\vert^2$ | Minimize intra-cluster variance |
| **PCA Reconstruction Error** | $\min_W \vert\vert X - XWW^T \vert\vert_F^2$ | Unsupervised dimensionality reduction |
| **Negative Log-Likelihood** | $\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta -\sum_i \log P_\theta(x_i)$ | Density estimation (MLE) |
| **SSL Combined Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{sup} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{unsup}$ | Joint supervised + unsupervised signal |
| **Bellman Expectation** | $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a\mid s)\sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s,a)[R + \gamma V^\pi(s')]$ | Recursive value estimation |
| **Bellman Optimality** | $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s,a)[R + \gamma V^*(s')]$ | Defines optimal policy value |
| **Discounted Return** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative reward signal in RL |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Supervised learning powers medical diagnosis and autonomous driving perception; unsupervised learning enables anomaly detection in network security; semi-supervised learning is critical for low-resource NLP; reinforcement learning drives robotics, game AI (AlphaGo), and dynamic pricing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Empirical Risk to Gradient Descent Update Rule

We derive the canonical gradient descent update for a supervised model parameterized by $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Start with the empirical risk objective:**
$$\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient with respect to parameters $\theta$:**
$$\nabla_\theta \hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$$

The gradient measures the local rate of change of loss. We move in the **opposite direction** to descend.

**Step 3 — Apply the gradient descent update rule:**
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \hat{R}(\theta_t)$$

where $\eta > 0$ is the learning rate.

**Step 4 — Expand with chain rule for a linear model** $f_\theta(x) = w^T x + b$ and MSE loss:
$$\mathcal{L}_i = (y_i - w^T x_i - b)^2$$

**Step 5 — Partial derivative w.r.t. weight vector $w$:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_i}{\partial w} = -2 (y_i - w^T x_i - b) \, x_i$$

**Step 6 — Update weight vector:**
$$w_{t+1} = w_t + \frac{2\eta}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - w_t^T x_i - b_t) \, x_i$$

**Step 7 — Convergence condition:**
The algorithm converges when the gradient norm vanishes, i.e., $\vert\vert \nabla_\theta \hat{R}(\theta^*) \vert\vert < \epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is a small tolerance (e.g., $10^{-4}$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bellman Equation for Value Iteration

We derive the recursive Bellman optimality equation starting from the discounted return.

**Step 1 — Define the discounted return at time $t$:**
$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^2 r_{t+3} + \ldots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$$

**Step 2 — Factor out the immediate reward:**
$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+2}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the right-hand sum as $G_{t+1}$:**
$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}$$

**Step 4 — Take expectation conditioned on current state $s_t = s$:**
$$\mathbb{E}[G_t \mid s_t = s] = \mathbb{E}[r_{t+1} \mid s] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}[G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s]$$

**Step 5 — Use the Markov property to condition on next state:**
$$\mathbb{E}[G_{t+1} \mid s_t = s] = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \, V^*(s')$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and combine into the Bellman Optimality Equation:**
$$V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \, V^*(s') \right]$$

**Step 7 — Value Iteration algorithm applies the Bellman backup repeatedly:**
$$V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \, V_k(s') \right]$$

This converges to $V^*$ as $k \to \infty$ under standard contraction mapping arguments.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: K-Means Objective Convergence

We prove that K-Means monotonically decreases the intra-cluster variance $J$.

**Step 1 — Define the objective at iteration $t$:**
$$J^{(t)} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k^{(t)}} \vert\vert x - \mu_k^{(t)} \vert\vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Assignment step:** Assign each point to the nearest centroid.
$$C_k^{(t+1)} = \{x : \vert\vert x - \mu_k^{(t)} \vert\vert^2 \leq \vert\vert x - \mu_j^{(t)} \vert\vert^2, \forall j\}$$

This step cannot increase $J$ because each point is assigned to the closest centroid.

**Step 3 — Update step:** Recompute centroid as the mean of assigned points.
$$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t+1)} \vert} \sum_{x \in C_k^{(t+1)}} x$$

This minimizes $J$ for fixed assignments because the mean is the unique minimizer of squared Euclidean distance.

**Step 4 — Convergence guarantee:**
$$J^{(0)} \geq J^{(1)} \geq J^{(2)} \geq \ldots \geq J^{(\infty)}$$

Since $J$ is bounded below by $0$, K-Means converges in finite steps.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Supervised | Unsupervised | Semi-Supervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Loss Signal Type** | Direct supervision | None / Reconstruction | Hybrid | Delayed reward |
| **Feedback Loop** | None | None | None | Sequential, online |
| **Optimization Principle** | ERM | MLE / EM | Regularized ERM | Bellman consistency |
| **Data Requirement** | High (labeled) | Medium (unlabeled) | Low labeled, high unlabeled | Medium (simulator) |
| **Mathematical Core** | Gradient descent on $\hat{R}$ | Minimize $- \log P(X)$ | Joint loss minimization | Solve Bellman equations |
| **Stability** | Stable, well-posed | Sensitive to initialization | Sensitive to label quality | Non-stationary target |
| **Evaluation Metric** | Accuracy, F1, RMSE | Silhouette, NMI | Accuracy, label propagation rate | Cumulative reward, regret |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Four Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw Dataset] --> B{Supervisory Signal Present?}
    B -- Yes, Fully Labeled --> C[Supervised Learning Module]
    B -- No Labels --> D[Unsupervised Learning Module]
    B -- Mixed Labels --> E[Semi-Supervised Learning Module]
    A --> F[Interactive Environment]
    F --> G[Reinforcement Learning Module]

    C --> C1[Feature Extraction f_theta x]
    C1 --> C2[Loss Computation L y_pred y_true]
    C2 --> C3[Gradient Descent Update]
    C3 --> C4[Trained Classifier / Regressor]

    D --> D1[Pattern Discovery]
    D1 --> D2[Clustering / Density Estimation]
    D2 --> D3[Latent Representation]

    E --> E1[Supervised Path on Labeled Set]
    E1 --> E2[Unsupervised Path on Unlabeled Set]
    E2 --> E3[Combined Loss Minimization]
    E3 --> E4[Refined Predictor]

    G --> G1[Agent Observes State s_t]
    G1 --> G2[Policy pi chooses Action a_t]
    G2 --> G3[Environment returns Reward r_t]
    G3 --> G4[Value Update via Bellman Equation]
    G4 --> G5[Optimized Policy pi_star]
```

---

### 4.2 Supervised Learning Workflow (Detailed Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Training Input x_i] --> A2[Model f_theta]
    A3[True Label y_i] --> A4[Loss Function L]
    A2 --> A4
    A4 --> A5[Compute Gradient dL by dtheta]
    A5 --> A6[Optimizer SGD / Adam]
    A6 --> A7[Updated Parameters theta_new]
    A7 --> A2
    A2 --> A8[Prediction on Test Data]
    A8 --> A9[Evaluation Metric Accuracy / RMSE]
```

---

### 4.3 Reinforcement Learning Agent-Environment Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Environment State s_t] --> R2[Agent]
    R2 --> R3[Action a_t from Policy pi]
    R3 --> R1
    R1 --> R4[Reward r_t+1]
    R4 --> R2
    R2 --> R5[Update Value Function Q s a]
    R5 --> R6[Improve Policy pi_new]
    R6 --> R3
```

---

### 4.4 Paradigm Decision Flowchart (Choosing the Right Paradigm)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Is labeled data available?}
    Q1 -- Yes, fully --> S1[Use Supervised Learning]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Need to discover structure?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> S2[Use Unsupervised Learning]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Have small labels + large unlabeled?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> S3[Use Semi-Supervised Learning]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Decision-making under sequential feedback?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> S4[Use Reinforcement Learning]
    Q4 -- No --> S5[Re-evaluate problem definition]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Differentiate between Supervised and Unsupervised Learning with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Labeled dataset $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$ | Unlabeled dataset $\{x_i\}$ |
| **Goal** | Learn mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ | Discover hidden structure in $X$ |
| **Feedback** | Direct supervision from labels | No external feedback |
| **Example** | Email spam classification | Customer market segmentation |
| **Algorithms** | SVM, Decision Tree, Linear Regression | K-Means, DBSCAN, PCA |

The fundamental distinction is the **presence or absence of labeled targets**. Supervised learning optimizes prediction accuracy, while unsupervised learning optimizes some intrinsic measure such as cluster compactness or reconstruction fidelity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Explain the concept of Reinforcement Learning with the agent-environment interaction loop.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a paradigm where an **agent** learns by interacting with an **environment**. At each discrete time step $t$:

1. The agent observes the current **state** $s_t \in \mathcal{S}$.
2. Based on its **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$, the agent selects an **action** $a_t \in \mathcal{A}$.
3. The environment transitions to a new state $s_{t+1}$ and emits a scalar **reward** $r_{t+1}$.
4. The agent updates its policy to maximize the expected cumulative discounted return $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$.

The objective is to discover the **optimal policy** $\pi^*$ that maximizes long-term reward, formalized through the **Bellman equation**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Supervised and Semi-Supervised Learning** (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) principle in supervised learning. Derive the gradient descent update rule for a linear regression model with Mean Squared Error loss. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of ERM:**
Empirical Risk Minimization states that the optimal parameters $\theta^*$ minimize the average loss over the training set:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$$

**Step 2 — Linear Model Assumption:**
Let $f_\theta(x) = w^T x + b$ where $\theta = \{w, b\}$.

**Step 3 — MSE Loss Definition:**
$$\mathcal{L}_i = (y_i - w^T x_i - b)^2$$

**Step 4 — Gradient Computation:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_i}{\partial w} = -2(y_i - w^T x_i - b)\, x_i$$
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_i}{\partial b} = -2(y_i - w^T x_i - b)$$

**Step 5 — Update Rule:**
$$w_{t+1} = w_t + \frac{2\eta}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - w_t^T x_i - b_t)\, x_i$$
$$b_{t+1} = b_t + \frac{2\eta}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - w_t^T x_i - b_t)$$

**Step 6 — Convergence:**
Training continues until $\vert\vert \nabla_\theta \hat{R} \vert\vert < 10^{-4}$ or a maximum of $T$ epochs is reached.

**(b)** Discuss Semi-Supervised Learning. Explain two popular SSL methods with their mathematical formulations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Semi-Supervised Learning trains on a dataset with $\ell$ labeled and $u$ unlabeled samples, leveraging structure in unlabeled data to enhance generalization.

**SSL Assumption:** Points close in feature space likely share labels (cluster assumption).

**Method 1 — Self-Training:**
- Train a model on the $\ell$ labeled samples.
- Predict pseudo-labels on unlabeled samples with confidence above threshold $\tau$.
- Add high-confidence pseudo-labeled samples to the training set.
- Repeat until convergence.

**Method 2 — Co-Training:**
- Train two independent models $f_1$ and $f_2$ on **disjoint feature views** $X_1$ and $X_2$.
- Each model labels unlabeled data for the other.
- High-confidence predictions from $f_1$ augment $f_2$'s training set and vice versa.

**Combined SSL Objective:**
$$\mathcal{L}_{SSL} = \sum_{i=1}^{\ell} \mathcal{L}_{sup}(f(x_i), y_i) + \lambda \sum_{j=\ell+1}^{\ell+u} \mathcal{L}_{unsup}(f(x_j))$$

where $\mathcal{L}_{unsup}$ is often **entropy minimization**: $\mathcal{L}_{unsup} = -\sum_c \hat{p}_c \log \hat{p}_c$.

---

#### **Question B — Unsupervised and Reinforcement Learning** (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm. Derive its objective function and explain how it monotonically decreases the objective. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Algorithm Initialization:**
Choose $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_K\}$ randomly from the dataset.

**Step 2 — Objective Function:**
$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \vert\vert x - \mu_k \vert\vert_2^2$$

This measures the total intra-cluster squared distance.

**Step 3 — Iterative Updates (two alternating steps):**

**Assignment Step:** Each point is assigned to the nearest centroid:
$$C_k = \{x : \vert\vert x - \mu_k \vert\vert^2 \leq \vert\vert x - \mu_j \vert\vert^2 \; \forall j\}$$

**Update Step:** Each centroid is moved to the mean of its cluster:
$$\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x \in C_k} x$$

**Step 4 — Monotonic Convergence Proof:**
- The **assignment step** does not increase $J$ because each point is mapped to its minimum-distance centroid.
- The **update step** minimizes $J$ for fixed assignments because the mean is the unique minimizer of $\sum_x \vert\vert x - \mu \vert\vert^2$.
- Hence $J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}$ for all $t$, and since $J \geq 0$, K-Means converges in finite steps.

**Step 5 — Termination:** Algorithm stops when assignments no longer change or $J$ change is below threshold $\epsilon$.

**(b)** Formulate Reinforcement Learning as a Markov Decision Process (MDP). Derive the Bellman optimality equation from the definition of the discounted return. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — MDP Definition:**
The RL problem is formalized as the tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$.

**Step 2 — Discounted Return:**
$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$$

**Step 3 — Recursive Decomposition:**
$$G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k+2} = r_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}$$

**Step 4 — Optimal State-Value Function:**
$$V^*(s) = \max_\pi \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid s_t = s]$$

**Step 5 — Bellman Optimality Equation:**
$$V^*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \, V^*(s') \right]$$

**Step 6 — Action-Value Function $Q^*(s,a)$:**
$$Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]$$

**Step 7 — Optimal Policy Extraction:**
$$\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^*(s, a)$$

**Step 8 — Value Iteration Algorithm:**
$$V_{k+1}(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \, V_k(s') \right]$$

This contraction converges to $V^*$ with rate $\gamma^k$.

---

### KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Confusing paradigms:** Students frequently classify PCA as supervised (it is unsupervised). Reinforcement Learning is **NOT** a form of supervised learning — there are no ground-truth labels, only rewards.
> - **Misapplying ERM:** Forgetting to mention the i.i.d. sampling assumption in ERM derivations costs marks.
> - **Bellman confusion:** Mixing up the **Bellman Expectation Equation** (for a fixed policy) with the **Bellman Optimality Equation** (for the optimal policy). The latter has a $\max$ operator.
> - **SSL assumption missing:** Failing to state the **Cluster Assumption** or **Manifold Assumption** when describing SSL.
> - **Reward vs. Label confusion:** In RL, the reward is **not** a ground-truth label — it is a delayed, noisy signal of action quality.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Supervised Learning** uses labeled input-output pairs and minimizes empirical risk via gradient descent.
- **Unsupervised Learning** discovers latent patterns using objectives like reconstruction loss or cluster compactness.
- **Semi-Supervised Learning** combines a small labeled set with a large unlabeled set under the Cluster or Manifold assumption.
- **Reinforcement Learning** learns an optimal policy through interaction, formalized as an MDP and solved via the Bellman equation.
- **ERM** is the foundational principle of supervised learning; its gradient gives rise to **SGD/Adam** updates.
- **K-Means** monotonically decreases intra-cluster variance via alternating assignment and centroid updates.
- **Bellman Optimality Equation** encodes the dynamic-programming principle: optimal future value decomposes into immediate reward plus discounted optimal next-state value.
- **Discount factor $\gamma$** trades off immediate vs. future rewards; $\gamma = 0$ is greedy, $\gamma \to 1$ is far-sighted.
- **Pseudo-labeling** is the simplest SSL technique; **Co-Training** exploits multiple feature views.
- **Always remember**: RL uses **trial-and-error exploration** rather than supervised examples — this is its defining signature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Parameter Estimation - Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum a Posteriori Estimation (MAP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basics of Parameter Estimation - Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum a Posteriori Estimation (MAP) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Parameter Estimation in Machine Learning **Parameter Estimation** is the statistical process of using observed data to infer the values of parameters that define a probability distribution or a model. In the context of Machine Learning, virtually every probabilistic model (e.g., Naive Bayes, Gaussian Mixture Models, Logistic Regression, Hidden Markov Models) is governed by parameters $\theta$ that must be "learned" from data. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Parameter Estimation refers to the procedure of inferring the underlying parameters of an assumed probability distribution, given a finite set of observed data samples, such that the chosen model best explains the observed empirical evidence.* The two principal paradigms for this task, covered in Module 1, are: 1. **Frequentist Approach:** Maximum Likelihood Estimation (**MLE**) 2. **Bayesian Approach:** Maximum a Posteriori Estimation (**MAP**) --- ## 1.2 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) — Formal Definition **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** is a frequentist method that estimates the parameters $\theta$ of a statistical model by finding the value of $\theta$ that **maximizes the Likelihood Function** $L(\theta \vert D)$, which is the probability of observing the given data $D$ under the assumed model. Formally: $$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max} \, L(\theta \vert D) = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max} \, P(D \vert \theta)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Idea:** We do not ask *"What is the probability of the parameter?"* (Bayesian view), but rather *"Given the parameter, how probable is the data we just observed?"* We pick the parameter that makes our observed data **most likely**. --- ## 1.3 Maximum a Posteriori Estimation (MAP) — Formal Definition **Maximum a Posteriori Estimation (MAP)** is a Bayesian method that estimates the parameters by finding the value of $\theta$ that **maximizes the posterior probability** $P(\theta \vert D)$, incorporating a **prior belief** $P(\theta)$ about the parameter. Using Bayes' Theorem: $$\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max} \, P(\theta \vert D) = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max} \, P(D \vert \theta) \cdot P(\theta)$$ The denominator $P(D)$ is a normalization constant and is dropped during maximization. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Idea:** MLE + Prior Belief = MAP. MAP is essentially a *regularized* version of MLE, where the prior $P(\theta)$ acts as a soft constraint penalizing implausible parameter values. --- ## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy ### 🍕 Analogy for MLE — "The Detective with No Bias" Imagine a detective trying to estimate the weight ($\theta$) of a suspicious package using 5 weight measurements (data $D$) from a faulty scale. The detective has **no prior knowledge** of typical package weights. He simply asks: *"Which weight value makes these 5 specific readings most plausible?"* He picks the weight that **maximizes the chance** of seeing exactly those 5 numbers. This is pure MLE. ### 🎯 Analogy for MAP — "The Detective with Experience" Now imagine the same detective has worked in the postal service for 20 years. He *knows* (a prior) that 95% of packages weigh between 0.5 kg and 5 kg. So when he sees the 5 readings, he doesn't blindly maximize likelihood — he balances two things: - **How likely are these readings** under the candidate weight? *(Likelihood)* - **How plausible is this weight** based on his experience? *(Prior)* He picks a weight that satisfies both. If the readings suggest 50 kg, he will lean back towards a more reasonable value because of his strong prior. **This is MAP.** > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** MLE is purely **data-driven**. MAP fuses **data with prior knowledge**. When the prior is **uniform** (non-informative), MAP $\equiv$ MLE. Hence, MLE is a special case of MAP. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Workflow of MLE — Step-by-Step Logic The MLE algorithm follows a strict, deterministic pipeline: 1. **Model Assumption:** Select a probability distribution $P(x \vert \theta)$ believed to have generated the data (e.g., Bernoulli, Gaussian, Poisson). 2. **Data Collection:** Gather an i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) dataset $D = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$. 3. **Likelihood Construction:** Since samples are i.i.d., the joint likelihood is the product of individual probabilities: $$L(\theta \vert D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert \theta)$$ 4. **Log-Transformation (Log-Likelihood):** Convert the product into a sum using the natural logarithm $\log(\cdot)$ — the **monotonicity** of $\log$ guarantees that the argmax is preserved. This also prevents numerical underflow. $$\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta \vert D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \vert \theta)$$ 5. **Differentiation:** Compute the partial derivative of the log-likelihood with respect to $\theta$ and set it to zero. $$\frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$$ 6. **Solve for $\theta$:** The solution $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$ is the Maximum Likelihood Estimate. > [!NOTE] > **Why log?** It converts products (which are computationally unstable and difficult to differentiate) into summations. It also makes the calculus tractable. --- ## 2.2 Operational Workflow of MAP — Step-by-Step Logic MAP extends the MLE pipeline by injecting a prior: 1. **Define Prior:** Specify a prior distribution $P(\theta)$ over the parameter space. 2. **Posterior Formulation:** Using Bayes' rule, the posterior is proportional to the likelihood-prior product: $$P(\theta \vert D) \propto P(D \vert \theta) \cdot P(\theta)$$ 3. **Log-Posterior:** Take the log to convert products to sums: $$\log P(\theta \vert D) \propto \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \vert \theta) + \log P(\theta)$$ 4. **Differentiation:** Differentiate the log-posterior and set to zero. 5. **Solve for $\theta$:** The solution $\hat{\theta}_{MAP}$ is the Maximum a Posteriori Estimate. > [!IMPORTANT] > The prior term $\log P(\theta)$ acts as a **regularizer**. In machine learning, this is why MAP estimation is often mathematically equivalent to MLE with a regularization penalty (e.g., L2 prior $\Rightarrow$ weight decay). --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is a **must-memorize** cheat sheet for the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). All symbols are standard. | # | Concept | Formula | Notation | Remarks | |---|---------|---------|----------|---------| | 1 | **Likelihood Function** | $L(\theta \vert D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert \theta)$ | $\theta$ : parameter, $D$ : dataset | Assumes i.i.d. samples | | 2 | **Log-Likelihood** | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \vert \theta)$ | $\log$ : natural logarithm | Monotonic transform of $L$ | | 3 | **MLE Objective** | $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{\theta} \ell(\theta)$ | $\arg\max$ : argument that maximizes | First-order condition: $\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \theta} = 0$ | | 4 | **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(\theta \vert D) = \frac{P(D \vert \theta) P(\theta)}{P(D)}$ | $P(\theta)$ : prior, $P(D \vert \theta)$ : likelihood | $P(D)$ is evidence (ignored in MAP) | | 5 | **MAP Objective** | $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} \left[ \log P(D \vert \theta) + \log P(\theta) \right]$ | Posterior maximization | MLE + Prior (log) | | 6 | **MLE for Bernoulli** | $\hat{\theta} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | $\theta \in [0,1]$ | Sample mean of binary outcomes | | 7 | **MLE for Gaussian Mean** | $\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | $\mu$ : mean | Unbiased estimator | | 8 | **MLE for Gaussian Variance** | $\hat{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \hat{\mu})^{2}$ | $\sigma^{2}$ : variance | Biased but consistent | | 9 | **MAP (Beta Prior on Bernoulli)** | $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \frac{\alpha + \sum x_i - 1}{\alpha + \beta + n - 2}$ | $\alpha, \beta$ : Beta hyperparameters | For $\text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ prior | | 10 | **MLE $\equiv$ MAP** | When $P(\theta) = \text{Uniform}(\theta)$ | Uniform prior is non-informative | MAP reduces to MLE | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **MLE** is the workhorse behind **speech recognition systems** (HMM parameter estimation via Baum-Welch), **computer vision** (Gaussian Mixture Models for skin segmentation), and **medical diagnosis** (estimating disease prevalence). - **MAP** is critical in **autonomous vehicles** (sensor fusion with strong prior maps), **spam filtering** (Bayesian spam classifiers), and **reinforcement learning** (policy priors in robotics). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: MLE for a Bernoulli Distribution **Problem Setup:** Let $D = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ be i.i.d. samples from a Bernoulli distribution with unknown parameter $\theta$, where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$. The Bernoulli probability mass function is: $$P(x_i \vert \theta) = \theta^{x_i} (1 - \theta)^{1 - x_i}$$ ### Step 1: Write the Likelihood Function $$L(\theta \vert D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \theta^{x_i} (1 - \theta)^{1 - x_i}$$ ### Step 2: Take the Natural Logarithm (Log-Likelihood) $$\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta \vert D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \left[ \theta^{x_i} (1 - \theta)^{1 - x_i} \right]$$ Using the property $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$: $$\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ x_i \log \theta + (1 - x_i) \log(1 - \theta) \right]$$ ### Step 3: Separate the Sums $$\ell(\theta) = \log\theta \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + \log(1 - \theta) \sum_{i=1}^{n} (1 - x_i)$$ ### Step 4: Differentiate the Log-Likelihood with respect to $\theta$ $$\frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{\theta}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - \frac{1}{1 - \theta}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (1 - x_i)$$ ### Step 5: Set the Derivative to Zero (First-Order Optimality Condition) $$\frac{1}{\theta}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \frac{1}{1 - \theta}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (1 - x_i)$$ ### Step 6: Cross-Multiply and Solve for $\theta$ $$(1 - \theta)\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \theta\sum_{i=1}^{n} (1 - x_i)$$ $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - \theta\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \theta n - \theta\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$ $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \theta n$$ ### Step 7: Final MLE Estimate $$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The MLE for a Bernoulli parameter is simply the **sample mean** of the observed binary outcomes. For example, if you flip a coin 10 times and get 7 heads, $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = 0.7$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: MLE for a Gaussian Distribution (Mean $\mu$, Known Variance $\sigma^{2}$) **Problem Setup:** Let $D = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ be i.i.d. samples from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ with known $\sigma^{2}$. Estimate $\mu$. The Gaussian density is: $$P(x_i \vert \mu, \sigma^{2}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right)$$ ### Step 1: Likelihood Function $$L(\mu \vert D) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right)$$ ### Step 2: Log-Likelihood $$\ell(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ -\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^{2}) - \frac{(x_i - \mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right]$$ The constant $-\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^{2})$ is independent of $\mu$, so it can be dropped during maximization: $$\ell(\mu) \propto -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^{2}$$ ### Step 3: Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$ $$\frac{\partial \ell(\mu)}{\partial \mu} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \cdot 2\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)(-1) = \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)$$ ### Step 4: Set to Zero and Solve $$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n\mu = 0$$ $$\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: MAP for Bernoulli with Beta Prior **Problem Setup:** Same Bernoulli data, but now assume a **prior** $P(\theta) = \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$, which is the **conjugate prior** of the Bernoulli likelihood. Beta PDF: $$P(\theta) = \frac{1}{B(\alpha, \beta)} \theta^{\alpha - 1} (1 - \theta)^{\beta - 1}, \quad \theta \in [0, 1]$$ ### Step 1: Posterior $\propto$ Likelihood $\times$ Prior $$P(\theta \vert D) \propto \left[ \prod_{i=1}^{n} \theta^{x_i}(1 - \theta)^{1 - x_i} \right] \cdot \theta^{\alpha - 1}(1 - \theta)^{\beta - 1}$$ ### Step 2: Combine the Exponents $$P(\theta \vert D) \propto \theta^{\left(\sum x_i + \alpha - 1\right)} (1 - \theta)^{\left(n - \sum x_i + \beta - 1\right)}$$ ### Step 3: Log-Posterior $$\log P(\theta \vert D) \propto \left(\sum x_i + \alpha - 1\right)\log\theta + \left(n - \sum x_i + \beta - 1\right)\log(1 - \theta)$$ ### Step 4: Differentiate and Set to Zero $$\frac{\partial \log P(\theta \vert D)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{\sum x_i + \alpha - 1}{\theta} - \frac{n - \sum x_i + \beta - 1}{1 - \theta} = 0$$ ### Step 5: Solve for $\theta$ $$(1 - \theta)\left(\sum x_i + \alpha - 1\right) = \theta\left(n - \sum x_i + \beta - 1\right)$$ After algebraic simplification: $$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Comparison with MLE:** > - **MLE:** $\hat{\theta} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$ > - **MAP:** $\hat{\theta} = \frac{\sum x_i + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2}$ > > The terms $(\alpha - 1)$ and $(\beta - 2)$ act as **pseudo-counts** (Bayesian smoothing). When $n \to \infty$, MAP $\to$ MLE. For small $n$, the prior dominates, preventing extreme estimates (e.g., $\hat{\theta} = 0$ or $1$ from sparse data). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MLE vs MAP > [!IMPORTANT] > This comparison is **examination-critical** and is frequently asked as a 7-mark or 14-mark question in KTU ESE. | **Aspect** | **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** | **Maximum a Posteriori (MAP)** | |---|---|---| | **Paradigm** | Frequentist | Bayesian | | **Objective** | Maximize $P(D \vert \theta)$ | Maximize $P(\theta \vert D) \propto P(D \vert \theta)P(\theta)$ | | **Prior Required?** | No | Yes | | **Output** | Point estimate $\hat{\theta}$ (single value) | Point estimate $\hat{\theta}$ (single value) | | **Uncertainty?** | No (point estimate only) | Prior encodes uncertainty | | **Data Efficiency** | Needs more data to be reliable | Robust even with small data | | **Overfitting** | Prone (especially with small data) | Regularized via prior | | **Computational Cost** | Lower (closed-form in many cases) | Slightly higher (involves prior) | | **Relation to Each Other** | MAP with uniform prior | Generalization of MLE | | **Connection to ML** | Basis of Logistic Regression, GMMs | Basis of Bayesian Neural Networks, L2-regularized models | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End MLE Pipeline (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Observed Dataset D] --> B[Choose Probabilistic Model P x given theta] B --> C[Construct Likelihood Function L theta given D] C --> D[Apply Log Transform: ell theta = log L theta] D --> E[Differentiate w.r.t. theta] E --> F[Set derivative to zero] F --> G{Are boundary conditions satisfied?} G -->|Yes| H[Compute theta hat MLE] G -->|No| I[Use numerical optimization Newton Raphson, Gradient Descent] H --> J[Final Parameter Estimate] I --> J ``` --- ## 4.2 MAP Estimation Pipeline (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Observed Dataset D] --> B[Choose Likelihood P D given theta] K[Prior Belief P theta] --> C[Combine via Bayes Theorem] B --> C C --> D[Posterior P theta given D proportional to Likelihood times Prior] D --> E[Apply Log Transform] E --> F[Differentiate w.r.t. theta] F --> G[Set derivative to zero] G --> H[Solve for theta hat MAP] H --> I[Final Parameter Estimate with prior regularization] ``` --- ## 4.3 Comparative Decision Flow: When to Use MLE vs MAP ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need to estimate parameter theta] --> Q1{Do you have prior
domain knowledge?} Q1 -->|No, or uniform belief| MLE[Use MLE] Q1 -->|Yes, strong belief| Q2{Is dataset large
or small?} Q2 -->|Large n| MLE2[MLE sufficient
Prior becomes negligible] Q2 -->|Small n| MAP[Use MAP for robustness] MLE --> Out1[Purely data-driven estimate] MLE2 --> Out2[Data dominates, prior ignored] MAP --> Out3[Balanced estimate with regularization] ``` --- ## 4.4 Conceptual Hierarchy: Bayes → MAP → MLE ```mermaid graph TD Bayes[Full Bayesian Inference
P theta given D] --> MAP[MAP Estimation
argmax of posterior] MAP --> MLE[MLE Estimation
argmax of likelihood] MLE --> OLS[Ordinary Least Squares
special case under Gaussian noise] ``` > [!NOTE] > This hierarchy illustrates that as we **discard information** (full posterior $\to$ point estimate $\to$ no prior), we get simpler but less informative estimators. The KTU examiner may test this hierarchy as a "trick" question. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define Maximum Likelihood Estimation. How does it differ from Maximum a Posteriori Estimation?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **MLE (1.5 marks):** Maximum Likelihood Estimation is a frequentist parameter estimation technique that finds the value of the parameter $\theta$ that maximizes the probability (likelihood) of observing the given dataset $D$, i.e., $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(D \vert \theta)$. - **MAP (1.5 marks):** MAP is a Bayesian technique that maximizes the posterior probability of the parameter given the data, incorporating a prior distribution $P(\theta)$. Formally, $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(D \vert \theta) P(\theta)$. Unlike MLE, MAP uses prior knowledge. When the prior is uniform, MAP reduces to MLE. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State Bayes' Theorem and explain its role in Maximum a Posteriori Estimation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Bayes' Theorem (1.5 marks):** $$P(\theta \vert D) = \frac{P(D \vert \theta) P(\theta)}{P(D)}$$ where $P(\theta \vert D)$ is the posterior, $P(D \vert \theta)$ is the likelihood, $P(\theta)$ is the prior, and $P(D)$ is the evidence. - **Role in MAP (1.5 marks):** In MAP, the goal is to find $\theta$ that maximizes the posterior $P(\theta \vert D)$. Since $P(D)$ does not depend on $\theta$, MAP simplifies to maximizing the numerator $P(D \vert \theta) \cdot P(\theta)$. The prior $P(\theta)$ allows incorporation of domain knowledge, regularizing the estimate and making it robust for small datasets. --- ## 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **a) Derive the Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE) for the parameter $\theta$ of a Bernoulli distribution given $n$ independent observations. (7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **(i) Bernoulli PMF (1 mark):** For $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$: $$P(x_i \vert \theta) = \theta^{x_i}(1 - \theta)^{1 - x_i}$$ **(ii) Likelihood Function (1 mark):** $$L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \theta^{x_i}(1 - \theta)^{1 - x_i}$$ **(iii) Log-Likelihood (2 marks):** $$\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ x_i \log\theta + (1 - x_i)\log(1 - \theta) \right]$$ $$\ell(\theta) = \log\theta \sum x_i + \log(1 - \theta)\sum (1 - x_i)$$ **(iv) Differentiation (1.5 marks):** $$\frac{d\ell}{d\theta} = \frac{\sum x_i}{\theta} - \frac{n - \sum x_i}{1 - \theta} = 0$$ **(v) Final Result (1.5 marks):** $$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$ --- **b) Explain Maximum a Posteriori (MAP) estimation. Derive the MAP estimate for a Bernoulli likelihood with a Beta$(\alpha, \beta)$ prior. Show that MAP reduces to MLE as $n \to \infty$. (7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **(i) MAP Definition (1 mark):** $$\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(\theta \vert D) = \arg\max_{\theta}\left[\log P(D \vert \theta) + \log P(\theta)\right]$$ **(ii) Beta Prior (1 mark):** $$P(\theta) \propto \theta^{\alpha - 1}(1 - \theta)^{\beta - 1}$$ **(iii) Posterior Derivation (2 marks):** $$P(\theta \vert D) \propto \theta^{\sum x_i + \alpha - 1}(1 - \theta)^{n - \sum x_i + \beta - 1}$$ **(iv) MAP Estimate (2 marks):** $$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \frac{\sum x_i + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2}}$$ **(v) Limiting Case (1 mark):** As $n \to \infty$, the $\alpha, \beta$ terms become negligible: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \hat{\theta}_{MLE}$$ --- ### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **a) Given $n$ i.i.d. samples from a Gaussian distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ with known $\sigma^{2}$, derive the MLE for $\mu$. (7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **(i) Gaussian PDF (1 mark):** $$P(x_i \vert \mu) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$ **(ii) Likelihood (1 mark):** $$L(\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$ **(iii) Log-Likelihood (1.5 marks):** $$\ell(\mu) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^{2} + \text{constant}$$ **(iv) Differentiation (2 marks):** $$\frac{d\ell}{d\mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = 0$$ **(v) Final Result (1.5 marks):** $$\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}$$ --- **b) Compare and contrast MLE and MAP estimation. Discuss the role of the prior distribution in MAP. (7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **(i) MLE Definition (1 mark):** MLE maximizes the likelihood function: $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(D \vert \theta)$. It is purely data-driven with no prior. **(ii) MAP Definition (1 mark):** MAP maximizes the posterior: $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(D \vert \theta) P(\theta)$. It incorporates a prior. **(iii) Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Aspect | MLE | MAP | |---|---|---| | Prior | Not required | Required | | Data Need | Large data needed | Robust with small data | | Overfitting | Prone | Mitigated by prior | | Output | Point estimate | Point estimate | | Computation | Faster | Slightly slower | **(iv) Role of Prior (2 marks):** The prior $P(\theta)$ encodes **domain knowledge** and acts as a **regularizer**. It: - Prevents overfitting in low-data regimes - Provides Bayesian smoothing (e.g., Laplace smoothing in NLP) - Guides the estimator toward plausible values - Reduces to a non-informative uniform prior when no prior is known, in which case MAP $\equiv$ MLE --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to take the log:** Many students write $L(\theta)$ and try to differentiate the product directly. This is **mathematically correct but computationally wrong** and earns **zero credit** for the differentiation step. > 2. **Dropping the log transformation mid-way:** If you write $\log L$ but then forget the chain rule for $\log(1 - \theta)$ during differentiation, you will lose 2 marks. > 3. **Confusing prior and posterior in MAP:** Writing $P(D \vert \theta)$ as the prior is a fatal error. **Prior = $P(\theta)$**, **Posterior = $P(\theta \vert D)$**. > 4. **Not stating the i.i.d. assumption:** Always explicitly write "Assuming i.i.d. samples" before constructing the likelihood. > 5. **Missing the limiting case:** When asked about MAP vs MLE, always include the $\lim_{n \to \infty}$ argument. It is a 1–2 mark differentiator. > 6. **Sign error in Gaussian derivation:** Be careful with the negative sign in $\exp[-(x_i - \mu)^2 / 2\sigma^2]$. A sign flip will lead to $\hat{\mu} = -\bar{x}$, which is **wrong**. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **MLE** estimates $\theta$ by maximizing the **likelihood** $P(D \vert \theta)$ — purely frequentist, data-driven. - ✅ **MAP** estimates $\theta$ by maximizing the **posterior** $P(\theta \vert D)$ — Bayesian, uses a prior. - ✅ **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(\theta \vert D) = \frac{P(D \vert \theta)P(\theta)}{P(D)}$ - ✅ MAP $\equiv$ MLE when the prior is **uniform** (non-informative). - ✅ MAP $\to$ MLE as $n \to \infty$ (data dominates prior). - ✅ For **Bernoulli** data: MLE = sample mean. - ✅ For **Gaussian** data (known $\sigma^2$): MLE mean = sample mean. - ✅ For **Beta-Bernoulli MAP:** $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \frac{\sum x_i + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2}$ — prior hyperparameters act as **pseudo-counts**. - ✅ Log-likelihood is used for **numerical stability** and **ease of differentiation**. - ✅ MLE estimates are **consistent** (converge to true value as $n \to \infty$) but may be **biased** for finite $n$. - ✅ MAP addresses the **overfitting** problem of MLE in small-sample regimes. - ✅ The prior in MAP is mathematically equivalent to an **L2 regularization** term in many ML models. - ✅ Always state the **i.i.d. assumption** before writing the likelihood function. - ✅ Remember the **hierarchy**: Full Bayesian $\to$ MAP $\to$ MLE $\to$ OLS (each is a special case of the previous under certain assumptions). ---

topics.title: Introduction to Bayesian Formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Bayesian Formulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bayesian Formulation** is a rigorous statistical and probabilistic framework for reasoning under uncertainty, which updates the probability of a hypothesis as new evidence or data becomes available. Rooted firmly in **Thomas Bayes' Theorem (1763)**, it provides a mathematical inverse-probability mechanism to compute the **posterior probability** of an event or model parameter conditioned on observed data, by combining a **prior belief** with the **likelihood** of the observed evidence.

In the context of Machine Learning, Bayesian formulation provides the theoretical backbone for:
- **Bayesian Inference & Learning**
- **Bayesian Classification** (e.g., Naive Bayes, Bayesian Belief Networks)
- **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Estimation**
- **Bayesian Decision Theory**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT742):** Bayesian formulation is foundational for the *Supervised Learning* and *Probabilistic Reasoning* units. It is the most heavily tested topic in Module 1 of ESE/University examinations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Weather Forecaster" Story**

Imagine you are a weather forecaster in Kerala during the monsoon. You have two competing hypotheses:
- $H_1$: *It will rain heavily today.*
- $H_2$: *It will be sunny today.*

Before observing the sky, your **prior belief** is shaped by historical data — say, in June, it rains **85%** of the time. So your prior for $H_1$ is **0.85**.

Now, you observe new evidence $E$: *dark cumulonimbus clouds are forming over the Western Ghats.* The **likelihood** of seeing such clouds given heavy rain is very high (say, **0.95**), while the likelihood of such clouds given sunshine is very low (say, **0.10**).

The Bayesian formulation asks: **"Given the new cloud evidence, how should I update my belief that it will rain?"**

The answer is the **posterior probability**, computed by combining the prior, the likelihood, and the evidence. This is the essence of Bayesian reasoning — *learning from data to update beliefs.*

---

## 1.3 Core Components of the Bayesian Framework

The Bayesian formulation rests on **four fundamental components**, each of which must be mastered for KTU examinations:

| Component | Symbol | Meaning | Analogy (Weather) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prior Probability** | $P(H)$ | Belief *before* seeing evidence | 85% chance of rain in June |
| **Likelihood** | $P(E \mid H)$ | Probability of evidence under hypothesis | 95% chance of dark clouds if raining |
| **Evidence (Marginal)** | $P(E)$ | Total probability of observing evidence | Probability of dark clouds in general |
