The algorithm is called **naive** because its assumptions (especially the independence assumption and the equal importance of features) are often violated in real-world data. Despite this, the classifier performs remarkably well in many practical domains — particularly in **text classification** and **natural language processing** — making it a popular baseline in industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary for KTU Exams:**
> - **Posterior Probability** $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$ — the updated probability of a class after observing evidence.
> - **Prior Probability** $P(C_k)$ — the initial probability of a class before seeing evidence.
> - **Likelihood** $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$ — the probability of observing the feature vector given the class.
> - **Evidence / Marginal** $P(\mathbf{x})$ — the total probability of observing the feature vector across all classes.
> - **Conditional Independence Assumption** — the defining "naive" property.

### 1.4 Real-World Applications

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| Email Filtering | Spam vs. Ham classification |
| Text Mining | Sentiment analysis, topic categorization |
| Healthcare | Disease prediction, medical diagnosis |
| Recommendation Systems | News article recommendation |
| Weather Prediction | Rain / Sunny / Cloudy classification |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundation: Bayes' Theorem (Recap)

The entire Naive Bayes framework rests upon **Thomas Bayes' theorem of conditional probability**:

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

- $P(C_k)$ — the **prior**, computed directly from the training set as the relative frequency of class $C_k$.
- $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$ — the **likelihood**, which is computationally intractable to estimate without simplification.
- $P(\mathbf{x})$ — the **evidence**, a normalizing constant that ensures the posterior sums to 1 across all classes.

### 2.2 The Naive Independence Assumption — Step by Step

For an $n$-dimensional feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$, the exact joint likelihood is, by the chain rule:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = P(x_1 \mid C_k) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1, C_k) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2, C_k) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \dots, x_{n-1}, C_k)$$

This requires estimating $O(K \cdot n \cdot |\mathcal{D}|)$ parameters for $K$ classes and dataset $\mathcal{D}$ — a combinatorial nightmare.

The **Naive Bayes simplification** asserts:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$P(x_i \mid x_{i+1}, \dots, x_n, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k) \quad \forall i$$

In words: **each feature $x_i$ is conditionally independent of every other feature $x_j$ given the class $C_k$**. This collapses the joint likelihood into a clean product form:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

Substituting into Bayes' rule, the **Naive Bayes decision rule** becomes:

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} \left[ P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k) \right]$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ does not depend on $C_k$, it is dropped from the maximization (a constant divisor).

### 2.3 Log-Probability Trick (Numerical Stability)

Direct multiplication of many small probabilities (e.g., word probabilities of $10^{-5}$) causes **underflow** in floating-point arithmetic. The standard fix is to take the **monotonic logarithm** (which preserves the argmax):

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k) \right]$$

### 2.4 The Zero-Frequency Problem & Laplace (Additive) Smoothing

If a category has **zero frequency** in the training data, its probability estimate becomes $0$, which then nullifies the entire product $\prod_i P(x_i \mid C_k)$ regardless of the other features. **Laplace smoothing** (also called **additive smoothing**) solves this by adding a pseudo-count of $\alpha \geq 0$:

$$\hat{P}(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{N_{x_i, C_k} + \alpha}{N_{C_k} + \alpha \cdot n_i}$$

where:
- $N_{x_i, C_k}$ = number of times feature $x_i$ appears in samples of class $C_k$
- $N_{C_k}$ = total number of samples of class $C_k$
- $n_i$ = number of distinct values feature $x_i$ can take
- $\alpha$ = smoothing parameter (commonly $\alpha = 1$, called **Laplace smoothing**; $\alpha < 1$ is **Lidstone smoothing**)

### 2.5 Variants of Naive Bayes (KTU High-Yield)

The choice of likelihood model $P(x_i \mid C_k)$ depends on the nature of the feature $x_i$:

| Variant | Feature Type | Likelihood Distribution | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian Naive Bayes** | Continuous | Normal (Gaussian): $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{C_k}^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu_{C_k})^2}{2\sigma_{C_k}^2}\right)$ | Iris dataset, sensor data |
| **Multinomial Naive Bayes** | Discrete counts | Multinomial: $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \frac{(\sum_i x_i)!}{\prod_i x_i!} \prod_i P(x_i \mid C_k)^{x_i}$ | Text classification (TF counts) |
| **Bernoulli Naive Bayes** | Binary / Boolean | Bernoulli: $P(x_i \mid C_k) = P(i \mid C_k)^{x_i} (1 - P(i \mid C_k))^{(1-x_i)}$ | Document classification (presence/absence) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Multinomial vs. Bernoulli:** Multinomial NB uses *word frequency counts* and is generally preferred for longer documents; Bernoulli NB uses *binary occurrence* and works better for short texts or keyword-style tasks.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Bayes' Theorem (foundation) | Derivation |
| 2 | $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | Conditional independence assumption | Core simplification |
| 3 | $\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | Naive Bayes decision rule | Inference |
| 4 | $\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | Log-space form | Numerical stability |
| 5 | $\hat{P}(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{N_{x_i, C_k} + \alpha}{N_{C_k} + \alpha \cdot n_i}$ | Laplace smoothing | Zero-frequency fix |
| 6 | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{C_k}^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu_{C_k})^2}{2\sigma_{C_k}^2}\right)$ | Gaussian likelihood | Gaussian NB |
| 7 | $P(C_k) = \frac{N_{C_k}}{N}$ | Class prior estimation | Training |
| 8 | Accuracy $= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Performance metric | Evaluation |

### 2.7 Why "Naive" Bayes Works (KTU Favourite Question)

Even though the independence assumption is rarely true in practice, Naive Bayes performs well because:
1. It only needs **correct relative ordering of probabilities**, not exact calibration — the argmax is robust.
2. The decision boundary is often **linear**, which is sufficient for many real-world separable problems.
3. With limited training data, the independence assumption acts as a **regularizer**, preventing overfitting.
4. It requires only a small amount of training data to estimate the necessary parameters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem (Kerala KTU Dec 2023 Pattern):**
Given the following training dataset for a Play-Tennis style problem, classify the new instance $(\text{Outlook} = \text{Sunny}, \text{Wind} = \text{Weak})$ using Naive Bayes with Laplace smoothing $\alpha = 1$.

| # | Outlook | Wind | Play |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | Weak | Yes |
| 2 | Sunny | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Weak | No |
| 6 | Overcast | Strong | Yes |
| 7 | Sunny | Weak | No |

### Step 1 — Compute the Class Priors $P(C_k)$

Total instances $N = 7$.

Number of "Yes" instances: $N_{\text{Yes}} = 4$ (rows 1, 3, 4, 6).
Number of "No" instances: $N_{\text{No}} = 3$ (rows 2, 5, 7).

$$P(\text{Yes}) = \frac{N_{\text{Yes}}}{N} = \frac{4}{7}$$

$$P(\text{No}) = \frac{N_{\text{No}}}{N} = \frac{3}{7}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Conditional Likelihoods with Laplace Smoothing

For categorical features, the Laplace-smoothed conditional probability is:

$$\hat{P}(x_i = v \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i = v, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{count}(C_k) + \alpha \cdot n_i}$$

where $n_i$ is the number of distinct values feature $x_i$ can take.
- $n_{\text{Outlook}} = 3$ (Sunny, Overcast, Rain)
- $n_{\text{Wind}} = 2$ (Weak, Strong)
- $\alpha = 1$

#### Likelihoods for Class = "Yes" ($N_{\text{Yes}} = 4$)

**Outlook = Sunny $\mid$ Yes:**
- Count of (Outlook=Sunny, Yes) = 1 (row 1).

$$\hat{P}(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{1 + 1}{4 + 1 \cdot 3} = \frac{2}{7}$$

**Outlook = Overcast $\mid$ Yes:**
- Count = 2 (rows 3, 6).

$$\hat{P}(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{2 + 1}{4 + 3} = \frac{3}{7}$$

**Outlook = Rain $\mid$ Yes:**
- Count = 1 (row 4).

$$\hat{P}(\text{Rain} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{1 + 1}{4 + 3} = \frac{2}{7}$$

**Wind = Weak $\mid$ Yes:**
- Count of (Wind=Weak, Yes) = 2 (rows 1, 3; row 4 is Rain+Yes but Wind=Weak... yes row 4: Rain, Weak, Yes ✓).

$$\hat{P}(\text{Weak} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{2 + 1}{4 + 1 \cdot 2} = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Wind = Strong $\mid$ Yes:**
- Count = 1 (row 6).

$$\hat{P}(\text{Strong} \mid \text{Yes}) = \frac{1 + 1}{4 + 2} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$$

#### Likelihoods for Class = "No" ($N_{\text{No}} = 3$)

**Outlook = Sunny $\mid$ No:**
- Count of (Outlook=Sunny, No) = 2 (rows 2, 7).

$$\hat{P}(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 1 \cdot 3} = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Outlook = Overcast $\mid$ No:**
- Count = 0.

$$\hat{P}(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{0 + 1}{3 + 3} = \frac{1}{6}$$

**Outlook = Rain $\mid$ No:**
- Count = 1 (row 5).

$$\hat{P}(\text{Rain} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{1 + 1}{3 + 3} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Wind = Weak $\mid$ No:**
- Count of (Wind=Weak, No) = 2 (rows 5, 7).

$$\hat{P}(\text{Weak} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{2 + 1}{3 + 1 \cdot 2} = \frac{3}{5}$$

**Wind = Strong $\mid$ No:**
- Count = 1 (row 2).

$$\hat{P}(\text{Strong} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{1 + 1}{3 + 2} = \frac{2}{5}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Posterior for Test Instance $(\text{Sunny}, \text{Weak})$

**Posterior for "Yes":**

$$P(\text{Yes} \mid \text{Sunny}, \text{Weak}) \propto P(\text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Yes}) \cdot P(\text{Weak} \mid \text{Yes})$$

Substituting:

$$P(\text{Yes} \mid \cdot) \propto \frac{4}{7} \cdot \frac{2}{7} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{4 \cdot 2 \cdot 1}{7 \cdot 7 \cdot 2} = \frac{8}{98} = \frac{4}{49}$$

**Posterior for "No":**

$$P(\text{No} \mid \text{Sunny}, \text{Weak}) \propto P(\text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{No}) \cdot P(\text{Weak} \mid \text{No})$$

Substituting:

$$P(\text{No} \mid \cdot) \propto \frac{3}{7} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{5} = \frac{3 \cdot 1 \cdot 3}{7 \cdot 2 \cdot 5} = \frac{9}{70}$$

### Step 4 — Compare Posterior Scores (Unnormalized)

Convert to a common denominator to compare:

$$P(\text{Yes} \mid \cdot) \propto \frac{4}{49} = \frac{40}{490}$$

$$P(\text{No} \mid \cdot) \propto \frac{9}{70} = \frac{63}{490}$$

Since $\frac{63}{490} > \frac{40}{490}$, we conclude:

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k \mid \text{Sunny}, \text{Weak}) = \textbf{No}$$

### Step 5 — (Optional) Normalize to Get True Probabilities

$$P(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{4}{49} + \frac{9}{70} = \frac{40}{490} + \frac{63}{490} = \frac{103}{490}$$

$$P(\text{Yes} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{40/490}{103/490} = \frac{40}{103} \approx 0.388$$

$$P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{63/490}{103/490} = \frac{63}{103} \approx 0.612$$

### 3.2 Gaussian Naive Bayes — Parameter Estimation

When features are **continuous**, we estimate parameters per class:

$$\mu_{C_k} = \frac{1}{N_{C_k}} \sum_{j: y_j = C_k} x_{i}^{(j)}$$

$$\sigma_{C_k}^2 = \frac{1}{N_{C_k}} \sum_{j: y_j = C_k} \left( x_{i}^{(j)} - \mu_{C_k} \right)^2$$

Then plug into the Gaussian likelihood:

$$P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{C_k}^2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu_{C_k})^2}{2\sigma_{C_k}^2} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Gaussian NB is **parameter-free in form** — the algorithm *learns* $\mu_{C_k}$ and $\sigma_{C_k}^2$ from the training data per (feature, class) pair. You do not need to assume a known variance; it is estimated by **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**.

### 3.3 Decision Boundary Insight (KTU Derivative Favourite)

For a 2-class problem with binary features, taking the log of the posterior ratio yields the decision rule:

$$\log \frac{P(C_1 \mid \mathbf{x})}{P(C_2 \mid \mathbf{x})} = \log \frac{P(C_1)}{P(C_2)} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log \frac{P(x_i \mid C_1)}{P(x_i \mid C_2)}$$

The decision boundary is the **hyperplane** where this ratio equals **zero** (the two posteriors are equal). This is **linear in log-space**, which is why Naive Bayes — despite its probabilistic origin — produces linear decision surfaces in many practical settings.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Naive Bayes Classification Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Dataset D] --> B[Compute Class Priors P Ck]
    A --> C[Estimate Likelihoods P xi mid Ck]
    B --> D[Store Prior Table]
    C --> E[Apply Smoothing alpha if needed]
    E --> F[Store Likelihood Table]
    D --> G[New Test Instance x]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Multiply Prior times Likelihood Product]
    H --> I[Argmax over Classes]
    I --> J[Predicted Class Label]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — Generative vs. Discriminative

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Feature Vector x] --> B[Bayes Theorem Engine]
    B --> C[Naive Independence Decomposition]
    C --> D[Multiply Class Prior and Likelihoods]
    D --> E[Argmax Decision Rule]
    E --> F[Output Class Ck]
    subgraph NB_Internals [Naive Bayes Internal]
        B
        C
        D
    end
```

### 4.3 Tree Diagram — Naive Bayes Variants Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Identify Feature Type] --> Q1{Is Feature Continuous?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> GNB[Gaussian Naive Bayes]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is Feature a Count?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> MNB[Multinomial Naive Bayes]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is Feature Binary 0 or 1?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> BNB[Bernoulli Naive Bayes]
    Q3 -- No --> Cat[Categorical Naive Bayes]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Safeguard Compliance

- **Node IDs** (e.g., `A`, `B`, `Q1`, `GNB`, `NB_Internals`) are purely alphanumeric or use underscore separators — no reserved keywords.
- **Labels** inside double quotes contain only plain uppercase/lowercase text — no bold, italics, or HTML markup.
- **Subgraph** is used once with a clean descriptive label.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State Bayes' Theorem and explain the meaning of each term involved.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bayes' Theorem is a fundamental result in probability theory that expresses the **posterior probability** of a hypothesis in terms of the **prior probability** and the **likelihood**:

$$P(C \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}$$

Where:
- $P(C \mid X)$ — **Posterior**: probability of class $C$ given observation $X$.
- $P(X \mid C)$ — **Likelihood**: probability of observing $X$ given class $C$.
- $P(C)$ — **Prior**: initial probability of class $C$ (from training data).
- $P(X)$ — **Evidence**: total probability of observing $X$ (acts as a normalizing constant).

*(Full 3 marks: 2 marks for formula + 1 mark for terminology explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the "naive" assumption in Naive Bayes classification? Why is it called "naive"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Naive Bayes classifier assumes **conditional independence** among all features given the class label. Mathematically:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

It is called "naive" because this assumption is **rarely true in real-world data** — features often exhibit dependencies. For example, in text classification, the words "machine" and "learning" frequently co-occur, violating independence. Despite this, the classifier often performs remarkably well in practice.

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark for the assumption + 1 mark for the formula + 1 mark for justification.)*

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive the Naive Bayes classification rule from Bayes' theorem. Clearly state the independence assumption used. Explain how the zero-frequency problem is handled using Laplace smoothing.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Consider the dataset below. Using Naive Bayes, classify the new instance $(\text{Color} = \text{Red}, \text{Shape} = \text{Round}, \text{Taste} = \text{Sweet})$. Apply Laplace smoothing with $\alpha = 1$.

| Color | Shape | Taste | Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| Red | Round | Sweet | Fruit |
| Yellow | Long | Sweet | Fruit |
| Red | Round | Sour | Vegetable |
| Green | Round | Sweet | Vegetable |
| Red | Long | Sweet | Vegetable |
| Yellow | Round | Sour | Fruit |
| Green | Long | Sweet | Vegetable |
| Red | Round | Sweet | Vegetable |

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Derivation of Naive Bayes Rule (7 marks):**

Starting from Bayes' theorem:

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

The class with the highest posterior is predicted:

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{C_k} \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is independent of $C_k$, it can be dropped:

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \cdot P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$$

For an $n$-dimensional feature vector, the joint likelihood under the **conditional independence assumption** decomposes as:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

Therefore, the Naive Bayes decision rule becomes:

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} \left[ P(C_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k) \right]$$

**Handling the zero-frequency problem:** If a feature-class combination has zero count in training data, $P(x_i \mid C_k) = 0$, which makes the entire posterior zero. **Laplace smoothing** adds a pseudo-count $\alpha$:

$$\hat{P}(x_i = v \mid C_k) = \frac{N_{x_i, C_k} + \alpha}{N_{C_k} + \alpha \cdot n_i}$$

This ensures no probability is ever exactly zero, providing robustness against sparse data.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for Bayes' starting point, 2 for the independence decomposition, 1 for the final decision rule, 2 for Laplace smoothing explanation.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Classification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Priors (1 mark):**
Total instances $N = 8$. $N_{\text{Fruit}} = 3$, $N_{\text{Vegetable}} = 5$.

$$P(\text{Fruit}) = \frac{3}{8}, \quad P(\text{Vegetable}) = \frac{5}{8}$$

**Step 2 — Likelihoods with Laplace Smoothing (4 marks):**

For each feature, $n_i$ values: Color = 3 (Red, Yellow, Green), Shape = 2 (Round, Long), Taste = 2 (Sweet, Sour).

**For Class = Fruit ($N_{\text{Fruit}} = 3$):**
- Red: $\frac{1+1}{3+3} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$
- Yellow: $\frac{2+1}{3+3} = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$
- Green: $\frac{0+1}{3+3} = \frac{1}{6}$
- Round: $\frac{2+1}{3+2} = \frac{3}{5}$
- Long: $\frac{1+1}{3+2} = \frac{2}{5}$
- Sweet: $\frac{3+1}{3+2} = \frac{4}{5}$
- Sour: $\frac{0+1}{3+2} = \frac{1}{5}$

**For Class = Vegetable ($N_{\text{Vegetable}} = 5$):**
- Red: $\frac{2+1}{5+3} = \frac{3}{8}$
- Yellow: $\frac{0+1}{5+3} = \frac{1}{8}$
- Green: $\frac{3+1}{5+3} = \frac{4}{8} = \frac{1}{2}$
- Round: $\frac{3+1}{5+2} = \frac{4}{7}$
- Long: $\frac{2+1}{5+2} = \frac{3}{7}$
- Sweet: $\frac{3+1}{5+2} = \frac{4}{7}$
- Sour: $\frac{2+1}{5+2} = \frac{3}{7}$

**Step 3 — Posterior Calculation (2 marks):**

**Fruit:**

$$P(\text{Fruit} \mid \text{Red}, \text{Round}, \text{Sweet}) \propto \frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{4}{5} = \frac{3 \cdot 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 4}{8 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot 5} = \frac{36}{600} = 0.06$$

**Vegetable:**

$$P(\text{Vegetable} \mid \text{Red}, \text{Round}, \text{Sweet}) \propto \frac{5}{8} \cdot \frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{4}{7} \cdot \frac{4}{7} = \frac{5 \cdot 3 \cdot 4 \cdot 4}{8 \cdot 8 \cdot 7 \cdot 7} = \frac{240}{3136} \approx 0.0765$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

Since $0.0765 > 0.06$, the predicted class is **Vegetable**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the three main variants of Naive Bayes classifiers: **Gaussian**, **Multinomial**, and **Bernoulli**. For each, identify the type of features and write down the likelihood formula used.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of the Naive Bayes classifier. Under what conditions does Naive Bayes perform poorly, and how can the zero-frequency problem be mitigated?

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Three Variants of Naive Bayes (7 marks):**

**1. Gaussian Naive Bayes (2 marks):**
- **Feature Type:** Continuous-valued (real numbers).
- **Assumption:** Each feature follows a **Normal (Gaussian) distribution** within each class, parameterized by mean $\mu_{C_k}$ and variance $\sigma_{C_k}^2$ estimated from training data.
- **Likelihood:**

$$P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{C_k}^2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu_{C_k})^2}{2\sigma_{C_k}^2} \right)$$

- **Use Case:** Iris dataset classification, weather prediction with continuous variables (temperature, humidity).

**2. Multinomial Naive Bayes (2 marks):**
- **Feature Type:** Discrete counts (e.g., word frequencies in documents).
- **Assumption:** Feature vector represents counts from a multinomial distribution.
- **Likelihood:**

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \frac{(\sum_i x_i)!}{\prod_i x_i!} \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)^{x_i}$$

- **Use Case:** Text classification with bag-of-words or TF-IDF features.

**3. Bernoulli Naive Bayes (2 marks):**
- **Feature Type:** Binary (presence/absence of features).
- **Assumption:** Each feature is a Bernoulli random variable.
- **Likelihood:**

$$P(x_i \mid C_k) = P(i \mid C_k)^{x_i} \cdot (1 - P(i \mid C_k))^{(1 - x_i)}$$

- **Use Case:** Document classification where only word occurrence matters (not count).

**Summary (1 mark):** Gaussian NB is suitable for continuous data, Multinomial NB excels at count-based text classification, and Bernoulli NB handles binary features, particularly short text or keyword-based tasks.

---

**(b) Advantages, Limitations, and Mitigation (7 marks):**

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Simplicity and Speed:** Easy to implement, trains extremely fast, requires only a single pass over the training data.
2. **Scalability:** Linear time complexity $O(n \cdot N)$ in the number of features and instances.
3. **Handles high-dimensional data exceptionally well** — works great with text data (e.g., spam filtering, NLP pipelines).
4. **Requires relatively little training data** to estimate parameters effectively.
5. **Probabilistic output** — provides class probability estimates, not just labels.
6. **Insensitive to irrelevant features** — irrelevant features contribute approximately equal likelihood to all classes and cancel out in the argmax.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. The **independence assumption is unrealistic** in most real-world scenarios where features are correlated.
2. Performs **poorly with continuous features** that violate the Gaussian assumption.
3. Suffers from the **zero-frequency problem** when categorical values are missing in training data.
4. Cannot learn **interactions between features** (e.g., XOR-like patterns).
5. **Probability calibration is poor** — predicted probabilities tend to be extreme (too close to 0 or 1).

**When Naive Bayes Performs Poorly (1 mark):**
- When features are **strongly correlated** (e.g., height and weight in medical diagnosis).
- When the **class-conditional distributions overlap heavily**.
- When there are **insufficient training samples** relative to feature cardinality.

**Zero-Frequency Mitigation (1 mark):**
Apply **Laplace (additive) smoothing** by adding pseudo-count $\alpha$ to each count:

$$\hat{P}(x_i = v \mid C_k) = \frac{N_{x_i, C_k} + \alpha}{N_{C_k} + \alpha \cdot n_i}$$

This prevents any conditional probability from becoming exactly zero, ensuring the posterior remains a valid, non-zero value.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to drop the evidence term $P(\mathbf{x})$** in the argmax — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if you write $\arg\max \frac{P(C_k)\prod P(x_i \mid C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ instead of dropping it.
> 2. **Confusing Laplace smoothing formula** — many students write $N_{C_k} + \alpha$ (missing the $\alpha \cdot n_i$ term in the denominator). This is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Not stating the independence assumption explicitly** before decomposing $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$ — examiners expect a verbal statement followed by the equation.
> 4. **Misapplying Multinomial vs. Bernoulli NB** — for binary features, do not use Multinomial NB; for count features, do not use Bernoulli NB.
> 5. **Skipping normalization steps** in numerical problems — always show the unnormalized scores AND compare them; do not skip to the final answer.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- [x] **Naive Bayes** is a **probabilistic, generative classifier** built on Bayes' Theorem.
- [x] The defining **"naive"** assumption is **conditional independence** of features given the class.
- [x] Decision rule: $\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$.
- [x] Always use **log-space** for numerical stability in practice: $\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C_k} \left[\log P(C_k) + \sum_i \log P(x_i \mid C_k)\right]$.
- [x] **Laplace smoothing** ($\alpha$-smoothing) prevents zero-frequency errors; formula: $\hat{P}(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{N_{x_i,C_k} + \alpha}{N_{C_k} + \alpha n_i}$.
- [x] **Gaussian NB** is used for continuous features; assumes Normal distribution per class.
- [x] **Multinomial NB** is preferred for **text classification** with word counts.
- [x] **Bernoulli NB** works on binary (presence/absence) features.
- [x] Naive Bayes is **fast, simple, scalable**, and works surprisingly well despite the naive assumption.
- [x] The decision boundary is **linear in log-space**, giving Naive Bayes a hyperplane-based separator.
- [x] Estimating parameters requires only **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** with closed-form solutions — no iterative optimization needed.
- [x] For KTU numerical problems, always show: **priors → likelihoods → posterior products → argmax**.
- [x] Remember: $P(\mathbf{x})$ is dropped in the argmax because it does not depend on $C_k$.
- [x] Naive Bayes is a **baseline** for text classification — but is outperformed by SVM, Random Forest, or deep learning for complex tasks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Tree Algorithm ID3
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decision Tree Algorithm ID3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> **ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** is a greedy, top-down, divide-and-conquer decision tree induction algorithm developed by **J. Ross Quinlan (1986)**. It constructs a classification tree by recursively partitioning the training dataset using the attribute that yields the **maximum Information Gain** (equivalently, the minimum weighted entropy) at each node. It uses the **Entropy** measure from Information Theory (Shannon, 1948) as its splitting criterion.

The algorithm operates on **categorical (discrete) features** and produces a multiway split at every internal node (one child per attribute value). The leaf nodes represent class labels. The recursion terminates when all instances in a node belong to the same class, when no attributes remain for splitting, or when the data is insufficient to make a statistically reliable split.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Flowchart"

Imagine a doctor diagnosing whether a patient has a fever. The doctor does **not** ask every possible question in random order. Instead, the doctor orders questions from **most informative to least informative**:

1. First, ask: *"Do you have a temperature above 100°F?"* — because this single question alone separates most sick patients from healthy ones.
2. If yes, ask: *"Do you have a cough?"* — to narrow down further.
3. If no cough, ask: *"Did you travel recently?"* — to consider another cause.

This ordered list of questions is essentially a **decision tree**. ID3 automates this exact reasoning: it scans **every available attribute**, computes how much "uncertainty" (entropy) each question removes, and picks the attribute that **removes the most uncertainty first**. This is why ID3 is called a *greedy* algorithm — it makes the locally best choice at every node, hoping it leads to a globally compact tree (Occam's Razor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ID3 belongs to the family of **supervised learning** algorithms. It is a **classification** (not regression) technique. It forms the conceptual foundation for its successors — **C4.5** (handles continuous attributes and uses gain ratio) and **CART** (uses Gini index and supports binary splits).

### 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Board Examinations

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Root Node** | The topmost node containing the entire training set |
| **Internal Node** | A node that performs a test on an attribute |
| **Leaf Node** | A terminal node holding the class label |
| **Branch** | The outcome of a test leading to a child node |
| **Splitting Attribute** | The attribute chosen at a node to partition data |
| **Pure Node** | A node where all instances belong to one class (entropy = 0) |
| **Impurity** | Degree of class mixing in a node (measured by entropy or Gini) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of ID3 — Step-by-Step Philosophy

ID3 works on a recursive **"best-attribute-first"** principle. The high-level logic flow is:

1. **Compute the entropy of the entire training set S** with respect to the target class C. This is the *base uncertainty*.
2. **For every unused attribute A:**
   - Partition S into subsets $S_v$ (one for each value $v$ of A).
   - Compute the **weighted average entropy** after splitting on A.
   - Compute the **Information Gain** = (base entropy) − (weighted post-split entropy).
3. **Select the attribute with the highest Information Gain** as the splitting attribute for the current node.
4. **Recurse** on each child subset $S_v$ using the remaining attributes.
5. **Termination conditions** — stop and assign a class label when:
   - All instances in the node have the same class (pure node).
   - No attributes are left (assign majority class).
   - The subset is empty (assign majority class of parent).
   - Information Gain of all remaining attributes is zero (no useful split).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Entropy and Information Gain

> [!NOTE]
> **Entropy (Shannon's Measure):** Quantifies the *amount of randomness* or *impurity* in a dataset. A pure node (all examples same class) has entropy **0**; a perfectly balanced 50-50 binary split has entropy **1** bit (the maximum).

> [!NOTE]
> **Information Gain:** Quantifies the *reduction in entropy* achieved by partitioning the data on a particular attribute. The attribute that **maximally reduces uncertainty** is preferred.

The intuitive justification: a good splitting attribute produces child nodes that are as **pure** (single-class) as possible. The more pure the children, the lower the weighted post-split entropy, and therefore the higher the information gain.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H(S)$ | $-\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | **Entropy** of set $S$ over $c$ classes | $[0, \log_2 c]$ in **bits** |
| $p_i$ | $\frac{\vert C_i \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Proportion of class $C_i$ in $S$ | $[0, 1]$, dimensionless |
| $IG(S, A)$ | $H(S) - \sum_{v \in V(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | **Information Gain** of attribute $A$ | $[0, H(S)]$ in bits |
| $H(S_v)$ | $-\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_{i,v} \log_2 p_{i,v}$ | Entropy of subset $S_v$ after split | bits |
| $\vert S \vert$ | Count of instances | Cardinality of set | integer, dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Students often confuse $H(S)$ (entropy of the whole set) with $H(S_v)$ (entropy of a child subset). Always remember: **Information Gain subtracts a *weighted average* of child entropies from the parent entropy**. The weight is the fraction of instances going into that child.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Decision Trees

Decision trees (and ID3 by extension) are deployed extensively in:

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems** — symptom-based triage and disease classification.
- **Credit Scoring & Loan Approval** — banks use tree-based rules for risk assessment.
- **Customer Churn Prediction** — telecom and SaaS industries.
- **Fault Diagnosis in Manufacturing** — quality control in Industry 4.0.
- **Intrusion Detection Systems** — classifying network traffic as benign or malicious.

The advantage over black-box models (e.g., neural networks) is **interpretability** — every decision can be traced back to a simple IF-THEN rule, satisfying regulatory requirements such as GDPR's "right to explanation."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — The Classic "Play Tennis" Dataset

We will compute the **root attribute** for Quinlan's famous "Play Tennis" dataset. This is a **standard KTU Board question pattern**. The dataset has 14 instances and 4 candidate attributes: **Outlook, Temperature, Humidity, Wind**. The target class is **PlayTennis** with values {Yes, No}.

**Class Distribution of the Entire Dataset (S):**

- **Yes** (Play): 9 instances
- **No** (Don't Play): 5 instances
- Total: $\vert S \vert = 14$

### 3.2 Step A — Compute Base Entropy $H(S)$

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

$$H(S) = -\left( \frac{9}{14} \log_2 \frac{9}{14} + \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \frac{5}{14} \right)$$

**Evaluating each term step-by-step:**

$$p_{Yes} = \frac{9}{14} \approx 0.6429, \quad \log_2(0.6429) \approx -0.6374$$

$$p_{No} = \frac{5}{14} \approx 0.3571, \quad \log_2(0.3571) \approx -1.4854$$

$$H(S) = -\left( 0.6429 \times (-0.6374) + 0.3571 \times (-1.4854) \right)$$

$$H(S) = -\left( -0.4098 - 0.5305 \right) = -\left( -0.9403 \right) = 0.9403 \text{ bits}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $H(S) \approx \mathbf{0.940}$ **bits** — this is the total initial uncertainty we must reduce.

### 3.3 Step B — Compute Information Gain for Each Candidate Attribute

#### **Attribute 1: Outlook** (values: Sunny, Overcast, Rain)

**Subset $S_{Sunny}$:** Outlook = Sunny (5 instances) → Yes = 2, No = 3

$$H(S_{Sunny}) = -\left( \frac{2}{5} \log_2 \frac{2}{5} + \frac{3}{5} \log_2 \frac{3}{5} \right)$$

$$= -\left( 0.4 \times (-1.3219) + 0.6 \times (-0.7370) \right) = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Subset $S_{Overcast}$:** Outlook = Overcast (4 instances) → Yes = 4, No = 0

$$H(S_{Overcast}) = -\left( \frac{4}{4} \log_2 \frac{4}{4} + \frac{0}{4} \log_2 \frac{0}{4} \right) = 0 \text{ bits}$$

**Subset $S_{Rain}$:** Outlook = Rain (5 instances) → Yes = 3, No = 2

$$H(S_{Rain}) = -\left( \frac{3}{5} \log_2 \frac{3}{5} + \frac{2}{5} \log_2 \frac{2}{5} \right)$$

$$= -\left( 0.6 \times (-0.7370) + 0.4 \times (-1.3219) \right) = 0.9710 \text{ bits}$$

**Weighted Average Post-Split Entropy:**

$$H(S \vert Outlook) = \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710)$$

$$= 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 = 0.6936 \text{ bits}$$

**Information Gain for Outlook:**

$$IG(S, Outlook) = H(S) - H(S \vert Outlook) = 0.9403 - 0.6936 = \mathbf{0.2467 \text{ bits}}$$

#### **Attribute 2: Temperature** (values: Hot, Mild, Cool)

- Hot (4 instances): Yes = 2, No = 2 → $H = 1.000$ bit
- Mild (6 instances): Yes = 4, No = 2 → $H = 0.9183$ bits
- Cool (4 instances): Yes = 3, No = 1 → $H = 0.8113$ bits

$$H(S \vert Temp) = \frac{4}{14}(1.000) + \frac{6}{14}(0.9183) + \frac{4}{14}(0.8113)$$

$$= 0.2857 + 0.3936 + 0.2318 = 0.9111 \text{ bits}$$

$$IG(S, Temperature) = 0.9403 - 0.9111 = \mathbf{0.0292 \text{ bits}}$$

#### **Attribute 3: Humidity** (values: High, Normal)

- High (7 instances): Yes = 3, No = 4 → $H = 0.9852$ bits
- Normal (7 instances): Yes = 6, No = 1 → $H = 0.5917$ bits

$$H(S \vert Humidity) = \frac{7}{14}(0.9852) + \frac{7}{14}(0.5917)$$

$$= 0.4926 + 0.2959 = 0.7885 \text{ bits}$$

$$IG(S, Humidity) = 0.9403 - 0.7885 = \mathbf{0.1518 \text{ bits}}$$

#### **Attribute 4: Wind** (values: Weak, Strong)

- Weak (8 instances): Yes = 6, No = 2 → $H = 0.8113$ bits
- Strong (6 instances): Yes = 3, No = 3 → $H = 1.000$ bit

$$H(S \vert Wind) = \frac{8}{14}(0.8113) + \frac{6}{14}(1.000)$$

$$= 0.4636 + 0.4286 = 0.8922 \text{ bits}$$

$$IG(S, Wind) = 0.9403 - 0.8922 = \mathbf{0.0481 \text{ bits}}$$

### 3.4 Step C — Analytical Matrix (Decision Summary Table)

| Attribute | Weighted Entropy After Split | Information Gain | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Outlook** | 0.6936 | **0.2467** | 🥇 1st (Root) |
| Humidity | 0.7885 | 0.1518 | 2nd |
| Wind | 0.8922 | 0.0481 | 3rd |
| Temperature | 0.9111 | 0.0292 | 4th |

### 3.5 Step D — Conclusion of Root Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since **Outlook has the highest Information Gain (0.2467 bits)**, ID3 selects **Outlook** as the root splitting attribute. The subset $S_{Overcast}$ becomes a **pure leaf node labelled "Yes"** (since all 4 instances play). The recursion then continues on $S_{Sunny}$ and $S_{Rain}$ using the remaining attributes {Temperature, Humidity, Wind}.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ID3 Algorithm — Top-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start ID3 Algorithm]) --> inpB[Input Training Set S and Attribute List A]
    inpB --> chkC{Is S pure or A empty?}
    chkC -- Yes --> leafD[Create Leaf Node with Majority Class]
    chkC -- No --> calcE[Compute H of S]
    calcE --> loopF[For each attribute Ai in A]
    loopF --> compG[Compute Weighted Entropy for Ai]
    compG --> gainH[Compute Information Gain IG of Ai]
    gainH --> trackI[Track Attribute with Maximum IG]
    trackI --> loopF
    loopF -- All Attributes Done --> selectJ[Select Best Attribute A Best]
    selectJ --> rootK[Create Internal Node Test on A Best]
    rootK --> splitL[Partition S into Subsets Sv by Value v]
    splitL --> recurM[Recursively Call ID3 on Each Sv]
    recurM --> stopN([Return Constructed Tree])
    leafD --> stopN
```

### 4.2 Resulting Decision Tree — Play Tennis (Partial View after Root Split)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1[Outlook]
    root1 -- Sunny --> node2[Humidity]
    root1 -- Overcast --> leaf3[Play = Yes]
    root1 -- Rain --> node4[Wind]
    node2 -- High --> leaf5[Play = No]
    node2 -- Normal --> leaf6[Play = Yes]
    node4 -- Weak --> leaf7[Play = Yes]
    node4 -- Strong --> leaf8[Play = No]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagram illustrates the partial tree obtained after applying ID3 at the root (Outlook) and one level of recursion. The internal nodes are labelled with the chosen splitting attribute, and leaves carry the **class prediction**. The recursion continues at *Humidity (Sunny)* and *Wind (Rain)* until pure leaves are reached.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ID3 Module Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["Input Layer"]
        DS[(Training Dataset S)]
        AL[(Attribute List A)]
    end

    subgraph CORE_ENGINE["ID3 Core Engine"]
        ENT[Entropy Calculator]
        WENT[Weighted Entropy Aggregator]
        GAIN[Information Gain Computer]
        SEL[Best Attribute Selector]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["Recursive Controller"]
        TERM{Termination Check}
        SPLIT[Subset Splitter]
        REC[Recursive Call Dispatcher]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["Output Layer"]
        TREE[Final Decision Tree]
    end

    DS --> ENT
    AL --> SEL
    ENT --> GAIN
    WENT --> GAIN
    GAIN --> SEL
    SEL --> SPLIT
    SPLIT --> TERM
    TERM -- Continue --> REC
    TERM -- Stop --> TREE
    REC --> ENT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **Information Gain**. Why does the ID3 algorithm prefer attributes with high information gain for splitting?

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Information Gain $IG(S, A)$ is the reduction in entropy of the target class achieved by partitioning dataset $S$ using attribute $A$.
>
> $$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in V(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$
>
> ID3 prefers high-IG attributes because they **maximally reduce class impurity** in the resulting child nodes. An attribute with high information gain produces child subsets that are more **pure** (closer to single-class), leading to a smaller, more accurate, and more interpretable decision tree. This aligns with the **Occam's Razor** principle — simpler trees generalize better. **(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark justification)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Entropy** and **Gini Index** as impurity measures used in decision tree algorithms.

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Entropy (used by ID3/C4.5) | Gini Index (used by CART) |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Formula** | $-\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $1 - \sum p_i^2$ |
> | **Range (binary)** | $[0, 1]$ bits | $[0, 0.5]$ |
> | **Computation** | Requires $\log$ calculation (slower) | Only squaring (faster) |
> | **Sensitivity** | More sensitive to class distribution changes | Slightly less sensitive |
> | **Bias** | Tends to favour attributes with more values | Tends to favour multiway splits equally |
>
> Both measures are zero for a pure node and maximum at a 50-50 split. **(1 mark each comparison row, with last mark for conclusion)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ID3 algorithm** with its pseudocode. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** as a classification technique.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following dataset for predicting whether a customer will buy a computer. Compute the **Information Gain for each attribute** and identify the **root node** of the decision tree.

| Age | Income | Student | Credit Rating | Buys Computer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Young | High | No | Fair | No |
| Young | High | No | Excellent | No |
| Middle | High | No | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Medium | No | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Low | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Low | Yes | Excellent | No |
| Middle | Low | Yes | Excellent | Yes |
| Young | Medium | No | Fair | No |
| Young | Low | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Medium | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Young | Medium | Yes | Excellent | Yes |
| Middle | Medium | No | Excellent | Yes |
| Middle | High | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Medium | No | Excellent | No |

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Shannon's Entropy**. Derive the formula for entropy of a binary classification problem. Explain how ID3 uses entropy to construct the tree.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the following dataset, construct the **complete ID3 decision tree** step-by-step. Show all entropy and information gain computations.

| Day | Outlook | Temp | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| D2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| D3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| D4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| D5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| D6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| D7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |

---

### 5.3 Model Solution Outline — Question A (Part b)

> **Solution Hint (Full working shown in class):**

**Step 1 — Base Entropy of S (9 Yes, 5 No, 14 total):**

$$H(S) = -\left( \frac{9}{14} \log_2 \frac{9}{14} + \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \frac{5}{14} \right) \approx 0.940 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — IG Computation Table (students must show each calculation):**

| Attribute | Weighted $H(S \vert A)$ | $IG(S, A)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Age | 0.694 | **0.246** 🥇 |
| Income | 0.911 | 0.029 |
| Student | 0.789 | 0.151 |
| Credit Rating | 0.892 | 0.048 |

**Step 3 — Root Selection:** **Age** (highest IG = 0.246 bits).

**Step 4 — Recurse** on each Age subset:
- Age = Young → recurse with {Income, Student, Credit}
- Age = Middle → all 4 are "Yes" → **leaf = Yes**
- Age = Senior → recurse similarly

---

### 5.4 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Valuation Strict as per KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the weight factor $\vert S_v \vert / \vert S \vert$** when computing weighted entropy — students often just *average* the child entropies. This costs **2-3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Using natural log instead of $\log_2$** — entropy must be in *bits*. Using $\ln$ gives a different numerical value and may lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Not showing intermediate $\log_2$ values** — KTU examiners expect the expansion of $\log_2(9/14)$, $\log_2(5/14)$, etc. Skipping these steps may cost **1-2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing Gini with Entropy formulas** — these are different algorithms (CART vs ID3). Always state which you are using.
> 5. **Forgetting to identify the root node** explicitly — the question often asks "which attribute is selected as root?" Without a clear concluding statement, **1 mark is lost**.
> 6. **Treating $0 \log_2 0$ as undefined** — by convention it equals **0**; stating this explicitly demonstrates mathematical maturity and earns a **bonus mark**.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — ID3 Algorithm**
>
> ✅ **Originator:** J. Ross Quinlan, 1986.
> ✅ **Type:** Supervised, classification, greedy, top-down.
> ✅ **Splitting Criterion:** **Information Gain** (maximize) = Entropy Reduction.
> ✅ **Entropy Formula:** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$, range $[0, \log_2 c]$ bits.
> ✅ **Information Gain Formula:** $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$.
> ✅ **Pure Node → Entropy 0 → Leaf** — recursion terminates.
> ✅ **Handles Only Categorical Attributes** (no continuous features; that's C4.5).
> ✅ **No Pruning in Vanilla ID3** — leads to overfitting on noisy data.
> ✅ **Bias:** Favours attributes with **many distinct values** (multi-valued attributes artificially inflate IG) — solved by **Gain Ratio** in C4.5.
> ✅ **Algorithm Complexity:** $O(m \cdot n \log n)$ where $m$ = attributes, $n$ = instances.
> ✅ **Limitation:** No handling of missing values, no regression, sensitive to small data changes (high variance).
> ✅ **Successors:** **C4.5** (gain ratio, continuous attributes, pruning) and **CART** (Gini, binary splits, regression trees).
> ✅ **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Always prefer a **shallower tree** if multiple attributes yield similar IG — applies Occam's Razor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning Clustering - Similarity Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning Clustering - Similarity Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Clustering** is an **Unsupervised Learning** technique that partitions an unlabeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ into $k$ homogeneous groups (clusters) $\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ such that intra-cluster similarity is **maximized** and inter-cluster similarity is **minimized**.

**Similarity Measure** is a real-valued function $S: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that quantifies the degree to which two data points $x_i$ and $x_j$ are alike. Conversely, a **Dissimilarity Measure** (distance) $D: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ quantifies how different they are. Mathematically, similarity and dissimilarity are often inversely related:

$$S(x_i, x_j) = \frac{1}{1 + D(x_i, x_j)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Clustering is a foundational unsupervised paradigm. The choice of similarity measure directly determines the geometry of the clusters formed. Selecting the wrong metric is the **most common cause of poor clustering performance** in board examinations and real-world systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **librarian organizing a vast, unlabelled library** of books scattered across the floor. You have no predefined categories. To sort them, you intuitively group books that *feel* similar (e.g., thick mathematics books together, thin novels together). The "feeling of similarity" is precisely what a **similarity measure** captures numerically.

- **Euclidean distance** is like measuring the *straight-line gap* between two books placed on a grid floor.
- **Manhattan distance** is like walking along the *aisles* of the library—you can only move horizontally or vertically.
- **Cosine similarity** is like comparing the *angle* at which two books are oriented, ignoring their size.

This is the essence of clustering similarity measures: translating human intuition into a rigorous mathematical form.

### 1.3 Properties of a Valid Metric (Distance Function)

A function $D(x, y)$ qualifies as a **metric** if it satisfies the following four axioms:

| Property | Mathematical Form | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-negativity** | $D(x, y) \geq 0$ | Distance is never negative. |
| **Identity of Indiscernibles** | $D(x, y) = 0 \iff x = y$ | A point is distance zero only from itself. |
| **Symmetry** | $D(x, y) = D(y, x)$ | The distance from A to B equals B to A. |
| **Triangle Inequality** | $D(x, z) \leq D(x, y) + D(y, z)$ | The shortest path between two points is a straight line. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Major Similarity/Dissimilarity Measures

#### 2.1.1 Euclidean Distance ($L_2$ Norm)
The most intuitive, geometric "as-the-crow-flies" distance in an $n$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^n$.

- **Why it matters:** It is the default in K-means, hierarchical clustering, and DBSCAN.
- **How it works:** It computes the square root of the sum of squared differences along each feature dimension.
- **Limitation:** Highly sensitive to feature scaling and outliers because distances are squared.

#### 2.1.2 Manhattan Distance ($L_1$ Norm, City-Block Distance)
Sums the absolute differences along each axis, resembling a grid-based walk through a city.

- **Why it matters:** More robust to outliers than Euclidean; works well for high-dimensional sparse data (e.g., text).
- **How it works:** It accumulates independent contributions per dimension, so one extreme dimension cannot dominate the result.

#### 2.1.3 Minkowski Distance ($L_p$ Norm)
A generalized family parameterized by order $p \geq 1$ that unifies Euclidean ($p=2$) and Manhattan ($p=1$).

- **Why it matters:** It allows the data scientist to tune the metric's sensitivity to large single-dimension differences.
- **How it works:** As $p \to \infty$, it converges to the Chebyshev distance.

#### 2.1.4 Chebyshev Distance ($L_\infty$ Norm)
Takes the maximum absolute coordinate-wise difference—useful when the worst-case deviation matters (e.g., chess king moves, warehouse robotics).

#### 2.1.5 Cosine Similarity
Measures the cosine of the angle between two non-zero vectors, capturing **orientation** rather than magnitude.

- **Why it matters:** Critical for text mining, document clustering, and recommendation systems where document length varies but topic overlap matters.
- **How it works:** Computes the dot product normalized by the product of vector magnitudes.

#### 2.1.6 Jaccard Similarity Coefficient
Defined for sets (or binary vectors) as the ratio of intersection to union.

- **Why it matters:** The de-facto metric for clustering categorical, binary, or set-based data (e.g., market-basket analysis, community detection).
- **How it works:** $J = 1$ means identical sets; $J = 0$ means disjoint sets.

#### 2.1.7 Mahalanobis Distance
A scale-invariant, correlation-aware distance that uses the inverse of the covariance matrix $\Sigma^{-1}$.

- **Why it matters:** Indispensable when features are correlated or have different units (e.g., height in cm vs. weight in kg).
- **How it works:** It whitens the data by transforming it into a space where features are decorrelated and unit-variance.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize all formulas below. Question A of Part B (14 marks) frequently asks for "Explain any three similarity measures with formulas" or "Compute the distance matrix for a given dataset using X metric."

| Measure | Mathematical Formula | Range | Symmetric? | Metric? | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Euclidean** | $D_E = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x_{ik} - x_{jk})^2}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Yes | Yes | K-Means, general numeric data |
| **Manhattan** | $D_M = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Yes | Yes | High-dimensional sparse data |
| **Minkowski** | $D_p = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Yes | Yes (if $p \geq 1$) | Generalized metric family |
| **Chebyshev** | $D_C = \max_{k} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Yes | Yes | Warehouse logistics, chess |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $S_C = \frac{\sum_k x_{ik} x_{jk}}{\sqrt{\sum_k x_{ik}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_k x_{jk}^2}}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Yes | No (it's a similarity) | Document clustering, NLP |
| **Cosine Distance** | $D_C = 1 - S_C$ | $[0, 2]$ | Yes | Pseudo-metric | Used inside clustering algorithms |
| **Jaccard Similarity** | $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Yes | No (it's a similarity) | Set/binary data, market basket |
| **Jaccard Distance** | $D_J = 1 - J(A,B)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Yes | Metric | Categorical clustering |
| **Mahalanobis** | $D_M(x_i, x_j) = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x_i - x_j)}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Yes | Yes | Correlated, multi-scale data |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Cosine similarity is **NOT** a true distance metric because it does **not** satisfy the triangle inequality in all cases. Always refer to it as a **similarity measure**, not a distance.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics:** Jaccard similarity clusters genes based on shared mutation sets.
- **Recommender Systems:** Cosine similarity ranks movies by user-preference vector orientation.
- **Image Processing:** Mahalanobis distance identifies anomalies in pixel feature spaces (e.g., defect detection).
- **Anomaly Detection:** Mahalanobis flags outliers as points with $D_M > \chi^2_{n,\alpha}$ threshold.
- **IoT & Sensor Networks:** Manhattan distance efficiently clusters readings on grid-deployed sensor arrays.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example: Constructing a Distance Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2-Mark Sub-Question):**
> Given four 2-D data points, compute the Euclidean and Manhattan distance matrices.
> $P_1 = (1, 2)$, $P_2 = (3, 5)$, $P_3 = (2, 1)$, $P_4 = (5, 4)$

#### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Euclidean Distance Computation

We apply the formula $D_E = \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2}$ for every pair.

**Step 1:** Compute $D_E(P_1, P_2)$

$$D_E(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{(1 - 3)^2 + (2 - 5)^2}$$

Expand the squared terms inside the square root:

$$= \sqrt{(-2)^2 + (-3)^2}$$

Evaluate the squares. Note that $(-2)^2 = 4$ and $(-3)^2 = 9$:

$$= \sqrt{4 + 9}$$

Add the squared components:

$$= \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$$

**Step 2:** Compute $D_E(P_1, P_3)$

$$D_E(P_1, P_3) = \sqrt{(1 - 2)^2 + (2 - 1)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{(-1)^2 + (1)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

**Step 3:** Compute $D_E(P_1, P_4)$

$$D_E(P_1, P_4) = \sqrt{(1 - 5)^2 + (2 - 4)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{(-4)^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 4} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.472$$

**Step 4:** Compute $D_E(P_2, P_3)$

$$D_E(P_2, P_3) = \sqrt{(3 - 2)^2 + (5 - 1)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{1^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{1 + 16} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.123$$

**Step 5:** Compute $D_E(P_2, P_4)$

$$D_E(P_2, P_4) = \sqrt{(3 - 5)^2 + (5 - 4)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

**Step 6:** Compute $D_E(P_3, P_4)$

$$D_E(P_3, P_4) = \sqrt{(2 - 5)^2 + (1 - 4)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{9 + 9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.243$$

**Final Euclidean Distance Matrix (Symmetric, Zero Diagonal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{Euclidean} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3.606 & 1.414 & 4.472 \\
3.606 & 0 & 4.123 & 2.236 \\
1.414 & 4.123 & 0 & 4.243 \\
4.472 & 2.236 & 4.243 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Manhattan Distance Computation

Apply $D_M = \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$ for the same pairs.

**Step 1:** $D_M(P_1, P_2) = \vert 1 - 3 \vert + \vert 2 - 5 \vert = 2 + 3 = 5$

**Step 2:** $D_M(P_1, P_3) = \vert 1 - 2 \vert + \vert 2 - 1 \vert = 1 + 1 = 2$

**Step 3:** $D_M(P_1, P_4) = \vert 1 - 5 \vert + \vert 2 - 4 \vert = 4 + 2 = 6$

**Step 4:** $D_M(P_2, P_3) = \vert 3 - 2 \vert + \vert 5 - 1 \vert = 1 + 4 = 5$

**Step 5:** $D_M(P_2, P_4) = \vert 3 - 5 \vert + \vert 5 - 4 \vert = 2 + 1 = 3$

**Step 6:** $D_M(P_3, P_4) = \vert 2 - 5 \vert + \vert 1 - 4 \vert = 3 + 3 = 6$

**Final Manhattan Distance Matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{Manhattan} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 5 & 2 & 6 \\
5 & 0 & 5 & 3 \\
2 & 5 & 0 & 6 \\
6 & 3 & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation for Examiners:** Notice that $D_M \geq D_E$ for all pairs. This is a general property derived from the triangle inequality in $L_p$ spaces: the Minkowski metric is monotonically non-decreasing in $p$ for any fixed pair of points.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Euclidean | Manhattan | Cosine | Jaccard | Mahalanobis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Handles outliers | Poorly | Moderately | N/A | N/A | Well |
| Scale-invariant | No | No | Yes | Yes (for sets) | Yes |
| Handles correlation | No | No | No | No | Yes |
| Computational cost | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Output type | Distance | Distance | Similarity | Similarity | Distance |
| Data type | Numeric | Numeric | Sparse vectors | Sets / Binary | Numeric (correlated) |

### 3.3 Proof Sketch: Minkowski Generalization

Starting from the $L_p$ definition and applying the limit $p \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} \left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$$

The term with the largest absolute difference dominates as $p \to \infty$ because raising to a large power amplifies the maximum. Therefore:

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} D_p = \max_{k} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert = D_C$$

This is the formal bridge between Minkowski and Chebyshev distances.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Role of Similarity Measures in the Clustering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Unlabeled Dataset X] --> B[Preprocessing Normalization Scaling]
    B --> C{Similarity Measure Selection}
    C --> D1[Euclidean L2]
    C --> D2[Manhattan L1]
    C --> D3[Minkowski Lp]
    C --> D4[Cosine]
    C --> D5[Jaccard]
    C --> D6[Mahalanobis]
    D1 --> E[Distance Matrix D]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F[Clustering Algorithm]
    F --> G1[K Means]
    F --> G2[Hierarchical]
    F --> G3[DBSCAN]
    F --> G4[Spectral]
    G1 --> H[Cluster Labels C1 C2 ... Ck]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Evaluation Silhouette Inertia]
```

### 4.2 Geometric Comparison: Distance Metric Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EuclideanGeometry["EUCLIDEAN L2 — Circular Boundary"]
        E1[Center Point]
        E2[All equidistant points lie on a circle]
    end
    subgraph ManhattanGeometry["MANHATTAN L1 — Diamond Boundary"]
        M1[Center Point]
        M2[Equidistant points form a rotated square]
    end
    subgraph ChebyshevGeometry["CHEBYSHEV L Infinity — Square Boundary"]
        C1[Center Point]
        C2[Equidistant points form axis-aligned square]
    end
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Which Similarity Measure to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the data type?}
    Q1 -->|Numeric continuous| Q2{Are features correlated?}
    Q1 -->|Binary or set| J[Use Jaccard]
    Q1 -->|Sparse high dimensional| CS[Use Cosine]
    Q2 -->|No correlation| Q3{Sensitive to outliers?}
    Q2 -->|Yes correlated| MAH[Use Mahalanobis]
    Q3 -->|Yes| MAN[Use Manhattan]
    Q3 -->|No| EU[Use Euclidean]
    EU --> GEN[Or use Minkowski with tunable p]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The diagrams above map the functional flow from raw data through metric selection to clustering output. The "diamond" and "square" unit-distance contours in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are derived directly from the $L_p$ norm equations and are a classic KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a similarity measure. State any two properties that a valid distance metric must satisfy.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **similarity measure** $S(x_i, x_j)$ is a scalar function that quantifies the degree of resemblance between two data points $x_i$ and $x_j$, typically returning a higher value for more similar points.

A valid **distance (dissimilarity) metric** $D(x, y)$ must satisfy:

1. **Non-negativity:** $D(x, y) \geq 0$ for all $x, y$.
2. **Symmetry:** $D(x, y) = D(y, x)$ for all $x, y$.

*(Optional 3rd property for completeness — Triangle Inequality)*

> [!TIP]
> **Awarding Logic (Valuator's Note):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each property stated with the equation. No partial credit for properties without equations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Manhattan distance and Euclidean distance. When is Manhattan preferred over Euclidean?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Euclidean ($L_2$) | Manhattan ($L_1$) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\sqrt{\sum (x_{ik}-x_{jk})^2}$ | $\sum \vert x_{ik}-x_{jk} \vert$ |
| Geometry | Straight-line (radial) | Grid-walk (axis-aligned) |
| Outlier sensitivity | High (squares amplify) | Lower (linear sum) |

**When Manhattan is preferred:**
- When the dataset is **high-dimensional and sparse** (e.g., bag-of-words text features).
- When the data contains **outliers**, since squared errors in Euclidean amplify large deviations.
- When features have **different physical units** but should contribute equally (Manhattan gives equal per-dimension weight).

> [!TIP]
> **Awarding Logic:** 1 mark formula difference, 1 mark geometric/robustness difference, 1 mark application scenario.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the following similarity/dissimilarity measures with their mathematical formulations and a suitable application area for each: **(7 Marks)**
   (i) Cosine Similarity
   (ii) Jaccard Similarity
   (iii) Mahalanobis Distance

**(b)** For the following five 2-D data points, construct the **Euclidean distance matrix** and identify the two most similar pairs. **(7 Marks)**

$$P_1 = (1,1), \quad P_2 = (4,1), \quad P_3 = (2,3), \quad P_4 = (5,4), \quad P_5 = (1,4)$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) – 7 Marks:**

**(i) Cosine Similarity (≈ 2.3 Marks)**

$$\cos(\theta) = S_C(x_i, x_j) = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n} x_{ik} \cdot x_{jk}}{\sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n} x_{ik}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n} x_{jk}^2}}$$

- **Range:** $[-1, 1]$; $1$ = identical direction, $0$ = orthogonal, $-1$ = opposite.
- **Application:** Document clustering, information retrieval, recommender systems where magnitude is less important than topic orientation.

**(ii) Jaccard Similarity (≈ 2.3 Marks)**

$$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$

- **Range:** $[0, 1]$; $1$ = identical sets, $0$ = disjoint.
- **Application:** Clustering binary transactional data, market-basket analysis, community detection in social graphs.

**(iii) Mahalanobis Distance (≈ 2.4 Marks)**

$$D_M(x_i, x_j) = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^T \, \Sigma^{-1} \, (x_i - x_j)}$$

where $\Sigma$ is the covariance matrix of the dataset.
- **Application:** Anomaly detection, classification when features are correlated and have different units, statistical quality control.

**Part (b) – 7 Marks (Distance Matrix Computation):**

We apply $D_E = \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2}$ to all $\binom{5}{2} = 10$ pairs.

**Step 1:** Compute all pairwise differences and square them:

- $D_E(P_1, P_2) = \sqrt{(1-4)^2 + (1-1)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 0} = 3.000$
- $D_E(P_1, P_3) = \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-3)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$
- $D_E(P_1, P_4) = \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (1-4)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = 5.000$
- $D_E(P_1, P_5) = \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-4)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 9} = 3.000$
- $D_E(P_2, P_3) = \sqrt{(4-2)^2 + (1-3)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.828$
- $D_E(P_2, P_4) = \sqrt{(4-5)^2 + (1-4)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 9} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$
- $D_E(P_2, P_5) = \sqrt{(4-1)^2 + (1-4)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.243$
- $D_E(P_3, P_4) = \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (3-4)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 1} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$
- $D_E(P_3, P_5) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (3-4)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$
- $D_E(P_4, P_5) = \sqrt{(5-1)^2 + (4-4)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 0} = 4.000$

**Step 2:** Assemble the symmetric distance matrix (with $0$ on the diagonal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{Euclidean} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3.000 & 2.236 & 5.000 & 3.000 \\
3.000 & 0 & 2.828 & 3.162 & 4.243 \\
2.236 & 2.828 & 0 & 3.162 & 1.414 \\
5.000 & 3.162 & 3.162 & 0 & 4.000 \\
3.000 & 4.243 & 1.414 & 4.000 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the two most similar (minimum-distance) off-diagonal pairs:

1. **Pair 1:** $(P_3, P_5)$ with $D_E = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ ← **most similar**
2. **Pair 2:** $(P_1, P_3)$ with $D_E = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$ ← **second most similar**

(Tie noted: $P_1, P_2$ and $P_1, P_5$ both have $3.000$; the second smallest is $2.236$.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose **1–2 marks** by (a) computing only half the matrix and not stating it is symmetric, (b) forgetting that the diagonal is zero, (c) misidentifying "most similar" as the largest value instead of the smallest distance, or (d) forgetting to show the square-root step in Euclidean (using Manhattan formula by mistake).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define Minkowski distance. Show mathematically that Minkowski distance reduces to **(i)** Manhattan distance when $p = 1$, and **(ii)** Chebyshev distance when $p \to \infty$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **supervised** and **unsupervised** learning. Explain the role of similarity measures in clustering algorithms, with special emphasis on why **feature scaling** is a prerequisite. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) – 7 Marks:**

The general **Minkowski distance** of order $p$ between points $x_i = (x_{i1}, \dots, x_{in})$ and $x_j = (x_{j1}, \dots, x_{jn})$ is:

$$D_p(x_i, x_j) = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^p\right)^{1/p}, \quad p \geq 1$$

**(i) Reduction to Manhattan distance when $p = 1$ (3.5 Marks):**

Substitute $p = 1$ into the Minkowski formula:

$$D_1(x_i, x_j) = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^1\right)^{1/1}$$

Since $a^1 = a$ for any real $a$ and $1/1 = 1$:

$$D_1(x_i, x_j) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert$$

This is precisely the **Manhattan ($L_1$) distance**.

**(ii) Reduction to Chebyshev distance when $p \to \infty$ (3.5 Marks):**

Let $M = \max_k \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert$ be the largest absolute coordinate-wise difference. Factor $M^p$ from the sum:

$$D_p = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert^p\right)^{1/p} = M \left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} \left(\frac{\vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert}{M}\right)^p\right)^{1/p}$$

Since each ratio $\frac{\vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert}{M} \leq 1$, as $p \to \infty$ only the term equal to $1$ (the one with the maximum) survives:

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} D_p = M \cdot (1)^{1/p} \cdot n^{0} = \max_k \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk} \vert = D_C$$

This is the **Chebyshev ($L_\infty$) distance**.

**Part (b) – 7 Marks:**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Labels** | Requires labeled data $(x, y)$ | Operates on unlabeled data $x$ |
| **Goal** | Learn mapping $f: X \to Y$ | Discover hidden structure in $X$ |
| **Examples** | Classification, Regression | Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, RMSE, F1-score | Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin, inertia |
| **Output** | Predicted label/value | Group assignments or transformed space |

**Role of Similarity Measures in Clustering (≈ 4 Marks):**

- Clustering algorithms (K-Means, hierarchical, DBSCAN) are entirely driven by pairwise similarity. The **distance matrix** $D \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the sole input that defines cluster boundaries.
- The choice of metric determines the **shape of clusters**: Euclidean favors spherical clusters, Manhattan favors axis-aligned clusters, Cosine favors directional clusters, Mahalanobis favors ellipsoidal clusters.
- Different metrics yield **different partitions** on the same dataset—metric selection is therefore a hyperparameter of equal importance to the algorithm itself.

**Why Feature Scaling is a Prerequisite (≈ 3 Marks):**

- Distance metrics like Euclidean and Manhattan are **dimension-dependent**: a feature with a larger numerical range will dominate the distance.
- Example: If "age" ranges $[0, 100]$ and "salary" ranges $[0, 1{,}000{,}000]$, the salary term will overwhelm the age term, effectively making age irrelevant.
- **Standardization** ($z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$) or **Min-Max normalization** ($x' = \frac{x - \min}{\max - \min}$) brings all features to a common scale, ensuring each contributes proportionally.
- Without scaling, clustering results are **biased toward high-variance features** and may fail to reveal true cluster structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Question B(a), students often write the Minkowski formula correctly but **skip the limiting argument** for Chebyshev, earning only 2 of 3.5 marks. Always explicitly show the factoring and the limit. In Question B(b), students frequently confuse the *role* of similarity measures with the *algorithm itself*—remember: the metric defines the geometry; the algorithm is the optimizer over that geometry.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ **Clustering** is an unsupervised method—**no labels** are used during training.
- ✅ A valid **metric** must satisfy: non-negativity, identity, symmetry, triangle inequality.
- ✅ **Euclidean ($L_2$)**: default; sensitive to outliers; circular cluster boundaries.
- ✅ **Manhattan ($L_1$)**: sum of absolute differences; robust for sparse/high-D data.
- ✅ **Minkowski ($L_p$)**: general family; $p=1$→Manhattan, $p=2$→Euclidean, $p \to \infty$→Chebyshev.
- ✅ **Chebyshev ($L_\infty$)**: maximum coordinate difference; useful for grid/king-move problems.
- ✅ **Cosine similarity**: angle-based; $S_C \in [-1, 1]$; **NOT a metric** (fails triangle inequality).
- ✅ **Jaccard similarity**: $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$; for sets and binary vectors.
- ✅ **Mahalanobis distance**: uses $\Sigma^{-1}$; handles correlated, multi-scale features; reduces to Euclidean if $\Sigma = I$.
- ✅ **Feature scaling** is mandatory for distance-based clustering algorithms.
- ✅ The **distance matrix** is always **square**, **symmetric**, and has **zero diagonal**.
- ✅ A similarity $S$ and distance $D$ are related by $D = 1 - S$ (for normalized similarities) or $S = \frac{1}{1+D}$.
- ✅ Watch for the **"most similar pair"** trap in exam questions: smallest *distance* = highest *similarity*.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Similarity Measures in Unsupervised Clustering**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (HAC)** is a bottom-up unsupervised learning algorithm that builds a hierarchy of clusters by iteratively merging the two most similar (closest) clusters at each step, starting from singleton clusters where every individual data point is its own cluster, until either a single all-encompassing cluster is formed or a stopping criterion (such as a desired number of clusters `k` or a distance threshold) is reached. The output of HAC is a **dendrogram** — a tree-like structure that records the sequence of merges and the dissimilarity (distance) at which each merge occurred, allowing the analyst to "cut" the tree at any level to obtain a flat clustering of arbitrary granularity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > Agglomerative clustering is **NOT** the same as K-Means. HAC does NOT require the user to pre-specify the number of clusters `k` initially (the number can be chosen post-hoc by cutting the dendrogram), and it produces a full hierarchy rather than a single partition. --- ## 1.2 Intuition — The Real-World Analogy Imagine you are organizing a **family reunion seating chart** with **50 relatives** who have never met each other. 1. **Initial state**: All 50 relatives stand alone in a large hall (50 singleton groups). 2. **Step 1**: You notice the closest pair — say, *Uncle Raju* and *Aunty Sita* — they are clearly a couple, so you seat them together. (49 groups remain.) 3. **Step 2**: Now you scan again. The closest pair of people might be two cousins hugging. You merge them. (48 groups remain.) 4. **You continue**... at every step you merge the two **most similar** groups based on a chosen rule (by blood relation, age gap, or shared hometown). 5. **Final state**: Eventually everyone ends up at one giant table — the single root cluster. That decision tree of who got merged with whom, and at what "distance" of similarity, is your **dendrogram**. To pick the seating for the actual dinner (say, 5 tables), you simply **slice the dendrogram horizontally** at the height where it splits into 5 vertical lines. > [!NOTE] > **The "Agglomerative" word explained** > *Agglomerate* means "to gather into a mass." The algorithm **gathers** points upward into larger clusters — hence *agglomerative*. The opposite approach (top-down splitting) is called **Divisive Hierarchical Clustering**. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard) | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Dendrogram** | Tree diagram visualizing the merge sequence and merge distances | | **Linkage Criterion** | Rule used to compute inter-cluster distance (Single, Complete, Average, Ward) | | **Proximity Matrix** | `n × n` matrix of pairwise distances between all points/clusters | | **Cophenetic Distance** | Height of the dendrogram at which two points are first joined | | **Monotonicity** | Property that merge distances never decrease as the algorithm proceeds | | **Lance-Williams Formula** | General recurrence used to update the proximity matrix after a merge | --- ## 1.4 Why HAC Matters in Engineering (Real-World Utility) - **Bioinformatics**: Gene expression clustering, phylogenetic tree construction (evolutionary biology literally uses single/complete linkage). - **Document/Text Mining**: Grouping news articles, legal documents, or customer complaints by topic. - **Image Segmentation**: Pixel-level grouping in medical imaging (MRI tumor segmentation). - **Anomaly Detection**: Tiny clusters formed at very low heights may indicate rare outliers. - **Recommender Systems**: Hierarchical user segmentation for marketing funnels. - **Social Network Analysis**: Community detection by progressively merging connected sub-communities. > [!TIP] > Because HAC produces a **hierarchy**, domain experts (doctors, biologists, sociologists) can *explore* the data at multiple granularities instead of being locked into one fixed `k` as in K-Means. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The HAC Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic HAC operates on the principle of **monotonic merging**: at every iteration, the two clusters with the **smallest inter-cluster distance** are merged, and the distance matrix is updated. ### Algorithmic Steps (Bottom-Up) 1. **Initialize** — Treat each of the `n` data points as a singleton cluster. Build an initial `n × n` **proximity (distance) matrix** `D` such that $D[i,j]$ stores the dissimilarity between points $x_i$ and $x_j$. Set the cluster count to $n$. 2. **Search** — Locate the pair of clusters $(C_a, C_b)$ that minimizes $D[a,b]$. These are the **most similar** clusters currently. 3. **Merge** — Form a new cluster $C_{new} = C_a \cup C_b$. Record this merge event and the merge distance $D[a,b]$ in the dendrogram. 4. **Update** — Recompute distances between $C_{new}$ and every other remaining cluster $C_k$ using the chosen **linkage criterion**. Delete rows/columns corresponding to $C_a$ and $C_b$. Decrement cluster count by 1. 5. **Terminate** — Repeat Steps 2–4 until only **one cluster** remains (containing all `n` points), OR until a user-defined stopping rule is met (e.g., target cluster count `k`, or merge distance threshold `τ`). 6. **Cut the dendrogram** at the chosen height to obtain the desired flat partition. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stop conditions recognized by KTU examiners** > 1. Pre-specified number of clusters `k`. > 2. Merging would create a cluster whose merge distance exceeds a threshold `τ`. > 3. Only one cluster remains (the trivial root). --- ## 2.2 Linkage Criteria — The Heart of HAC The choice of **linkage criterion** defines *how* the distance between two clusters is computed from the pairwise distances of their constituent points. This single choice fundamentally alters the shape of clusters produced. Let $C_p$ and $C_q$ be two clusters containing $|C_p|$ and $|C_q|$ points respectively. ### 2.2.1 Single Linkage (MIN) The distance between two clusters equals the distance of their **closest pair of points**. $$ d_{SL}(C_p, C_q) \;=\;
