> 3. **Confusing $N_k$ with $N$**: $N_k$ is the *effective* number of points (sum of responsibilities), not the dataset size.
> 4. **Omitting the Lagrange multiplier step** for $\pi_k$ and writing the update without justification.
> 5. **Claiming EM finds the global optimum** — it only guarantees monotonic increase to a *local* optimum.
> 6. **Failing to note singularity issues** with $\Sigma_k$ during implementation; this is a common viva question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for EM Soft Clustering:**

- **GMM = weighted sum of K Gaussians**; mixing coefficients $\pi_k$ sum to 1.
- **Latent variable $z^{(n)}$** encodes which component generated each data point.
- **Log-likelihood has $\log$ outside a sum** — analytically intractable, motivating EM.
- **E-step** = compute responsibilities (posterior probability) $\gamma(z_k^{(n)})$ via Bayes' rule.
- **M-step** = update $\mu_k, \Sigma_k, \pi_k$ using responsibility-weighted formulas divided by $N_k$.
- **Three M-step update equations** (must be memorized):
  - $\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_n \gamma(z_k^{(n)}) x^{(n)}$
  - $\Sigma_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_n \gamma(z_k^{(n)}) (x^{(n)} - \mu_k^{\text{new}})(x^{(n)} - \mu_k^{\text{new}})^T$
  - $\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N}$
- **Monotonic convergence**: $\ell(\theta^{\text{new}}) \geq \ell(\theta^{\text{old}})$, but only to a *local* optimum.
- **ELBO** is the bridge — Jensen's inequality turns the intractable log-sum into a tractable sum-of-logs.
- **Soft vs. hard**: GMM-EM yields probabilistic memberships; K-Means yields binary assignments.
- **K-Means is a limit case** of GMM-EM with $\Sigma_k = \epsilon I$, $\epsilon \to 0$.
- **Singularity safeguard**: always add $\lambda I$ to $\Sigma_k$ for numerical stability.
- **Initialization matters**: K-Means pre-training of $(\mu_k, \Sigma_k, \pi_k)$ is recommended to escape poor local optima.
- **Termination**: stop when $|\ell^{\text{new}} - \ell^{\text{old}}| < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-6}]$).
- **Applications**: speaker recognition, image segmentation, anomaly detection, financial regime modeling, gene clustering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dimensionality Reduction** is the process of transforming high-dimensional data $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ (where $d$ is large) into a lower-dimensional representation $Z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ with $k < d$, while **preserving as much meaningful information** (statistical variance, geometric structure, or class separability) as possible.

**Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a *linear, unsupervised, variance-maximizing orthogonal transformation* technique that projects the original data onto a new orthogonal basis such that:
- The first principal component (PC1) captures the **largest possible variance**.
- Each subsequent component captures the **maximum remaining variance** subject to being **orthogonal** to all previous components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** PCA is a *deterministic, parametric* linear feature extraction method. It is **not feature selection** (which keeps original features), but **feature extraction** (which creates new features as linear combinations of the originals).

Mathematically, given centered data $\bar{X}$, PCA finds an **orthogonal matrix** $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ such that the projected data $Z = \bar{X}W$ has maximum variance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shadow on the Wall" Intuition

Imagine holding a 3D transparent cube in sunlight in front of a wall.

- The **cube (3D)** is your original data.
- The **shadow on the wall (2D)** is the reduced representation.
- The cube can be **rotated** in many ways, but only certain rotations produce a *wide, informative* shadow that preserves the cube's overall shape.
- PCA finds the **exact rotation angle** that produces the *most spread-out, least-overlapping* shadow — i.e., it captures maximum variance in as few dimensions as possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just drop a feature randomly?** Because that may discard the most discriminative direction. PCA intelligently *combines* correlated features into uncorrelated components, often improving downstream tasks like classification, visualization, and noise suppression.

### 1.3 When and Why Do We Need PCA?

| Real-World Scenario | Why PCA Helps |
|---|---|
| Face recognition (images with 10,000+ pixels) | Reduces pixels to ~100 "eigenfaces" |
| Genomics (gene expression across 20,000 genes) | Identifies dominant gene expression patterns |
| Customer segmentation (100+ features) | Visualizes clusters in 2D/3D |
| Collinear features in regression | Removes multicollinearity |
| Pre-processing for k-NN / SVM | Speeds up distance computation, reduces curse of dimensionality |

> [!TIP]
> The **Curse of Dimensionality** states that as dimensions grow, data becomes *sparse*, distances become *uninformative*, and models *overfit*. PCA is a frontline defense against this curse.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational "Why" Behind PCA

PCA is built on three equivalent optimization formulations:

1. **Variance Maximization:** Find directions $w$ such that the variance of projected data $\bar{X}w$ is maximized.
2. **Reconstruction Minimization (Least Squares):** Find a lower-dimensional subspace that minimizes the mean squared reconstruction error.
3. **Decorrelation:** Find an orthogonal transformation that produces uncorrelated (diagonal-covariance) features.

> [!NOTE]
> All three formulations lead to the **same mathematical solution**: the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.

### 2.2 The PCA Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

Given a dataset $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ with $n$ samples and $d$ features:

**Step 1: Mean Centering**
Subtract the column-wise mean so that each feature has zero mean. This is *essential* because PCA is variance-based.

**Step 2: Optional Standardization**
Standardize features to unit variance (especially when features are on different scales, e.g., height in cm vs. weight in kg). Use StandardScaler.

**Step 3: Covariance Matrix Computation**
Compute the $d \times d$ symmetric, positive semi-definite covariance matrix $\Sigma$.

**Step 4: Eigendecomposition**
Solve $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ for eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ and eigenvectors $v_i$.

**Step 5: Sort and Select**
Sort eigenvalues in descending order $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_d$. Select the top-$k$ eigenvectors to form the projection matrix $W$.

**Step 6: Project**
Compute the reduced representation $Z = \bar{X}W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates *every* formula required to answer PCA problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. All marks depend on the precise notation used in the university answer key.

| # | Formula | Description / Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{X} = X - \mu$, where $\mu_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij}$ | Mean-centered data matrix |
| 2 | $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}\bar{X}^{\top}\bar{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ | Sample covariance matrix (unbiased estimator uses $n-1$) |
| 3 | $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ | Eigenvalue equation |
| 4 | $W = [v_1 \; v_2 \; \dots \; v_k] \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ | Projection (weight) matrix of top-$k$ eigenvectors |
| 5 | $Z = \bar{X} W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ | Lower-dimensional projection of data |
| 6 | $\text{Var}(Z_j) = \lambda_j$ | Variance captured by $j$-th principal component |
| 7 | $\text{EVR}_j = \frac{\lambda_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$ | Explained Variance Ratio for $j$-th PC |
| 8 | $\text{Cumulative EVR}(k) = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k}\lambda_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_i}$ | Total variance retained with $k$ components (KTU typical threshold: $\geq 0.95$) |
| 9 | $\hat{X} = Z W^{\top} + \mu$ | Reconstructed data (approximation) |
| 10 | $\text{MSE}_{\text{recon}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert\vert x_i - \hat{x}_i\vert\vert^2 = \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} \lambda_j$ | Reconstruction error (sum of discarded eigenvalues) |
| 11 | $X - \mu = U \Sigma_V V^{\top}$ (SVD form) | SVD on mean-centered data; $V$ gives principal directions |
| 12 | $\text{Total Var} = \text{tr}(\Sigma) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$ | Total variance = trace = sum of eigenvalues |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Image Compression:** A $256 \times 256$ image ($d = 65{,}536$) can be compressed to $k = 50$ principal components, retaining 95% visual information.
- **Eigenfaces (Turk & Pentland, 1991):** Face recognition using top eigenvectors of face image covariance.
- **Pre-processing for ML Pipelines:** PCA before k-NN, SVM, or K-Means dramatically reduces computational cost and mitigates overfitting in high-dimensional regimes.
- **Spectral Denoising:** Discarding low-variance components (small $\lambda$) inherently filters noise.
- **Multicollinearity Remedy:** In linear regression, PCA converts correlated predictors into orthogonal ones, stabilizing the design matrix.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering rule of thumb (per KTU 2024 ESE valuation key):** Always *standardize* before PCA when features have different units. When all features are commensurate (e.g., pixel intensities), centering alone is acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — PCA as Variance Maximization

We seek a unit vector $w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ ($\vert\vert w \vert\vert = 1$) such that the variance of the projected data $z = \bar{X}w$ is maximized.

The variance of the projection is:

$$
\text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{n-1} z^{\top} z = \frac{1}{n-1} (\bar{X}w)^{\top}(\bar{X}w) = \frac{1}{n-1} w^{\top}\bar{X}^{\top}\bar{X} w
$$

Using the definition $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}\bar{X}^{\top}\bar{X}$, this simplifies to:

$$
\text{Var}(z) = w^{\top}\Sigma w
$$

We solve the constrained optimization problem:

$$
\max_{w} \; w^{\top}\Sigma w \quad \text{subject to} \quad w^{\top}w = 1
$$

Form the Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = w^{\top}\Sigma w - \lambda(w^{\top}w - 1)
$$

Differentiate with respect to $w$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 2\Sigma w - 2\lambda w = 0
$$

This yields the famous **eigenvalue equation**:

$$
\Sigma w = \lambda w
$$

Multiplying both sides by $w^{\top}$:

$$
w^{\top}\Sigma w = \lambda w^{\top}w = \lambda
$$

Hence, **maximizing the variance is equivalent to picking the eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue**. This is the core theoretical result of PCA.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reconstruction Error Equivalence

We now show that maximizing variance is *equivalent* to minimizing reconstruction error.

Let $W = [w_1, \dots, w_k]$ and $Z = \bar{X}W$. The reconstruction is $\hat{X} = ZW^{\top}$.

The squared reconstruction error is:

$$
J = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert\vert x_i - \hat{x}_i\vert\vert^2 = \frac{1}{n}\vert\vert \bar{X} - ZW^{\top}\vert\vert_F^2
$$

Using the orthonormality $W^{\top}W = I$, we can write $Z = \bar{X}W$, and the projection onto the orthogonal complement gives the residual:

$$
J = \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} \lambda_j
$$

This proves that **discarding the smallest $d - k$ eigenvalues minimizes the total squared reconstruction error** — connecting both formulations.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem Style)

**Given Data (5 samples, 2 features):**
$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 4 & 3 \\ 5 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply PCA. Compute the mean vector:
$$\mu = \left( \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5}, \frac{2+1+4+3+5}{5} \right) = (3, 3)$$

**Step 1: Mean-Center the Data**
$$\bar{X} = X - \mu = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ -1 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Covariance Matrix**

First, compute $\bar{X}^{\top}\bar{X}$:
$$\bar{X}^{\top}\bar{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 4+1+0+1+4 & 2+2+0+0+4 \\ 2+2+0+0+4 & 1+4+1+0+4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 8 \\ 8 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply the unbiased estimator formula with $n-1 = 4$:
$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 8 \\ 8 & 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 & 1.6 \\ 1.6 & 2.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Eigendecomposition of $\Sigma$**

The characteristic equation is $\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0$:

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 - \lambda & 1.6 \\ 1.6 & 2.0 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

Expanding:
$$(2.0 - \lambda)^2 - (1.6)^2 = 0$$
$$\lambda^2 - 4.0\lambda + 4.0 - 2.56 = 0$$
$$\lambda^2 - 4.0\lambda + 1.44 = 0$$

Apply the quadratic formula:
$$\lambda = \frac{4.0 \pm \sqrt{16.0 - 5.76}}{2} = \frac{4.0 \pm \sqrt{10.24}}{2} = \frac{4.0 \pm 3.2}{2}$$

Therefore:
$$\lambda_1 = 3.6, \quad \lambda_2 = 0.4$$

**Step 4: Compute the Eigenvectors**

For $\lambda_1 = 3.6$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 2.0 - 3.6 & 1.6 \\ 1.6 & 2.0 - 3.6 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = 0 \implies \begin{bmatrix} -1.6 & 1.6 \\ 1.6 & -1.6 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = 0$$

This gives $v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

For $\lambda_2 = 0.4$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1.6 & 1.6 \\ 1.6 & 1.6 \end{bmatrix} v_2 = 0$$

This gives $v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 5: Explained Variance Ratio**

Total variance = trace($\Sigma$) = $3.6 + 0.4 = 4.0$.

$$\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{3.6}{4.0} = 0.90, \quad \text{EVR}_2 = \frac{0.4}{4.0} = 0.10$$

**Step 6: Projection to 1D Using PC1**

The projection matrix is $W = v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

$$Z = \bar{X}W = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ -1 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ -3 \\ 1 \\ 1 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7: Reconstruction (for verification)**

$$\hat{X} = ZW^{\top} + \mu = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ -3 \\ 1 \\ 1 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 3 & 3 \\ 3 & 3 \\ 3 & 3 \\ 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Linearity | $Z = \bar{X}W$ | Linear combination of original features |
| Unsupervised | No labels $y$ used | Uses only input covariance |
| Orthogonality | $W^{\top}W = I_k$ | Components are uncorrelated |
| Variance Order | $\text{Var}(PC_1) \geq \text{Var}(PC_2) \geq \dots$ | Sorted by information content |
| Reconstruction | $\hat{X} = ZW^{\top} + \mu$ | Lossy but minimum MSE reconstruction |
| Invariance | PCA is invariant to rotation, not scaling | Always standardize for mixed units |
| Limitation | Captures only linear, global structure | Misses nonlinear manifolds → use t-SNE / Kernel PCA |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End PCA Pipeline (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Matrix X] --> B[Step 1 Compute Mean Vector mu]
    B --> C[Step 2 Mean Center X_bar equals X minus mu]
    C --> D{Different Units}
    D -->|Yes| E[Step 3a Standardize Each Feature to Unit Variance]
    D -->|No| F[Step 3b Skip Standardization]
    E --> G[Step 4 Compute Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Step 5 Eigendecomposition Sigma v equals lambda v]
    H --> I[Step 6 Sort Eigenvalues Descending]
    I --> J[Step 7 Select Top k Eigenvectors]
    J --> K[Step 8 Form Projection Matrix W]
    K --> L[Step 9 Compute Z equals X_bar W]
    L --> M[Step 10 Validate Cumulative EVR exceeds 0.95]
    M --> N[Reduced Representation Z in R n by k]
    N --> O[Optional Reconstruct X_hat equals Z W transpose plus mu]
    O --> P[Compute Reconstruction Error Sum of Discarded Eigenvalues]
```

### 4.2 Geometric Intuition Diagram — Variance Capture in 2D

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Original 2D Data Cloud]
    D1[Data Points Scattered Elliptically]
    end
    subgraph S2[PCA Rotated Coordinate System]
    PC1[PC1 Along Major Axis Maximum Variance]
    PC2[PC2 Orthogonal Along Minor Axis]
    end
    subgraph S3[Reduced 1D Projection]
    Z1[Projected Points on PC1 Retaining 90 percent Variance]
    end
    D1 --> PC1
    D1 --> PC2
    PC1 --> Z1
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Data Transformation Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Input Space R d]:::input --> P[PCA Linear Mapping W in R d by k]:::process
    P --> O[Output Space R k]:::output
    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Algorithmic Flow with Decision Branches (Comprehensive)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start with Dataset X n by d] --> A1[Compute Column Mean mu]
    A1 --> A2[Subtract mu to Get X_bar]
    A2 --> A3[Decide Standardize or Not]
    A3 -->|Yes| A4[Divide by Standard Deviation]
    A3 -->|No| A5[Proceed to Covariance]
    A4 --> A6[Compute Sigma equals 1 over n minus 1 times X_bar transpose X_bar]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Compute Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors]
    A7 --> A8[Sort Eigenvalues Descending]
    A8 --> A9[Choose k using Scree Plot or EVR Threshold]
    A9 --> A10[Build W from Top k Eigenvectors]
    A10 --> A11[Project Z equals X_bar W]
    A11 --> A12[Output Reduced Dataset Z]
    A12 --> A13[Optional Reconstruction and Error Analysis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note on Mermaid:** The block diagrams above are intentionally rendered as *functional architecture flows* rather than literal physical drawings, in compliance with the KTU Premier Engine's Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no markdown inside quoted labels).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

*Define Principal Component Analysis. State any two of its limitations.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

PCA is a linear dimensionality reduction technique that performs an orthogonal transformation of the data to a new coordinate system such that the greatest variance lies along the first axis (first principal component), the second greatest variance along the second axis, and so on.

**Two limitations:**

1. **Linearity Assumption:** PCA captures only linear relationships in data. It fails on nonlinear manifolds (e.g., Swiss roll dataset). Kernel PCA or t-SNE is preferred in such cases.
2. **Sensitivity to Scaling:** PCA is sensitive to the scaling of features. Features measured in different units can dominate the principal components unless standardized.
3. *(Optional third for partial credit)* PCA assumes directions of high variance correspond to high signal, which may not hold when variance reflects noise rather than information.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Retrospective]**

*Explain the role of eigenvalues and eigenvectors in PCA. What does the magnitude of an eigenvalue represent?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In PCA, eigenvectors of the covariance matrix define the **directions** of the new orthogonal axes (principal components), while eigenvalues quantify the **amount of variance** captured along each corresponding direction.

- The eigenvector $v_i$ is the direction of the $i$-th principal component.
- The eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ represents the variance of the data when projected onto $v_i$.
- A larger $\lambda_i$ implies that $v_i$ captures more information, justifying its selection in the top-$k$ components.
- Components with very small eigenvalues are often associated with noise and can be discarded.

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option A**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Given the following 2D dataset with 4 samples:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 \\ 6 & 7 \\ 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply Principal Component Analysis to reduce the data to 1D. Show all steps including mean-centering, covariance matrix computation, eigenvalues, and eigenvectors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the Explained Variance Ratio (EVR)? If PCA on a 4-feature dataset yields eigenvalues $[4.5, 1.0, 0.3, 0.2]$, determine how many components are required to retain at least 95% variance. Discuss the Scree plot method for choosing $k$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — PCA on the Given Data:**

**Step 1:** Compute the mean vector:
$$\mu_x = \frac{2+4+6+8}{4} = 5, \quad \mu_y = \frac{3+5+7+9}{4} = 6$$

**Step 2:** Mean-center the data:
$$\bar{X} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\bar{X}^{\top}\bar{X}$:
$$\bar{X}^{\top}\bar{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 9+1+1+9 & 9+1+1+9 \\ 9+1+1+9 & 9+1+1+9 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 20 \\ 20 & 20 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute covariance matrix with $n-1 = 3$:
$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 20 & 20 \\ 20 & 20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.667 & 6.667 \\ 6.667 & 6.667 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Solve the characteristic equation. The trace is $13.333$ and the determinant is $0$, so the eigenvalues are:
$$\lambda_1 = 13.333, \quad \lambda_2 = 0$$

**Step 6:** For $\lambda_1 = 13.333$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} -6.667 & 6.667 \\ 6.667 & -6.667 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = 0 \implies v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7:** Project to 1D using $W = v_1$:
$$Z = \bar{X}W = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} -3-3 \\ -1-1 \\ 1+1 \\ 3+3 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ -2 \\ 2 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

The data is now 1D and lies along the line $y = x$ (which is intuitive, since the original data is perfectly collinear).

---

**Part (b) — Explained Variance Ratio and Choice of $k$:**

The **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR)** for the $j$-th principal component is:
$$\text{EVR}_j = \frac{\lambda_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_i}$$

**Computation:**
- Total variance: $4.5 + 1.0 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 6.0$
- $\text{EVR}_1 = 4.5/6.0 = 0.75$
- $\text{EVR}_2 = 1.0/6.0 = 0.1667$
- $\text{EVR}_3 = 0.3/6.0 = 0.05$
- $\text{EVR}_4 = 0.2/6.0 = 0.0333$

**Cumulative variance:**
- After 1 component: $0.75$ (75%) — insufficient
- After 2 components: $0.75 + 0.1667 = 0.9167$ (91.67%) — still insufficient
- After 3 components: $0.9167 + 0.05 = 0.9667$ (96.67%) — **exceeds 95% ✓**

**Therefore, $k = 3$ components are required.**

**Scree Plot Method:** A scree plot is a line plot of eigenvalues (y-axis) vs. component index (x-axis). The "elbow" of the curve (the point where the slope flattens) indicates the optimal $k$. In the given data, the elbow occurs between component 2 and 3, again suggesting $k = 2$ or $k = 3$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option B**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** State the PCA optimization problem in two equivalent mathematical forms. Prove that both forms lead to the same eigenvalue equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the relationship between PCA and Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). How is SVD used to compute PCA numerically for high-dimensional data where the covariance matrix is too large to store? **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — The Two Equivalent Formulations:**

**Formulation 1: Variance Maximization**
$$\max_{w} \; w^{\top}\Sigma w \quad \text{subject to} \quad w^{\top}w = 1$$

**Formulation 2: Reconstruction Error Minimization**
$$\min_{w} \; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert\vert x_i - (w^{\top}x_i)w\vert\vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \vert\vert w \vert\vert = 1$$

**Proof of Equivalence:**

For Formulation 2, the reconstruction error is the residual variance perpendicular to $w$:
$$J(w) = \text{tr}(\Sigma) - w^{\top}\Sigma w$$

Since $\text{tr}(\Sigma)$ is a constant, minimizing $J(w)$ is equivalent to maximizing $w^{\top}\Sigma w$, which is Formulation 1.

**Solving Formulation 1 via Lagrange Multipliers:**

The Lagrangian is:
$$\mathcal{L}(w, \lambda) = w^{\top}\Sigma w - \lambda(w^{\top}w - 1)$$

Setting the gradient to zero:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 2\Sigma w - 2\lambda w = 0 \implies \Sigma w = \lambda w$$

This is the **eigenvalue equation**. Hence both formulations reduce to the same eigenvectors of $\Sigma$.

---

**Part (b) — PCA via Singular Value Decomposition:**

**Connection to SVD:**

The SVD of the mean-centered matrix $\bar{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ is:
$$\bar{X} = U S V^{\top}$$

where $U \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $S \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ is a diagonal matrix of singular values, and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$.

The covariance matrix can be written as:
$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}\bar{X}^{\top}\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n-1} V S^{\top} S V^{\top}$$

This shows that $V$ contains the eigenvectors of $\Sigma$, and the squared singular values relate to eigenvalues by:
$$\lambda_i = \frac{\sigma_i^2}{n-1}$$

**Why SVD is Used for High-Dimensional Data:**

When $d$ is very large (e.g., $d = 1{,}000{,}000$ for high-resolution images), the covariance matrix $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ requires $d^2$ storage — infeasible for big data.

**Advantages of SVD-based PCA:**

1. **No explicit covariance storage:** Algorithms compute SVD of $\bar{X}$ directly (e.g., randomized SVD, truncated SVD in scikit-learn).
2. **Numerical stability:** SVD algorithms (e.g., Golub-Kahan bidiagonalization) are numerically more stable than direct eigendecomposition of poorly conditioned matrices.
3. **Efficiency:** For sparse $\bar{X}$, sparse SVD methods can be used. Truncated SVD computes only the top-$k$ singular vectors.
4. **Handles $n < d$ case:** The economy SVD returns the same result regardless of whether $n$ or $d$ is smaller.

The projection becomes $Z = \bar{X}V_k$ and reconstruction is $\hat{X} = U_k S_k V_k^{\top} + \mu$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCA Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mean-center** before computing the covariance matrix — costs 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Using $n$ instead of $n-1$** in the covariance formula — the unbiased estimator is $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}\bar{X}^{\top}\bar{X}$. The KTU key explicitly tests this.
> 3. **Not normalizing eigenvectors** to unit length after solving the eigenvalue equation.
> 4. **Confusing eigenvalues with explained variance** — the eigenvalue *is* the variance, but EVR requires dividing by the sum.
> 5. **Skipping the projection step** — students often compute $W$ but forget to compute $Z = \bar{X}W$ explicitly. This is the final deliverable and carries marks.
> 6. **Reconstructing without adding back the mean** — $\hat{X} = ZW^{\top} + \mu$ is required; omitting $\mu$ loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Not stating that PCA is unsupervised** — if the question asks for "type of learning," saying "supervised" is a direct 1-mark cut.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam-Ready Bullets):**

- **PCA is unsupervised, linear, and deterministic** — no iterative training, no labels used.
- **Three equivalent formulations:** Variance Maximization, Reconstruction Minimization, Decorrelation.
- **Mean-centering is mandatory**; standardization is required when features have different units.
- **Covariance matrix is symmetric and positive semi-definite** — all eigenvalues are $\geq 0$.
- **Eigenvectors are orthogonal and unit-norm** — they form the new basis.
- **Largest eigenvalue = PC1 direction**, second largest = PC2, and so on.
- **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR)** is the fraction of total variance captured by one PC.
- **Cumulative EVR threshold** is typically 0.90, 0.95, or 0.99 depending on the application.
- **Scree plot elbow method** is a graphical way to choose $k$.
- **Reconstruction error** equals the sum of discarded eigenvalues: $J = \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} \lambda_j$.
- **PCA via SVD** is preferred for high-dimensional or sparse data — no need to form $\Sigma$ explicitly.
- **Limitation:** PCA captures only linear, Gaussian-distributed structure; use Kernel PCA for nonlinear manifolds.
- **PCA is NOT feature selection** — it is **feature extraction** (creates new features).
- **Invariance properties:** PCA is invariant to rotation, but **not** to scaling or translation.
- **Whitening (optional):** Dividing each PC by $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ decorrelates AND normalizes variances to 1.
- **Total variance invariance:** $\text{tr}(\Sigma) = \sum \lambda_i$ = variance of the original data.
- **Formula to remember cold:** $Z = \bar{X}W$, $\text{EVR}_j = \lambda_j / \sum \lambda_i$, $\hat{X} = ZW^{\top} + \mu$.
- **Numerical tip:** Always verify $\Sigma v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ by back-substitution in exam problems.
- **Computational complexity:** Eigendecomposition is $O(d^3)$; SVD-based PCA is $O(nd^2)$ or $O(n^2d)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neural Networks (NN) and Support Vector Machines (SVM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Neural Networks (NN) and Support Vector Machines (SVM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Neural Networks (NN) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Neural Network):**
> A **Neural Network (NN)** is a computational model inspired by the structure and functional behaviour of biological neurons in the human brain. It is a directed graph of interconnected processing units called *artificial neurons* (or *nodes*) organised into an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer. Each connection carries a *weight*, each neuron applies a non-linear *activation function* to its weighted sum of inputs, and the network *learns* by iteratively adjusting these weights to minimise a loss function over training data.

Mathematically, for a single neuron receiving inputs $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ with weights $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$, bias $b$, and activation function $\phi(\cdot)$:

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$$

$$a = \phi(z)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:** The pre-activation sum $z$ is called the **net input** or **logit**. The post-activation value $a$ is the **activation** of the neuron. A network with more than one hidden layer is called a **Deep Neural Network (DNN)**.

---

### 1.2 Support Vector Machine (SVM) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Support Vector Machine):**
> A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a supervised binary (and extended multi-class) classifier that finds the *optimal separating hyperplane* which maximises the **margin** — the perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the nearest data points (called **support vectors**) of any class. When classes are not linearly separable in the original feature space, SVM uses the **kernel trick** to implicitly map inputs into a higher-dimensional space where a linear separator does exist.

The canonical decision function is:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right)$$

where $K(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a **kernel function**, $\alpha_i$ are the learned Lagrange multipliers, $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ are class labels, and $b$ is the bias term.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogies — Plain-English Intuition

#### 🧠 Analogy for Neural Networks: *The Voting Committee*
> Imagine a company boardroom where every member (neuron) listens to a presentation (input features). Each member assigns *importance* (weight) to different parts of the pitch, adds their personal bias, and then casts a *vote* (activation output). The chair (next layer) listens to all these votes, weights them again, and decides the final verdict. Through many meetings (epochs), the board learns which members to trust more — exactly how backpropagation adjusts weights.

#### 📏 Analogy for SVM: *The Widest Possible Road Between Two Villages*
> Picture two rival villages on a map — Class A and Class B. Your job as a city planner is to build a *road boundary* (hyperplane) between them such that the road is as *wide* as possible. The few houses that touch the road's edges are the **support vectors** — they're the only constraints that matter. If the villages are interlocked (non-linear), you may need to first *lift* the entire map into the air (kernel mapping) where the boundary becomes a clean straight line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Both models are **discriminative classifiers** — they directly model the decision boundary $P(y \mid \mathbf{x})$ rather than modelling the full data distribution $P(\mathbf{x}, y)$ as in Naive Bayes or GMMs.

---

### 1.4 The Two Pillars at a Glance

| Aspect | Neural Network | Support Vector Machine |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric Idea** | Compositional non-linear function approximator | Maximum-margin hyperplane |
| **Core Engine** | Gradient descent + backpropagation | Convex quadratic programming |
| **Decision Boundary** | Highly non-linear, learned | Linear in feature (or kernel) space |
| **Training Objective** | Minimise empirical loss | Maximise geometric margin |
| **Solution** | Iterative (local minima possible) | Global optimum (convex) |
| **Kernel Support** | Implicit (via deep layers) | Explicit (kernel trick) |
| **Primary Hyperparameter** | Learning rate, depth, width | Regularisation $C$, kernel $\gamma$ |

---

### 1.5 Where They Are Used in Real Engineering

- **Neural Networks:** Image classification (CNN), speech recognition (RNN/LSTM), autonomous driving perception, medical diagnosis, large language models.
- **SVM:** Text categorisation (spam filtering), bioinformatics (cancer classification), handwritten digit recognition, face detection, anomaly detection in industrial systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Building Blocks of Neural Networks

#### 2.1.1 The McCulloch–Pitts (MP) Neuron (1943)
The earliest mathematical model. It receives binary inputs, computes a weighted sum, and fires (outputs 1) only if the sum exceeds a threshold $\theta$. No learning — purely rule-based.

$$y = 
\begin{cases} 
1, & \text{if } \sum_{i} w_i x_i \geq \theta \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}$$

#### 2.1.2 The Perceptron (Rosenblatt, 1958)
The first *learnable* neuron. Updates weights when a sample is misclassified. The **Perceptron Learning Rule** for a misclassified sample $(\mathbf{x}, y)$:

$$\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \eta \, (y - \hat{y}) \, \mathbf{x}$$

$$b \leftarrow b + \eta \, (y - \hat{y})$$

where $\eta \in (0, 1]$ is the **learning rate**, and $\hat{y}$ is the predicted output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perceptron Convergence Theorem:** If the data is *linearly separable*, the perceptron algorithm is guaranteed to converge in a finite number of steps. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU exams.

#### 2.1.3 Activation Functions (KTU Favourite!)
Without non-linear activations, a deep network collapses into a single linear transformation. The KTU syllabus expects you to know the following:

| Activation | Formula $\phi(z)$ | Derivative $\phi'(z)$ | Output Range | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid** | $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\phi(z)(1-\phi(z))$ | $(0, 1)$ | Binary classification output |
| **Tanh** | $\dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | $1 - \tanh^{2}(z)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | Hidden layers (zero-centred) |
| **ReLU** | $\max(0, z)$ | $\begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Default hidden layer |
| **Leaky ReLU** | $\max(\alpha z, z), \; \alpha \approx 0.01$ | $\begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ \alpha, & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Avoids dying ReLU |
| **Softmax** | $\dfrac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_{k} e^{z_k}}$ | $\delta_{ij}\phi(z_i) - \phi(z_i)\phi(z_j)$ | $(0, 1)$, sums to 1 | Multi-class output |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vanishing Gradient Problem:** Sigmoid and tanh squash large inputs into flat regions where the derivative $\approx 0$. During backpropagation, gradients get multiplied across many layers and *vanish*, halting learning. This is why **ReLU is the default choice** for hidden layers in modern deep networks.

#### 2.1.4 Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) Architecture
An MLP with one hidden layer performs the mapping:

$$\mathbf{h} = \phi_1(\mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1)$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \phi_2(\mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2)$$

- $\mathbf{W}_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the input-to-hidden weight matrix (m hidden units, n input features).
- $\mathbf{W}_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times m}$ is the hidden-to-output weight matrix (k output classes).
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A feedforward network with a single hidden layer containing a finite number of neurons can approximate *any continuous function* on compact subsets of $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ to arbitrary accuracy.

---

### 2.2 The Backpropagation Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** Backpropagation is the workhorse of neural network training. It is the application of the *chain rule of calculus* to compute gradients of the loss with respect to every weight, propagating the error signal *backwards* from output to input.

#### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Forward Pass:** Propagate a training sample $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ through the network layer-by-layer, computing and storing every activation $\mathbf{a}^{(l)}$ and pre-activation $\mathbf{z}^{(l)}$ for layer $l$.
2. **Compute Loss:** Evaluate the loss function $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ at the output. Common losses:
   - **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** $\mathcal{L} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^{2}$ (regression)
   - **Binary Cross-Entropy:** $\mathcal{L} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y^{(i)}\log\hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})\right]$
   - **Categorical Cross-Entropy:** $\mathcal{L} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{c}^{(i)} \log \hat{y}_{c}^{(i)}$
3. **Backward Pass:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$ layer by layer using the chain rule, starting from the output layer and moving backwards.
4. **Weight Update:** Apply gradient descent (or a variant like Adam, RMSProp):
$$\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(l)} - \eta \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD):** Updates weights using one sample at a time — noisy but fast and can escape shallow local minima.
> **Mini-batch GD:** Compromise using $B$ samples per update ($16 \leq B \leq 256$).
> **Batch GD:** Uses the entire dataset — stable but slow and memory-intensive.

---

### 2.3 Support Vector Machines — Deep Dive

#### 2.3.1 Linear SVM: Hard Margin
For a linearly separable dataset $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the hyperplane is defined by $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$. The **margin** (distance between the two parallel supporting hyperplanes $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = -1$) is:

$$\text{margin} = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2}$$

The hard-margin SVM optimisation problem is:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \;\; \dfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$$

$$\text{subject to} \;\; y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \;\; \forall \, i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

The factor $\dfrac{1}{2}$ is for mathematical convenience (makes the derivative $\mathbf{w}$ instead of $2\mathbf{w}$). This is a **convex quadratic program** with a unique global optimum.

#### 2.3.2 Soft Margin SVM (C-SVM)
When classes overlap or are not linearly separable, we introduce **slack variables** $\xi_i \geq 0$ and a regularisation parameter $C > 0$:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \;\; \dfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

$$\text{subject to} \;\; y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \;\; \xi_i \geq 0, \;\; \forall i$$

- $C$ small → wider margin, more misclassifications tolerated (high bias, low variance).
- $C$ large → narrower margin, fewer misclassifications (low bias, high variance, risk of overfitting).

#### 2.3.3 The Kernel Trick
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite 14-Mark Topic:** When data is not linearly separable, we map inputs into a higher-dimensional space via $\phi: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{H}$ where $H \gg n$. Directly computing $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ may be infeasible. The **kernel trick** exploits the fact that the dual formulation requires only inner products $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^{\top}\phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$, which can be computed efficiently without explicit mapping.

| Kernel | Formula $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Hyperparameters | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j$ | None | High-dimensional, sparse data (e.g., text) |
| **Polynomial** | $(\gamma \, \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j + r)^{d}$ | degree $d$, $\gamma$, $r$ | Image processing, moderate non-linearity |
| **RBF (Gaussian)** | $\exp(-\gamma \, \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^{2})$ | $\gamma > 0$ | Default choice, general-purpose non-linearity |
| **Sigmoid** | $\tanh(\gamma \, \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j + r)$ | $\gamma$, $r$ | Historically used, behaves like 2-layer NN |

#### 2.3.4 Hinge Loss (Primal View)
The soft-margin SVM objective is equivalent to regularised **hinge loss** minimisation:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \;\; \dfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \max(0, \; 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b))$$

The term $\max(0, 1 - y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i))$ is the **hinge loss** — zero if the sample is correctly classified beyond the margin, and linear otherwise.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every entry below. KTU Part B questions routinely expect direct application of these formulas.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Neuron net input | $z = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b$ | Pre-activation |
| 2 | Neuron output | $a = \phi(z)$ | Activation |
| 3 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ |
| 4 | ReLU | $\max(0, z)$ | Derivative is $1$ if $z>0$, else $0$ |
| 5 | Softmax | $\dfrac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_{k} e^{z_k}}$ | Multi-class output |
| 6 | Perceptron update | $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \eta(y - \hat{y})\mathbf{x}$ | On misclassification only |
| 7 | MSE Loss | $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}$ | Regression |
| 8 | Cross-Entropy | $-\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Classification |
| 9 | GD update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 10 | SVM hyperplane | $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Decision boundary |
| 11 | SVM margin | $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Distance between support planes |
| 12 | Hard-margin SVM | $\min \dfrac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^{2}$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i+b) \geq 1$ | Linearly separable |
| 13 | Soft-margin SVM | $\min \dfrac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^{2} + C\sum_i \xi_i$ | Slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ |
| 14 | Hinge loss | $\max(0,\, 1 - y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i))$ | Convex surrogate of 0–1 loss |
| 15 | Lagrange dual | $\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_i \alpha_i - \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j)$ | s.t. $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$, $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ |
| 16 | SVM prediction | $\text{sign}\!\left(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b\right)$ | Sparse: only $\alpha_i > 0$ contribute |
| 17 | RBF kernel | $\exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^{2})$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}$ |
| 18 | Polynomial kernel | $(\gamma \, \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j + r)^{d}$ | $d$ = degree |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NN in Industry:** Powers real-time perception in autonomous vehicles (Tesla, Waymo), medical imaging diagnostics (skin lesion classification, X-ray triage), predictive maintenance in manufacturing (LSTM on sensor streams), and large language models (GPT, BERT).
- **SVM in Industry:** Used in genomics (cancer subtype classification from gene expression), high-energy physics (Higgs boson discovery at CERN), text classification (spam, sentiment), and as a baseline in any new classification problem due to its strong theoretical guarantees and convex optimisation.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Gradient of MSE Loss for a Single Neuron

Consider a single neuron with linear activation (for derivation clarity): $\hat{y} = z = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b$. Loss over one sample:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Derivative w.r.t. output:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{y}}\left[\frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^{2}\right] = -(y - \hat{y})$$

**Step 2 — Derivative w.r.t. net input $z$** (since $\hat{y} = z$):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} = -(y - \hat{y}) \cdot 1 = -(y - \hat{y})$$

Define the **error signal** $\delta = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = (\hat{y} - y)$.

**Step 3 — Derivative w.r.t. weights $w_j$** (chain rule applied):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w_j} = \delta \cdot x_j$$

**Step 4 — Gradient vector:**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = \delta \, \mathbf{x} = (\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 5 — Weight update rule (gradient descent):**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} - \eta (\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{x}}$$

**Step 6 — Bias update:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = \delta \cdot 1 = \hat{y} - y \quad \Longrightarrow \quad b \leftarrow b - \eta (\hat{y} - y)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the similarity to the perceptron rule: $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \eta(y-\hat{y})\mathbf{x}$. The perceptron update is essentially gradient descent on the *0–1 loss* with $\eta$ = 1, while the above is gradient descent on the *squared loss* with continuous $\eta$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Backpropagation Through a Hidden Layer

Consider a 2-layer network. Layer 1 maps $\mathbf{x} \to \mathbf{h}$ with $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$ and $\mathbf{h} = \phi(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$. Layer 2 maps $\mathbf{h} \to \hat{y}$ with $z^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)}\mathbf{h} + b^{(2)}$ and $\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)})$. Loss: binary cross-entropy.

**Step 1 — Output layer gradient:**

For sigmoid + binary cross-entropy, the gradient simplifies elegantly (this is a classic KTU derivation):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \hat{y} - y$$

**Step 2 — Output layer weight gradient:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{h}^{\top}$$

**Step 3 — Propagate error to hidden layer** (chain rule through activation):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{z}^{(1)}} = (\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^{\top} (\hat{y} - y) \odot \phi'(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})$$

where $\odot$ is the element-wise (Hadamard) product.

**Step 4 — Hidden layer weight gradient:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \left[(\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^{\top} (\hat{y} - y) \odot \phi'(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})\right] \mathbf{x}^{\top}$$

**Step 5 — Update equations:**

$$\mathbf{W}^{(2)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(2)} - \eta \, (\hat{y} - y)\, \mathbf{h}^{\top}$$

$$\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(1)} - \eta \, \left[(\mathbf{W}^{(2)})^{\top} (\hat{y} - y) \odot \phi'(\mathbf{z}^{(1)})\right] \mathbf{x}^{\top}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The gradient at any layer is a *product* of downstream errors and upstream activations. This product nature is what causes gradients to vanish (when factors $\phi'(\cdot) < 1$ repeatedly multiply) or explode (when factors $> 1$). Techniques like **batch normalisation**, **gradient clipping**, and **residual connections** address this.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: SVM Margin Maximisation from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define two supporting hyperplanes** that separate the two classes with no points between them:

$$\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = +1 \quad \text{(for } y = +1 \text{ class)}$$

$$\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = -1 \quad \text{(for } y = -1 \text{ class)}$$

The choice of $\pm 1$ (rather than $\pm c$) is without loss of generality because we can rescale $(\mathbf{w}, b)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the margin** (perpendicular distance between the two hyperplanes):

The signed distance of a point $\mathbf{x}$ from the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ is $\dfrac{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$. Therefore the distance between the two supporting planes is:

$$d_{+} - d_{-} = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} - \frac{-1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 3 — Maximise the margin**, which is equivalent to minimising $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$:

$$\max_{\mathbf{w}, b} \;\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \;\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \;\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Add the constraint that every point is correctly classified beyond its supporting plane:**

$$y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall \, i$$

**Step 5 — Combine into the hard-margin SVM primal:**

$$\boxed{\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \; i = 1, \dots, N}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: SVM Dual via Lagrangian (Skeletal)

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian** by introducing multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for each constraint:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity conditions** (set partial derivatives to zero):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back** into the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j)$$

**Step 4 — State the dual problem:**

$$\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \; \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \quad \text{s.t.} \; \alpha_i \geq 0, \; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KKT Complementarity:** At the optimum, $\alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0$. Thus $\alpha_i > 0$ **only** for support vectors (points on the margin). All other $\alpha_i = 0$. This sparsity is why SVM predictions depend on a tiny subset of training points.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Backpropagation (NN) | Convex QP (SVM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective Type** | Non-convex | Convex (quadratic) |
| **Optimiser** | SGD, Adam, RMSProp | SMO, quadprog, CVXOPT |
| **Solution Property** | Local minima possible | Global optimum guaranteed |
| **Gradient Cost** | $O(\text{params})$ via backprop | $O(N^{2})$ Hessian (kernel matrix) |
| **Scalability** | Scales to millions of samples | Better for small/medium datasets ($N < 10^{5}$) |
| **Sparsity** | Dense (all weights active) | Sparse (only support vectors active) |
| **Probabilistic Output** | Direct (softmax) | Requires Platt scaling |
