topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maximum Margin Linear Separators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Margin Linear Separator:** A *Maximum Margin Linear Separator* is the optimal hyperplane that perfectly separates two linearly separable classes of data points such that the perpendicular distance (called the **margin**) from the hyperplane to the nearest data point of *each* class is **maximized**. The data points that lie exactly on the margin boundaries are called **Support Vectors**, and they alone determine the position and orientation of the separating hyperplane.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Machine Learning syllabus, this concept forms the geometric and optimization backbone of the **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** classifier. It is a deterministic, convex optimization problem with a unique global optimum — a property that distinguishes it from neural network–based classifiers.

For a dataset with input vectors $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and class labels $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the separating hyperplane is defined as:

$$\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

where $\mathbf{w}$ is the **weight (normal) vector** perpendicular to the hyperplane and $b$ is the **bias (offset) term**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Widest Road Between Two Cities"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine two hostile kingdoms (Class **+1** and Class **−1**) separated by a no-man's land. You, as a peacekeeper, must draw a **road** (the decision boundary) between them.
> - A *random* road might pass dangerously close to one kingdom's tents → risk of misclassification.
> - A *maximum margin* road is the **widest possible road** that touches only the **outermost tents** of each kingdom on either side.
> - Those outermost tents that "pin" the road in place are the **Support Vectors**.

This widest-road principle yields **superior generalization** on unseen data because the classifier leaves the largest possible "buffer zone" of uncertainty, making it robust to small perturbations in input.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Hyperplane** | A flat $(d-1)$-dimensional decision surface in a $d$-dimensional input space. |
| **Margin** | The perpendicular distance between the separating hyperplane and the closest data point from either class. |
| **Support Vector** | A training sample lying *exactly* on the margin boundary; it "supports" the hyperplane. |
| **Canonical Hyperplane** | A hyperplane scaled so that the closest points satisfy $\vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert = 1$. |
| **Geometric Margin** | The total margin width $= \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The Maximum Margin Classifier is constructed through the following rigorous logical chain:

1. **Assume linear separability:** There exists some hyperplane that classifies every training point correctly.
2. **Define the hyperplane equation:** All points satisfying $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ lie *on* the boundary.
3. **Classify using sign convention:**
   - $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b \geq 0 \Rightarrow y_i = +1$
   - $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b < 0 \Rightarrow y_i = -1$
4. **Impose canonical scaling:** Rescale $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ such that for the *closest* point on each side, $\vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert = 1$.
5. **Derive margin width:** Geometric margin $= \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.
6. **Maximize margin** $\Longleftrightarrow$ **minimize** $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ (since $2/\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert$ is largest when $\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert$ is smallest).
7. **Encode correct classification** via inequality constraints: $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ for all $i$.
8. **Solve the resulting convex Quadratic Programming (QP) problem** using Lagrange multipliers and the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Math

- **Why minimize $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ instead of $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$?** Because $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ is differentiable everywhere and strictly convex, leading to a unique optimum and faster numerical convergence.
- **Why use the canonical form ($\geq 1$ instead of $\geq k$)?** It fixes the scale of $(\mathbf{w}, b)$, removing redundancy. Any $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ and $(c\mathbf{w}, cb)$ describe the *same* hyperplane — scaling must be eliminated.
- **Why do Support Vectors alone determine the hyperplane?** Because they are the *only* points where the inequality constraint is *active* (equality holds), and by the KKT complementary slackness theorem, only active constraints contribute to the solution.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU University Exam (Module 4)**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Decision hyperplane equation |
| 2 | $y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i$ | Correct-classification constraint (canonical form) |
| 3 | $\gamma_i = \dfrac{y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Functional margin of point $i$ |
| 4 | $\gamma = \min_i \gamma_i$ | Geometric margin of the dataset |
| 5 | $\text{Margin Width} = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Total perpendicular width between two margin boundaries |
| 6 | $\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \dfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ | Primal objective (convex QP) |
| 7 | $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \dfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w}\Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]$ | Lagrangian (primal form) |
| 8 | $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ | Optimal weight vector (dual solution) |
| 9 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i^* y_i = 0$ | Bias-constraint from $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial b = 0$ |
| 10 | $\alpha_i^* > 0 \iff \mathbf{x}_i$ is a Support Vector | KKT complementary slackness |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Maximum margin separators are deployed in:
- **Bioinformatics:** Microarray cancer classification (high-dimensional, low-sample).
- **Text categorization:** Spam filtering and sentiment analysis.
- **Image recognition:** Face detection, handwriting recognition (OCR).
- **Fault diagnosis:** Industrial machinery predictive maintenance.
- **Geostatistics:** Mineral/ore boundary delineation.

Their appeal lies in **theoretical guarantees**: by maximizing the margin, the model achieves a low **VC dimension**, providing strong bounds on generalization error even in high-dimensional spaces (per Vapnik–Chervonenkis theory).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Margin Width

**Goal:** Show that for a canonical hyperplane, the geometric margin equals $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

**Step 1 — Set up the two margin boundaries.**
For a hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$, the two parallel supporting hyperplanes (one for each class) are:

$$\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = +1 \quad (\text{Class } +1 \text{ boundary})$$

$$\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = -1 \quad (\text{Class } -1 \text{ boundary})$$

**Step 2 — Use the perpendicular distance formula.**
The perpendicular distance from any point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ is:

$$d = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_0 + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to both boundaries using canonical scaling.**
Since the nearest point on the $+1$ boundary satisfies $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = +1$:

$$d_+ = \dfrac{\vert +1 \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \dfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

Similarly for the $-1$ boundary:

$$d_- = \dfrac{\vert -1 \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \dfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 4 — Total margin.**
The total geometric margin is the sum of the two perpendicular distances:

$$\gamma = d_+ + d_- = \dfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} + \dfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Primal Optimization Problem

**Goal:** Convert "maximize margin" into a tractable convex optimization problem.

**Step 1 — Maximize margin.**
We want:

$$\max_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent reformulation.**
Since maximizing $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ is identical to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$, and $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ is non-negative, this is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$. The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is included for mathematical convenience:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \dfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Encode the "no misclassification" rule.**
For every training sample, correct classification with canonical margin $\geq 1$ requires:

$$y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$$

**Step 4 — State the Primal Problem.**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
& \min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \dfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \\
& \text{subject to} \; y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}}$$

This is a **Quadratic Programming (QP)** problem with a strictly convex objective and linear inequality constraints — guaranteeing a unique global minimum.

---

## 3.3 Lagrangian Dual Formulation

**Goal:** Transform the primal into a dual problem solved more efficiently, especially when using kernels.

**Step 1 — Introduce Lagrange multipliers.**
Assign $\alpha_i \geq 0$ to each constraint and form the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \dfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero.**

$$\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0$$

$$\Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $b$ and set to zero.**

$$\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

$$\Longrightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain the dual.**
After substitution, the dual problem (in terms of $\alpha_i$ only) is:

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
& \max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \; \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j \\
& \text{subject to} \; \alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** Notice that the dual depends only on **inner products** $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$. This is the algebraic doorway through which the **kernel trick** enters — replacing inner products with kernel functions $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ to enable non-linear classification.

---

## 3.4 KKT Complementary Slackness

The KKT conditions imply:

$$\alpha_i^* \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) - 1 \right] = 0, \quad \forall i$$

This means:
- If $\alpha_i^* > 0 \Rightarrow y_i (\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) = 1 \Rightarrow \mathbf{x}_i$ lies *on* the margin $\Rightarrow$ **Support Vector**.
- If $\mathbf{x}_i$ is *not* a support vector $\Rightarrow \alpha_i^* = 0 \Rightarrow$ it does not affect $\mathbf{w}^*$.

**Conclusion:** $\mathbf{w}^*$ is determined *entirely* by the support vectors.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Layout — Maximum Margin Classifier

> [!NOTE]
> The figure below illustrates the canonical geometry of a maximum-margin hyperplane in a 2-D feature space. The diagram is rendered as a **functional block flow** mapping each geometric element to its mathematical role, ensuring Mermaid compatibility.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INPUT SPACE: R-DIMENSIONAL FEATURE SPACE"] --> B["CLASS +1 REGION: w-Top-x + b GREATER THAN 1"]
    A --> C["CLASS -1 REGION: w-Top-x + b LESS THAN -1"]
    A --> D["DECISION BOUNDARY: w-Top-x + b EQUALS 0"]
    B --> E["SUPPORT VECTOR SV-PLUS: y EQUALS PLUS 1"]
    C --> F["SUPPORT VECTOR SV-MINUS: y EQUALS MINUS 1"]
    E --> G["MARGIN WIDTH COMPUTATION: 2 OVER NORM OF w"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["OUTPUT: MAXIMUM MARGIN HYPERPLANE WITH W-STAR AND B-STAR"]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
```

## 4.2 Optimization Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["TRAINING DATA: X-I, Y-I"] --> P2["PRIMAL QP: MINIMIZE HALF NORM-W SQUARED"]
    P2 --> P3["LAGRANGIAN L WITH ALPHA-I"]
    P3 --> P4["GRADIENT EQUALS ZERO: FIND W-STAR AND BIAS SUMMATION"]
    P4 --> P5["DUAL QP: MAXIMIZE SUM ALPHA-I MINUS HALF DOUBLE SUM"]
    P5 --> P6["SOLVE FOR ALPHA-I-STAR USING SMO OR QP SOLVER"]
    P6 --> P7["IDENTIFY SUPPORT VECTORS: ALPHA-I-STAR GREATER THAN ZERO"]
    P7 --> P8["COMPUTE W-STAR = SUM ALPHA-I-STAR Y-I X-I"]
    P8 --> P9["FINAL CLASSIFIER: SIGN OF w-STAR-Top-x PLUS b-STAR"]
```

## 4.3 Decision-Rule Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["NEW TEST POINT X-NEW"] --> R{"COMPUTE: w-STAR-Top x-NEW + b-STAR"}
    R -->|"GREATER THAN 0"| S["PREDICT CLASS +1"]
    R -->|"LESS THAN 0"| T["PREDICT CLASS -1"]
    R -->|"EQUAL TO 0"| U["POINT LIES ON HYPERPLANE BORDERLINE CASE"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define a support vector. How does it relate to the maximum margin hyperplane?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **support vector** is a training data point that lies *exactly* on one of the two margin boundaries of the separating hyperplane — i.e., it satisfies $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$. Only support vectors have non-zero Lagrange multipliers ($\alpha_i^* > 0$), and the optimal weight vector is computed solely from them: $\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$. All other training points satisfy strict inequality and play no role in defining the hyperplane. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**State the primal optimization problem for finding the maximum margin linear separator.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The maximum margin linear separator is obtained by solving the convex Quadratic Programming problem:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \dfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \; y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \; \forall i = 1, \dots, n$$

This minimizes the squared norm of the weight vector (maximizing the margin $2/\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert$) while ensuring all training points are correctly classified with at least unit functional margin. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Forgetting the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in the objective is a **−1 mark** deduction.
> - Writing the constraint as $\geq 0$ instead of $\geq 1$ loses **2 marks** (canonical scaling).
> - Failing to mention **strict convexity / uniqueness of solution** in derivation-based questions loses **1–2 marks**.
> - Skipping the KKT complementary slackness explanation loses **up to 3 marks** in support-vector proofs.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the optimization problem for the maximum margin linear classifier starting from the geometric definition of margin. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the geometric intuition of support vectors and margin width with a clearly labelled sketch. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Full Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The separating hyperplane in $d$-dimensional space is $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$, with class labels $y_i \in \{+1, -1\}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The perpendicular distance from any point $\mathbf{x}_i$ to the hyperplane is $\dfrac{\vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Imposing canonical scaling, the two margin boundaries are $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = -1$, giving a total margin of $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** To maximize the margin, we minimize $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$. Adding the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ yields the convex objective $\tfrac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Correct-classification constraints in canonical form: $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ for all $i$.

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Final QP problem:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \dfrac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \; y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \; \forall i$$

with note that the problem is **strictly convex** with linear constraints, guaranteeing a unique global solution. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Geometric Intuition (7 Marks):**

- **(2 marks)** Hyperplane drawn as a solid straight line separating two clouds of points (Class +1 above, Class −1 below).
- **(2 marks)** Two parallel dashed lines drawn on either side of the hyperplane at distances $\pm 1/\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert$, representing the **margin boundaries**.
- **(2 marks)** The data points lying exactly on the dashed boundaries are highlighted as **support vectors** (typically 3 to 5 in number depending on dataset).
- **(1 mark)** Annotation showing total margin width $= 2/\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert$ and noting that only support vectors determine $\mathbf{w}^*$ and $b^*$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme – Alternative]`

**(a)** Formulate the Lagrangian dual of the maximum margin optimization problem and derive the expressions for the optimal weight vector and bias constraint. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the KKT complementary slackness condition, prove that the optimal hyperplane is determined solely by the support vectors. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Lagrangian Dual Formulation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Primal problem statement:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \dfrac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \; y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \geq 0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \dfrac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$:

$$\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

$$\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute back into $\mathcal{L}$ to obtain the dual:

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \; W(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)$$

subject to $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$. The dual is a QP in $\alpha_i$ only, suitable for kernelization. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — KKT Complementary Slackness Proof (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The KKT complementary slackness condition states:

$$\alpha_i^* \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) - 1 \right] = 0, \quad \forall i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Case 1 — *Non-support vectors:* For any $\mathbf{x}_i$ strictly inside its class region, $y_i(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) > 1$, so the bracket term is positive. For the product to be zero, we require $\alpha_i^* = 0$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Case 2 — *Support vectors:* For any $\mathbf{x}_i$ lying on the margin, $y_i(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b^*) = 1$, making the bracket zero, so $\alpha_i^*$ can be strictly positive.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Therefore,

$$\mathbf{w}^* = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

since only support vectors contribute non-zero $\alpha_i^*$. Hence the optimal hyperplane depends *solely* on the support vectors. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things You Must Remember Before Walking Into the KTU Exam Hall:**

- ✅ The decision hyperplane equation is **$\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$** — always write it in this canonical form.
- ✅ The total geometric margin width is **$2 / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$** — a frequently asked formula worth **2 marks** by itself.
- ✅ Maximizing margin $\equiv$ Minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ — explain both directions for full marks.
- ✅ Canonical constraint is **$y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$** (NOT $\geq 0$); getting this wrong breaks the derivation.
- ✅ The objective is **strictly convex**, ensuring a **unique** global optimum — mention this in derivations.
- ✅ **Support vectors** are points lying exactly on the margin boundaries; they alone determine $\mathbf{w}^*$ and $b^*$.
- ✅ Lagrange multipliers: $\alpha_i > 0 \iff$ Support Vector (complementary slackness).
- ✅ Dual problem involves only **inner products** $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ — this is what enables the **kernel trick** in Module 5.
- ✅ The primal is solved in $\mathbf{w}, b$ (size $d+1$); the dual is solved in $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ (size $n$). For large $n$, kernel methods are preferred.
- ✅ Final classification rule: **$\hat{y} = \operatorname{sign}(\mathbf{w}^{*\top} \mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} + b^*)$**.
- ✅ Decision function is **linear in input space** — for non-linear boundaries, kernels / soft-margin / feature maps are required.
- ✅ Common pitfalls: forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$, writing wrong sign convention, omitting KKT conditions, skipping the uniqueness argument.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soft Margin SVM Classifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Soft Margin SVM Classifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Soft Margin SVM Classifier** is a generalized formulation of the Support Vector Machine that relaxes the strict separability constraint of the Hard Margin SVM by introducing non-negative **slack variables** $\xi_i \geq 0$ and a **regularization parameter** $C > 0$. It is designed to handle **non-linearly separable data** and is robust to **outliers** and **noise**, making it the practical standard for real-world classification problems in the KTU 2024 Machine Learning syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Soft Margin SVM is the most exam-relevant extension of linear SVM. Questions frequently appear as 7-mark derivations of the primal/dual problem, 3-mark definitions of slack variables, and 14-mark comprehensive problems combining the objective function, dual form, KKT conditions, and the role of the penalty parameter $C$.

### 1.2 Why Soft Margin? — Motivation

The Hard Margin SVM requires that **every training point** lie outside or exactly on the margin boundaries:

$$y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 \quad \forall i$$

This is a brittle assumption in real datasets because:

- **Outliers:** A single noisy point can drastically shrink the margin or make the problem infeasible.
- **Class Overlap:** In most real-world data, classes overlap and are not perfectly linearly separable.
- **Generalization:** An overly tight margin may lead to **overfitting**, harming test-time performance.

The Soft Margin SVM addresses these issues by allowing certain points to violate the margin constraint — at a **cost** controlled by the parameter $C$.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Flexible Fence**
>
> Imagine you are building a fence (the decision boundary) to separate two herds of sheep (the two classes).
> - The **Hard Margin SVM** is a *rigid* fence: every sheep must be on the correct side, and the fence must be perfectly straight. Even one stubborn sheep ruins the plan.
> - The **Soft Margin SVM** is a *flexible* fence: you allow a few sheep to graze on the wrong side, but each misbehaving sheep incurs a **fine** (the slack penalty $\xi_i$). The parameter $C$ controls how strict the fine system is — high $C$ means a strict shepherd, low $C$ means a lenient one.
> - The fence-builder's goal is to maximize the gap between the two herds (the margin) while minimizing the total fines paid.

### 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Slack Variable | $\xi_i$ | Permitted violation for point $i$ |
| Penalty Parameter | $C$ | Trade-off knob between margin and error |
| Margin Width | $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$ | Geometric distance between support boundaries |
| Hinge Loss | $\max(0, 1 - y_i f(x_i))$ | Per-point penalty in soft formulation |
| Support Vectors | Points with $\alpha_i > 0$ | Points lying on or inside the margin |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Position of Data Points (Three Regions)

In the Soft Margin formulation, every training point $x_i$ falls into exactly one of three geometric regions relative to the canonical decision boundary and the margin:

| Region | Condition on $y_i(w^T x_i + b)$ | Slack $\xi_i$ | Status |
|--------|----------------------------------|---------------|--------|
| **Outside or on margin (correct side)** | $\geq 1$ | $\xi_i = 0$ | Correctly classified, outside the margin tube |
| **Inside margin, correctly classified** | $0 \leq y_i(w^T x_i + b) < 1$ | $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$ | Inside the margin, on the correct side |
| **Misclassified** | $y_i(w^T x_i + b) < 0$ | $\xi_i > 1$ | On the wrong side of the hyperplane |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking: *"When is $\xi_i = 0$ for a data point?"* The answer: when the point lies outside or exactly on its respective margin boundary (i.e., $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$).

### 2.2 The Penalty Parameter $C$ — The Trade-off Knob

The regularization parameter $C > 0$ controls the strictness of the classifier:

- **Large $C$ (e.g., $C \to \infty$):**
  - Penalizes every unit of slack very heavily.
  - The model behaves almost like a **Hard Margin SVM**.
  - Risk: **Overfitting** on noisy data; narrow margin.
- **Small $C$ (e.g., $C \to 0$):**
  - Allows many misclassifications for the sake of a wider margin.
  - The model is highly tolerant of errors.
  - Risk: **Underfitting**; the margin may become too wide and lose discriminative power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the dual form, $C$ appears as the **upper bound** on the Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i$: $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$. This is a *very* frequently asked board question.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Primal Objective (Soft Margin)** | $\min_{w,b,\xi} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{n}\xi_i$ | Regularized hinge loss formulation |
| 2 | **Primal Constraints** | $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i,\ \xi_i \geq 0$ | Slack-augmented separability |
| 3 | **Hinge Loss** | $L_{\text{hinge}}(y, f(x)) = \max(0, 1 - y f(x))$ | Equivalent per-point penalty |
| 4 | **Dual Objective** | $\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)$ | Same as Hard Margin, **different bounds** |
| 5 | **Dual Constraints** | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C,\ \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0$ | Box constraints due to soft margin |
| 6 | **Width of Margin** | $\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\|w\|}$ | Geometrically identical to Hard Margin |
| 7 | **Weight Vector Recovery** | $w = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i x_i$ | Only support vectors contribute |
| 8 | **KKT Complementarity** | $\alpha_i [y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i] = 0$ | Linking $\alpha_i$ to margin status |
| 9 | **KKT on $\xi_i$** | $(C - \alpha_i)\xi_i = 0$ | Unique to soft margin |
| 10 | **Decision Function** | $f(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i (x_i \cdot x) + b\right)$ | At test time |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Soft Margin SVM is the workhorse of:
- **Bioinformatics** (cancer classification from gene expression),
- **Text Categorization** (spam filtering, sentiment analysis),
- **Image Recognition** (handwritten digit recognition, face detection),
- **Fault Diagnosis** in mechanical systems, where noisy sensor readings are common.

Its robustness to outliers makes it preferable to Hard Margin SVM in essentially all industrial deployments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Primal Problem from First Principles

**Step 1: Recall the Hard Margin Primal**

$$
\min_{w,b} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2
\quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1,\ \forall i \in \{1, \dots, n\}
$$

This requires perfect separability. We will relax it.

**Step 2: Introduce Slack Variables**

To permit margin violations, introduce a non-negative slack variable $\xi_i \geq 0$ for every training point. The constraint is softened to:

$$
y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i
$$

If $\xi_i = 0$, the original Hard Margin constraint is recovered. If $\xi_i > 0$, the point is allowed to penetrate the margin.

**Step 3: Add a Penalty Term to the Objective**

To prevent trivial solutions where all $\xi_i \to \infty$, penalize large slacks by adding $C \sum_i \xi_i$ to the objective:

$$
\min_{w,b,\xi} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n}\xi_i
$$

The factor $C > 0$ balances margin width and total training error.

**Step 4: Final Primal Formulation**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\min_{w,b,\xi} \quad & \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n}\xi_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i,\ i = 1, \dots, n \\
& \xi_i \geq 0,\ i = 1, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The first term $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$ is the **margin maximization** term (small $\|w\|$ = wide margin). The second term $C\sum\xi_i$ is the **error penalty** term. The parameter $C$ trades off these competing goals.

### 3.2 Connection to Hinge Loss

Observe that the per-point contribution of slack to the objective is:

$$
C \cdot \xi_i = C \cdot \max(0,\ 1 - y_i(w^T x_i + b))
$$

Therefore, the Soft Margin SVM primal is **equivalent** to the **regularized empirical risk minimization** with hinge loss:

$$
\min_{w,b} \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2}_{\text{regularizer}} + \underbrace{C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \max(0,\ 1 - y_i(w^T x_i + b))}_{\text{sum of hinge losses}}
$$

This is the standard form in modern ML libraries (e.g., scikit-learn's `LinearSVC`).

### 3.3 Derivation of the Dual Problem

**Step 1: Form the Lagrangian**

Introduce Lagrange multipliers:
- $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for the constraint $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ (i.e., $1 - \xi_i - y_i(w^T x_i + b) \leq 0$)
- $\mu_i \geq 0$ for the constraint $\xi_i \geq 0$ (i.e., $-\xi_i \leq 0$)

The Lagrangian is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(w, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) = \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n}\xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\mu_i \xi_i
$$

**Step 2: Take Partial Derivatives and Set to Zero**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad w = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i x_i
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha_i + \mu_i = C,\ \mu_i \geq 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C
$$

The last implication is the **crucial difference** from Hard Margin: in the Hard Margin case we have $\alpha_i \geq 0$ only; here we get the **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.

**Step 3: Substitute Back to Eliminate Primal Variables**

Plug $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ into the Lagrangian. After algebraic simplification (substituting and cancelling $\xi_i$ terms using $\mu_i = C - \alpha_i$):

$$
\mathcal{L}_D = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)
$$

**Step 4: Write the Dual Problem**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\alpha} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C,\ i = 1, \dots, n \\
& \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The dual form is **identical** to the Hard Margin dual, **except** for the upper bound $C$ on $\alpha_i$. This is a classic 7-mark question: *"Compare the dual of Hard Margin and Soft Margin SVM."*

### 3.4 Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Conditions

The KKT conditions provide the optimality criteria. For the Soft Margin SVM, they are:

| # | KKT Condition | Meaning |
|---|---------------|---------|
| 1 | $\alpha_i \geq 0$ | Non-negativity of multipliers |
| 2 | $\mu_i \geq 0$ | Non-negativity of slack multipliers |
| 3 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0$ | Stationarity w.r.t. $b$ |
| 4 | $w = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i x_i$ | Stationarity w.r.t. $w$ |
| 5 | $\alpha_i + \mu_i = C$ | Stationarity w.r.t. $\xi_i$ |
| 6 | $\alpha_i [y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i] = 0$ | Complementarity w.r.t. margin constraint |
| 7 | $\mu_i \xi_i = 0 \iff (C - \alpha_i)\xi_i = 0$ | Complementarity w.r.t. slack |

**Three cases from the KKT conditions:**

- **Case A:** $\alpha_i = 0 \Rightarrow \xi_i = 0$ and $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$. The point is correctly classified and outside the margin.
- **Case B:** $0 < \alpha_i < C \Rightarrow \xi_i = 0$ and $y_i(w^T x_i + b) = 1$. The point lies **exactly on the margin** — these are the *free support vectors* used to compute $b$.
- **Case C:** $\alpha_i = C \Rightarrow \xi_i \geq 0$ and the point is either inside the margin ($\xi_i \leq 1$) or misclassified ($\xi_i > 1$). These are *bounded support vectors*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram of Soft Margin SVM Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Training Data X and Labels Y] --> B[Check Linear Separability]
    B --> C{Data Linearly Separable?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Hard Margin SVM]
    C -- No --> E[Introduce Slack Variables Xi]
    E --> F[Formulate Primal Problem with Penalty C]
    F --> G[Construct Lagrangian with Multipliers Alpha and Mu]
    G --> H[Apply KKT Stationarity Conditions]
    H --> I[Derive Dual Problem with Box Constraints 0 to C]
    I --> J[Solve Quadratic Programming for Alpha]
    J --> K[Recover Weight Vector w and Bias b]
    K --> L[Decision Function f of x equals sign of w transpose x plus b]
    L --> M[Classify New Test Samples]
```

### 4.2 Three Geometric Regions of Data Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Region_A[Region A - Outside Margin]
        P1[Point with y times f of x greater than or equal to 1]
    end
    subgraph Region_B[Region B - Inside Margin Correct Side]
        P2[Point with 0 less than y times f of x less than 1]
    end
    subgraph Region_C[Region C - Misclassified]
        P3[Point with y times f of x less than 0]
    end
    H[Decision Hyperplane w transpose x plus b equals 0] --- M1[Margin y times f of x equals 1]
    M1 --- M2[Margin y times f of x equals minus 1]
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
```

### 4.3 KKT Case Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Optimal Alpha and Xi for Point i] --> Q1{Alpha i equals 0}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Point Outside Margin, Xi equals 0, Not a Support Vector]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Alpha i equals C}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Bounded Support Vector, Xi greater than or equal 0, May be Misclassified]
    Q2 -- No --> R3[Free Support Vector, Xi equals 0, Lies Exactly on Margin, Used to Compute b]
```

### 4.4 Effect of Parameter C on Margin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small C] --> B[Wide Margin, Many Violations Allowed, High Bias, Low Variance]
    C[Large C] --> D[Narrow Margin, Few Violations Allowed, Low Bias, High Variance]
    E[Trade-off] --> A
    E --> C
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] What are slack variables in Soft Margin SVM? Why are they introduced?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ are non-negative quantities introduced into the SVM optimization problem to permit certain training points to violate the margin constraint. They are introduced because real-world data is often **not perfectly linearly separable** — there may be overlapping classes, noise, or outliers. By replacing the strict constraint $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$ with the relaxed form $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$, the model can still find a meaningful separating hyperplane while tolerating a controlled amount of misclassification. **(3 Marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Explain the role of the penalty parameter $C$ in the Soft Margin SVM formulation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The parameter $C > 0$ in the Soft Margin objective $\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_i \xi_i$ controls the **trade-off** between margin maximization and training error minimization. A **large $C$** assigns a heavy penalty to margin violations, forcing the model to classify training points correctly even at the cost of a narrower margin (low bias, high variance). A **small $C$** allows more misclassifications in exchange for a wider, more robust margin (high bias, low variance). In the dual formulation, $C$ appears as the **upper bound** on each Lagrange multiplier: $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ is the most common deduction — students write only $\alpha_i \geq 0$, which is the Hard Margin result.
> 2. **Skipping the $\xi_i$ stationarity step** ($\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = 0$) loses 2 marks because it is the *only* place where the soft-margin-specific bound arises.
> 3. **Confusing hinge loss with 0–1 loss** in the error term — they are *not* the same. Hinge loss is $\max(0, 1 - y_i f(x_i))$, a convex surrogate.
> 4. **Not writing KKT conditions explicitly** in 14-mark questions — they are mandatory for full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Formulate the **primal optimization problem** for the Soft Margin SVM. Clearly state the objective function, the constraints, and the role of the slack variables and the penalty parameter $C$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the primal, derive the **dual optimization problem**. Show all steps including the construction of the Lagrangian, the stationarity conditions, and the final dual form. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Primal Formulation (7 Marks):**

The Soft Margin SVM primal is stated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{w,b,\xi} \quad & \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{n}\xi_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i,\ \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, n \\
& \xi_i \geq 0,\ \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of components:**

- **$\frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2$:** Margin maximization term. Minimizing $\|w\|$ maximizes the geometric margin $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$.
- **$C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i$:** Penalty on total slack — represents training error.
- **$C > 0$:** Regularization constant. Large $C$ = strict (less tolerance); small $C$ = lenient.
- **$\xi_i \geq 0$:** Non-negativity of slack variables — they are *amounts* of violation, not negative.
- **$y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$:** Softened separability. If $\xi_i = 0$, point is on or outside margin; if $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$, inside margin; if $\xi_i > 1$, misclassified.

**(b) Dual Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Lagrangian.** Introduce multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ (for the margin constraint) and $\mu_i \geq 0$ (for the slack non-negativity):

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{n}\xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\mu_i \xi_i
$$

**Step 2: Stationarity.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; w = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i x_i
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha_i = C - \mu_i
$$

Since $\mu_i \geq 0$ and $\alpha_i \geq 0$, we obtain the **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.

**Step 3: Substitution.** Substitute $w$ back into $\mathcal{L}$. Using $\mu_i = C - \alpha_i$, all $\xi_i$ terms cancel:

$$
\mathcal{L}_D = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j)
$$

**Step 4: Final Dual Problem.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\alpha} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i \cdot x_j) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C,\ \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Observation:** The dual objective is **identical** to the Hard Margin dual; the *only* difference is the upper bound $C$ on $\alpha_i$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **geometric interpretation** of the slack variable $\xi_i$. Discuss the three possible cases for a training point based on the value of $\xi_i$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** State and explain the **KKT conditions** for the Soft Margin SVM. Using these conditions, classify the training points into three categories based on the value of $\alpha_i$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Geometric Interpretation of $\xi_i$ (7 Marks):**

The slack variable $\xi_i$ measures the **amount of margin violation** for the $i$-th training point, expressed as a distance proportional to $\|w\|$. Three cases arise:

**Case 1: $\xi_i = 0$ (No violation)**

$$
y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1
$$

The point lies on or outside the correct margin boundary. The classifier makes no error on this point.

**Case 2: $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$ (Margin violation, correct side)**

$$
0 \leq y_i(w^T x_i + b) < 1
$$

The point lies **between** the decision hyperplane and the margin boundary, on the correct side. It is correctly classified but contributes to margin shrinkage.

**Case 3: $\xi_i > 1$ (Misclassification)**

$$
y_i(w^T x_i + b) < 0
$$

The point lies on the **wrong side** of the decision hyperplane — a true misclassification.

> [!NOTE]
> Total training error is bounded by $\sum_i \xi_i$, and the objective minimizes this sum weighted by $C$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) KKT Conditions (7 Marks):**

The KKT conditions for the Soft Margin SVM primal are:

**Stationarity:**

$$
w = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i x_i, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n}\alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad \alpha_i + \mu_i = C
$$

**Primal Feasibility:** $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$.

**Dual Feasibility:** $\alpha_i \geq 0,\ \mu_i \geq 0$.

**Complementarity:**

$$
\alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] = 0, \quad \mu_i \xi_i = 0
$$

The second complementarity condition can be rewritten as $(C - \alpha_i)\xi_i = 0$.

**Three categories of points based on $\alpha_i$:**

| Category | $\alpha_i$ Value | $\xi_i$ Value | Position | Role |
|----------|------------------|---------------|----------|------|
| Non-support vector | $\alpha_i = 0$ | $\xi_i = 0$ | Outside margin | No influence on $w$ |
| Free support vector | $0 < \alpha_i < C$ | $\xi_i = 0$ | On margin | Used to compute $b$ |
| Bounded support vector | $\alpha_i = C$ | $\xi_i \geq 0$ | Inside or beyond | Margin violators |

**Computation of bias $b$:** For any free support vector with $0 < \alpha_i < C$, we have $y_i(w^T x_i + b) = 1$, giving:

$$
b = y_i - w^T x_i
$$

In practice, $b$ is averaged over all free support vectors for numerical stability. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Soft Margin SVM** generalizes Hard Margin SVM by allowing margin violations via slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$.
- The **primal objective** is $\min \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C\sum_i \xi_i$, subject to $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$.
- The **parameter $C$** balances margin width against classification error: large $C$ = strict (low bias), small $C$ = lenient (high bias).
- The **dual problem** has the same form as Hard Margin but with the **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ instead of $\alpha_i \geq 0$.
- The **hinge loss** $L(y, f(x)) = \max(0, 1 - y f(x))$ provides a convex, continuous surrogate for 0–1 classification loss.
- **Three geometric regions:** outside margin ($\xi_i = 0$), inside margin ($0 < \xi_i \leq 1$), misclassified ($\xi_i > 1$).
- **KKT conditions** classify points into non-support vectors ($\alpha_i = 0$), free support vectors ($0 < \alpha_i < C$), and bounded support vectors ($\alpha_i = C$).
- The **weight vector** $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ depends only on support vectors, making SVM memory-efficient at test time.
- For **linearly separable data**, the Soft Margin SVM with very large $C$ recovers the Hard Margin SVM solution.
- **Practical tip:** Always **standardize features** before training Soft Margin SVM so that $C$ acts uniformly across dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-linear SVM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Non-linear SVM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Non-linear Support Vector Machine (Non-linear SVM)** is a supervised maximum-margin classifier that extends the linear SVM to handle datasets that are **not linearly separable** in the original input space. It achieves this by employing the **Kernel Trick**, which implicitly maps the input data into a higher-dimensional (often infinite-dimensional) **Feature Space** $\mathcal{F}$ via a non-linear mapping function $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$, where a linear separating hyperplane is constructed.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The core differentiator of a non-linear SVM from a linear SVM is the substitution of the inner product $\langle x_i, x_j \rangle$ in the dual formulation with a **Kernel Function** $K(x_i, x_j) = \langle \phi(x_i), \phi(x_j) \rangle_{\mathcal{F}}$. This avoids the computationally prohibitive explicit computation of $\phi(x)$.

Formally, the decision function for a non-linear SVM is given by:

$$f(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i^* y_i K(x_i, x) + b^*\right)$$

where $K(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a valid Mercer kernel, $\alpha_i^*$ are the optimal Lagrange multipliers, $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ are class labels, and $b^*$ is the optimal bias term.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a pile of red and blue marbles scattered on a **circular table** (2D plane). No straight stick (line) can separate them. 

Now, imagine you **lift the marbles vertically** based on how far they are from the center — marbles near the center get raised high, while marbles far from the center stay low. Suddenly, in 3D space, you can slide a **flat cardboard sheet** between the two colored groups to perfectly separate them!

- The **table (2D)** is the original input space $\mathbb{R}^d$.
- The **vertical lifting** is the non-linear mapping $\phi(x)$ to feature space $\mathcal{F}$.
- The **flat cardboard** is the linear hyperplane in higher dimensions.
- The **"Kernel Trick"** is the magic that lets you find this separating sheet **without physically lifting the marbles** — you just compute distances directly using a kernel formula.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Constants and Hyperparameters used in Non-linear SVM:**
> - **$\gamma$ (Gamma):** Kernel coefficient for RBF, polynomial, and sigmoid kernels.
> - **$C$ (Regularization Parameter):** Trade-off between margin maximization and classification error (soft margin). Default range: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{3}$.
> - **Degree ($d$):** Polynomial degree for polynomial kernels. Default = **3**.
> - **$\varepsilon$ (Tolerance):** Numerical stopping criterion for the SMO optimization solver. Default = **$10^{-3}$**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Linear SVM Fails on Non-linear Data

In the linear case, the decision boundary is constrained to be a hyperplane of the form $w^T x + b = 0$. For datasets where classes are arranged in **concentric circles, XOR patterns, or spiral shapes**, no such hyperplane exists. Linear SVM yields an accuracy close to random guessing on such data.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Non-linear SVM

### Pillar 1: Explicit Feature Mapping $\phi(x)$
- Map each input vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ to a high-dimensional feature space: $\phi(x) \in \mathcal{F}$.
- Example: For $x = (x_1, x_2) \in \mathbb{R}^2$, a quadratic mapping is:
$$\phi(x) = (1, \sqrt{2}x_1, \sqrt{2}x_2, x_1^2, \sqrt{2}x_1 x_2, x_2^2) \in \mathbb{R}^6$$
- **Problem:** Explicit mapping is computationally expensive (curse of dimensionality).

### Pillar 2: The Dual Formulation Advantage
The linear SVM dual problem depends on training points **only through inner products** $\langle x_i, x_j \rangle$. By replacing this inner product with $K(x_i, x_j)$, we obtain the kernelized dual — without ever computing $\phi$.

### Pillar 3: Mercer's Condition (Valid Kernels)
A function $K: \mathbb{R}^d \times \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a valid kernel if and only if:
1. It is **symmetric**: $K(x_i, x_j) = K(x_j, x_i)$.
2. The **Gram matrix** $G_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ is **positive semi-definite** for any finite dataset.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following table contains all high-yield formulas for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Non-linear SVM.

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Kernel Function Definition | $K(x_i, x_j) = \langle \phi(x_i), \phi(x_j) \rangle_{\mathcal{F}}$ | Avoids explicit feature mapping |
| Linear Kernel (baseline) | $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i^T x_j$ | Reduces to standard linear SVM |
| Polynomial Kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = (\gamma \, x_i^T x_j + r)^d$ | Image processing, text classification |
| RBF / Gaussian Kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^2)$ | Most popular, infinite-dimensional features |
| Sigmoid Kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = \tanh(\gamma \, x_i^T x_j + r)$ | Neural network analog (not always Mercer-valid) |
| Kernelized Dual Objective | $\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$ | Optimization target in feature space |
| Constraints (Dual) | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ | Box constraints from soft margin |
| Kernelized Decision Function | $f(x) = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i K(x_i, x) + b^*$ | Prediction at test time |
| Margin Width in Feature Space | $\text{margin} = \frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ where $w = \sum \alpha_i y_i \phi(x_i)$ | Geometric interpretation |
| RBF Bandwidth | $\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}$ | Controls locality of influence |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics:** Protein classification and cancer subtype detection (RBF kernel dominant).
- **Computer Vision:** Face recognition, handwritten digit recognition (MNIST benchmarks).
- **Geophysics:** Seismic event classification with non-linearly separable fault signals.
- **Text & NLP:** Sentiment classification with polynomial or string kernels.
- **Industrial Fault Diagnosis:** Bearing defect detection in rotating machinery using time-series kernels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Linear SVM Primal to Kernelized Dual

### Step 1 — Linear SVM Primal (Soft Margin)
The starting point is the soft-margin linear SVM optimization problem:

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to the constraints:

$$y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}$$

**Logic:** The first term maximizes the geometric margin $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$, and the second term penalizes misclassifications via slack variables $\xi_i$.

### Step 2 — Construct the Lagrangian
Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for the margin constraints and $\mu_i \geq 0$ for the non-negativity constraints on $\xi_i$:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i \xi_i$$

### Step 3 — Apply KKT Stationarity Conditions
Take partial derivatives and set them to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 0 \implies w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = 0 \implies C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \implies 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back to Obtain the Dual
Substituting $w = \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i x_i$ back into the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j)$$

Therefore, the dual optimization problem becomes:

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^T x_j$$

subject to: $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.

### Step 5 — The Kernel Trick Substitution
**Critical observation:** The dual problem depends on training points **only through the inner product** $x_i^T x_j$. We now perform the central substitution:

$$x_i^T x_j \quad \longmapsto \quad K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$$

This yields the **Kernelized Dual**:

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(x_i, x_j)$$

subject to: $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.

### Step 6 — Kernelized Decision Function
The optimal weight vector in feature space is $w^* = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i^* y_i \phi(x_i)$. The decision function for a test point $x$ is:

$$f(x) = \text{sign}\left( (w^*)^T \phi(x) + b^* \right) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i^* y_i \, \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x) + b^* \right)$$

Applying the kernel substitution:

$$f(x) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i \, K(x_i, x) + b^* \right)$$

Note that the summation runs only over **support vectors** (where $\alpha_i > 0$), making prediction efficient.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Verification: Polynomial Kernel Expansion

Let $x = (x_1, x_2)$ and $z = (z_1, z_2)$. Define the quadratic feature map:

$$\phi(x) = (x_1^2, \sqrt{2} \, x_1 x_2, x_2^2)$$

**Step A:** Compute the inner product in feature space:

$$\phi(x)^T \phi(z) = x_1^2 z_1^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 z_1 z_2 + x_2^2 z_2^2$$

**Step B:** Recognize the binomial expansion:

$$\phi(x)^T \phi(z) = (x_1 z_1 + x_2 z_2)^2 = (x^T z)^2$$

**Step C:** Therefore, the corresponding kernel is:

$$K(x, z) = (x^T z)^2$$

This confirms that the kernel $K(x,z) = (x^T z)^2$ implicitly computes the inner product in a 3-dimensional feature space **without ever computing $\phi$**.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Kernel Selection

| Scenario | Recommended Kernel | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $N \gg d$ (many samples, few features) | Linear or RBF | Avoids overfitting; RBF for non-linearity |
| $N \approx d$ | RBF or Polynomial | Balances flexibility and complexity |
| $N \ll d$ (few samples, many features) | Linear | Prevents curse of dimensionality |
| Known polynomial structure | Polynomial kernel ($d \leq 5$) | Encodes prior knowledge |
| No prior knowledge | RBF (default) | Universal approximator properties |
| Need probabilistic output | Any kernel + Platt scaling | Calibrated probabilities |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Non-linear SVM Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Data X in R^d] --> B[Feature Space Mapping phi X]
    B --> C[High Dimensional Feature Space F]
    C --> D[Optimal Separating Hyperplane in F]
    D --> E[Compute Margin and Support Vectors]
    E --> F[Kernelized Decision Function f x]
    G[Kernel Function K xi xj] -.-> B
    G -.-> D
    G -.-> F
    H[Training Labels yi] --> D
    H --> F
    I[New Test Point x test] --> F
    F --> J[Predicted Class Label sign f x]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Flow: Kernel Trick Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT SPACE R2"]
        X1[Red Class Points]
        X2[Blue Class Points]
        X1 --- X2
    end
    subgraph TRICK["KERNEL TRICK"]
        K1[Compute K xi xj directly]
        K2[Avoid explicit phi computation]
        K1 --> K2
    end
    subgraph FEATURE["FEATURE SPACE R3 or higher"]
        F1[Lifted Points]
        F2[Linear Hyperplane separator]
        F1 --> F2
    end
    INPUT --> TRICK
    TRICK --> FEATURE
```

## 4.3 SVM Training Workflow with Kernel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Load Dataset] --> S2[Choose Kernel Type and Parameters]
    S2 --> S3[Construct Kernel Matrix K_ij]
    S3 --> S4[Solve Kernelized Dual using SMO]
    S4 --> S5[Extract alpha star and b star]
    S5 --> S6{Identify Support Vectors}
    S6 --> S7[alpha i greater than 0]
    S7 --> S8[Store SVs and Their alpha values]
    S8 --> S9[End Training: Ready for Prediction]
```

## 4.4 Kernel Function Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    KROOT[Kernels K] --> KL[Linear]
    KROOT --> KP[Polynomial]
    KROOT --> KRBF[RBF Gaussian]
    KROOT --> KS[Sigmoid]
    KROOT --> KC[Custom Mercer Kernels]
    KP --> KP1[Degree 2]
    KP --> KP2[Degree 3]
    KP --> KP3[Higher Degrees]
    KRBF --> KR1[Localized influence]
    KRBF --> KR2[Infinite dimensional]
    KS --> KS1[Neural network analogy]
    KS --> KS2[Not always PSD]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the kernel trick in SVM. Why is it preferred over explicit feature mapping?**

**Model Answer:**
The **kernel trick** is a mathematical technique that allows a Support Vector Machine to operate in a high-dimensional feature space **without ever explicitly computing the coordinates** of the data in that space. It replaces the inner product $x_i^T x_j$ in the dual formulation with a kernel function $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$.

It is preferred over explicit feature mapping because:
1. **Computational Efficiency:** Explicitly computing $\phi(x)$ for high-dimensional mappings (e.g., degree-10 polynomial on 100 features yields billions of features) is intractable.
2. **Memory Efficiency:** Only the $N \times N$ kernel matrix needs to be stored, not the transformed features.
3. **No Knowledge of $\phi$:** For kernels like RBF, the feature space is infinite-dimensional and $\phi$ cannot be written explicitly.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Mercer's theorem and list any two conditions for a function to be a valid kernel.**

**Model Answer:**
**Mercer's Theorem:** A continuous, symmetric function $K(x, z)$ defined on a domain can be expressed as an inner product in some high-dimensional feature space $K(x, z) = \phi(x)^T \phi(z)$ if and only if the Gram matrix $G$ formed by $G_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ is **positive semi-definite** for any finite set of inputs $\{x_1, \ldots, x_N\}$.

**Two Conditions for a Valid Kernel:**
1. **Symmetry:** $K(x, z) = K(z, x)$ for all $x, z$.
2. **Positive Semi-Definiteness:** The Gram matrix $G$ satisfies $v^T G v \geq 0$ for all real vectors $v$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the dual optimization problem for a soft-margin linear SVM starting from the primal. Show explicitly how the kernel function $K(x_i, x_j)$ replaces the inner product $x_i^T x_j$ to obtain the kernelized dual. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel** with its mathematical form. Discuss the effect of the parameter $\gamma$ on model complexity. Why is the RBF kernel considered a universal approximator? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Primal Problem:** Starting from the soft-margin linear SVM:

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

**Step 1:** Form the Lagrangian with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \alpha_i [y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i$$

**Step 2:** KKT stationarity gives $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ and $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$, with $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.

**Step 3:** Substituting $w$ into the Lagrangian yields the dual:

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j)$$

**Step 4 — Kernel Substitution:** Replace $x_i^T x_j$ with $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$:

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$$

subject to $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.

The decision function becomes $f(x) = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i K(x_i, x) + b^*$.

#### Part (b) — RBF Kernel Analysis (7 marks)

The **RBF (Gaussian) kernel** is defined as:

$$K(x_i, x_j) = \exp\left(-\gamma \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^2\right), \quad \gamma > 0$$

**Effect of $\gamma$:**
- **Large $\gamma$ (e.g., $\gamma = 10$):** Kernel value drops sharply as distance increases. Each training point has **localized influence** only. This leads to **high model complexity**, possible **overfitting**, and a highly wiggly decision boundary.
- **Small $\gamma$ (e.g., $\gamma = 0.001$):** Kernel value decays slowly; influence is far-reaching. This leads to **low model complexity**, smoother decision boundary, and possible **underfitting**.

**Universal Approximator Property:** The RBF kernel is a universal approximator because:
1. Its Taylor series expansion involves **all polynomial degrees** of the input features, so the corresponding feature space is **infinite-dimensional**.
2. For any continuous function on a compact domain, there exists an RBF-kernel SVM that approximates it to arbitrary accuracy (Micchelli's theorem).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Exam 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram and derivation, show that $K(x, z) = (x^T z)^2$ is a valid kernel corresponding to a specific feature map $\phi(x)$. Identify the feature space dimensionality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Linear, Polynomial, RBF, and Sigmoid** kernels in terms of mathematical form, feature space dimensionality, hyperparameter sensitivity, and typical use cases. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Polynomial Kernel Validity (7 marks)

Consider $x = (x_1, x_2, x_3)$ and $z = (z_1, z_2, z_3)$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$. Define the feature map:

$$\phi(x) = (x_1^2, x_2^2, x_3^2, \sqrt{2} x_1 x_2, \sqrt{2} x_1 x_3, \sqrt{2} x_2 x_3) \in \mathbb{R}^6$$

Compute the inner product in feature space:

$$\phi(x)^T \phi(z) = x_1^2 z_1^2 + x_2^2 z_2^2 + x_3^2 z_3^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 z_1 z_2 + 2 x_1 x_3 z_1 z_3 + 2 x_2 x_3 z_2 z_3$$

This is exactly the binomial square expansion:

$$\phi(x)^T \phi(z) = (x_1 z_1 + x_2 z_2 + x_3 z_3)^2 = (x^T z)^2$$

Therefore, $K(x, z) = (x^T z)^2$ is a valid kernel, and the feature space dimensionality is **6** (for $d = 3$ inputs, polynomial of degree 2 gives $\binom{d+2}{2} = 10$ total monomial terms including the constant).

#### Part (b) — Kernel Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Kernel | Mathematical Form | Feature Space Dim. | Hyperparameters | Typical Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linear** | $K(x,z) = x^T z$ | $d$ (original) | None | High-dimensional sparse data (text) |
| **Polynomial** | $K(x,z) = (\gamma x^T z + r)^d$ | $\binom{d+p}{p}$ (finite) | $\gamma, r, d$ | Image processing, normalized data |
| **RBF** | $K(x,z) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert x - z \Vert^2)$ | Infinite | $\gamma$ | Default for non-linear problems |
| **Sigmoid** | $K(x,z) = \tanh(\gamma x^T z + r)$ | Not always valid (Mercer) | $\gamma, r$ | Neural network analog |

**Discussion:**
- **Linear** is the fastest but cannot handle non-linear boundaries.
- **Polynomial** allows control over interaction terms but is sensitive to degree.
- **RBF** is the most flexible, with a single hyperparameter $\gamma$, but is computationally expensive for large $N$.
- **Sigmoid** is inspired by neural networks but does not always satisfy Mercer's condition (Gram matrix may not be PSD).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing $\phi(x)$ with $K(x, z)$:** Students often write $K(x, z) = \phi(x) \phi(z)$ instead of $K(x, z) = \phi(x)^T \phi(z)$. Always include the **transpose** for the inner product.
> 2. **Forgetting KKT box constraints:** When writing the kernelized dual, students omit $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ or $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$. This is a **2-mark deduction** per constraint missed.
> 3. **Sigmoid kernel validity:** Many students wrongly claim the sigmoid kernel is always a valid Mercer kernel. State clearly: it is **conditionally valid** (depends on $\gamma$ and $r$).
> 4. **Mixing primal and dual:** The kernel trick applies **only to the dual formulation**, not the primal. Writing $K$ in the primal is incorrect and will lose marks.
> 5. **$\gamma$ interpretation:** Reversing the effect of large vs. small $\gamma$ on the RBF kernel. **Memorize:** large $\gamma$ → narrow kernel → overfitting.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Idea:** Non-linear SVM uses the **kernel trick** to operate in a high-dimensional feature space without computing $\phi$ explicitly.
- **Key Formula:** $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$ replaces $x_i^T x_j$ in the **dual** objective.
- **Most Popular Kernel:** RBF (Gaussian) — single hyperparameter $\gamma$, infinite-dimensional feature space.
- **Mercer's Theorem:** Symmetry + Positive Semi-Definite Gram Matrix = valid kernel.
- **Decision Function:** $f(x) = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i^* y_i K(x_i, x) + b^*$ — only support vectors contribute.
- **Dual Constraints:** $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.
- **Common Kernels:** Linear, Polynomial, RBF, Sigmoid (and custom string/graph kernels for specialized tasks).
- **Tuning Trade-off:** Large $C$ → low bias, high variance (overfitting); Small $C$ → high bias, low variance (underfitting).
- **$\gamma$ Trade-off:** Large $\gamma$ → tight fit (overfitting); Small $\gamma$ → smooth fit (underfitting).
- **Solver:** SMO (Sequential Minimal Optimization) is the standard quadratic programming solver used in libraries like `sklearn.svm.SVC` and `LIBSVM`.
- **Computational Cost:** Kernelized SVM has training cost $O(N^2)$ to $O(N^3)$; inference cost is $O(N_{SV} \cdot d)$.
- **Scaling Required:** Kernel methods (especially RBF and polynomial) are **sensitive to feature scaling** — always standardize inputs using StandardScaler.
- **Sigmoid Caveat:** Not a true Mercer kernel for all $\gamma, r$ — can lead to non-convex optimization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kernels for Learning Non-linear Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT742 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kernels for Learning Non-linear Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KERNEL METHOD (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *kernel* is a function $K(x, x')$ that computes the inner product between two data points $x$ and $x'$ in some (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature space $\mathcal{F}$, without ever having to explicitly compute the coordinates of the data in that space. Formally,
> $$K(x, x') = \langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle_{\mathcal{F}}$$
> where $\phi : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{F}$ is the *feature map*. This indirect computation is known as the **kernel trick** (Schölkopf & Smola, 2002).

In the context of **learning non-linear functions**, kernels enable linear learning algorithms (e.g., Support Vector Machines, Ridge Regression, Perceptron) to operate in a high-dimensional feature space where the decision boundary becomes linear, while remaining computationally efficient in the original input space.

### 1.1.1 Why Kernels are Needed

Many real-world classification/regression problems (e.g., XOR, spiral, image segmentation) are **not linearly separable** in the original input space $\mathbb{R}^d$. To handle such data:

1. **Explicit mapping** $\phi(x)$ to a higher-dimensional space $\mathcal{F}$ where the problem becomes linear.
2. **Direct computation** of $\phi(x)$ is often infeasible (curse of dimensionality).
3. **Kernel trick** computes $\langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle$ *implicitly* using a simple function $K(x, x')$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Concert Hall" Intuition**
>
> Imagine you are at a crowded concert hall trying to identify whether two people are "similar" (e.g., share musical taste). Instead of dragging both people through a long questionnaire ($\phi(x)$ explicitly), you simply ask each to hum a tune and listen for harmonic overlap. The *correlation of the hums* — a simple one-number answer — is your **kernel**. The hidden infinite-dimensional "taste-space" is never enumerated; the similarity is computed directly.
>
> Similarly, kernels let algorithms reason about similarity in a transformed, ultra-rich feature space without ever leaving the comfort of the original data.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Feature Map | $\phi(x)$ | Maps input $x \in \mathcal{X}$ to feature space $\mathcal{F}$ |
| Kernel Function | $K(x, x')$ | Inner product in feature space: $\langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle$ |
| Gram Matrix | $\mathbf{K}$ | $n \times n$ matrix with $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ |
| Positive Semi-Definite | PSD | Matrix $M$ with $v^\top M v \geq 0, \forall v$ |
| Mercer's Condition | — | Continuous, symmetric kernel is valid iff PSD |
| Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space | RKHS | Function space with inner product $\langle f, K(\cdot, x) \rangle = f(x)$ |

## 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping for this Module:**
> - **CO4:** *Apply kernel methods to enable non-linear extensions of linear learning algorithms and analyze their generalization properties.*
> - **Bloom's Level:** L3 (Apply) → L4 (Analyze)
> - **Estimated Self-Study Time:** 4 hours
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Dual Role of Inner Products

A linear classifier in feature space has the form:

$$f(x) = w^\top \phi(x) + b, \quad w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \phi(x_i)$$

Substituting $w$ into $f(x)$:

$$f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \underbrace{\langle \phi(x_i), \phi(x) \rangle}_{K(x_i, x)} + b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i K(x_i, x) + b$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The decision function depends on the data **only through inner products** (kernels). The explicit coordinates $\phi(x)$ never appear. This is the *Representer Theorem* in action.

## 2.2 Construction Logic — Why Each Step Matters

- **Step 1 — Problem is not linearly separable in $\mathcal{X}$.**
  *Why:* Real data (XOR, spirals, etc.) cannot be separated by a hyperplane in low dimensions.
  *How:* We attempt separation in a richer space $\mathcal{F}$.

- **Step 2 — Choose a feature map $\phi : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{F}$.**
  *Why:* To linearize the relationship.
  *How:* E.g., $\phi([x_1, x_2]^\top) = [x_1^2, \sqrt{2}x_1 x_2, x_2^2]^\top$.

- **Step 3 — Observe that the algorithm uses only $\langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle$.**
  *Why:* The dual formulation rewrites the problem using inner products.
  *How:* Replace every inner product with a cheap function $K(x, x')$.

- **Step 4 — Verify $K$ is a valid (Mercer) kernel.**
  *Why:* Not every function is a valid inner product; invalid $K$ breaks convexity.
  *How:* Check PSD property of the Gram matrix for all training sets.

## 2.3 The Four Pillars of Kernel Methods

1. **Mercer's Theorem** — characterizes valid kernels.
2. **Kernel Trick** — bypasses explicit feature mapping.
3. **Gram Matrix** — encodes all pairwise similarities.
4. **RKHS Theory** — guarantees existence of a function space.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE FOLLOWING TABLE IS ESSENTIAL FOR THE KTU UNIVERSITY EXAM. Memorize all entries.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Kernel Definition | $K(x, x') = \langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle$ | Scalar value |
| 2 | Symmetry | $K(x, x') = K(x', x)$ | Required property |
| 3 | Positive Semi-Definiteness | $\sum_{i,j} c_i c_j K(x_i, x_j) \geq 0$ | Mercer's condition |
| 4 | Gram Matrix | $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ | Symmetric $n \times n$ |
| 5 | Linear Kernel | $K(x, x') = x^\top x'$ | Identity feature map |
| 6 | Polynomial Kernel (homogeneous) | $K(x, x') = (x^\top x')^p$ | Degree $p \in \mathbb{N}$ |
| 7 | Polynomial Kernel (inhomogeneous) | $K(x, x') = (\gamma x^\top x' + c)^p$ | $\gamma > 0$, $c \geq 0$ |
| 8 | Gaussian / RBF Kernel | $K(x, x') = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert\vert x - x' \vert\vert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\sigma > 0$, *infinite-dim* |
| 9 | Sigmoid Kernel | $K(x, x') = \tanh(\gamma x^\top x' + c)$ | Not always PSD |
| 10 | Laplacian Kernel | $K(x, x') = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert\vert x - x' \vert\vert_1}{\sigma}\right)$ | L1 distance |
| 11 | Kernel Ridge Regression (dual) | $\alpha = (\mathbf{K} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} y$ | Closed form |
| 12 | Kernel SVM Prediction | $f(x) = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b$ | Only SVs contribute |
| 13 | Representer Theorem | $w^* = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \phi(x_i)$ | Always finite expansion |
| 14 | Kernel Composition | $K = K_1 + K_2$, $K = c \cdot K_1$ (c > 0), $K = K_1 \cdot K_2$ | Closure properties |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Kernels power **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** for face recognition, **Gaussian Processes** for Bayesian regression, **Kernel PCA** for nonlinear dimensionality reduction, and **String Kernels** for bioinformatics (e.g., protein classification).

## 2.5 Mercer's Theorem — Deep Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MERCER'S THEOREM (1909):**
> A continuous, symmetric function $K(x, x')$ on a compact domain $\mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X}$ admits an expansion
> $$K(x, x') = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \lambda_k \psi_k(x) \psi_k(x')$$
> with $\lambda_k \geq 0$ (non-negative eigenvalues) if and only if for any square-integrable $f$,
> $$\iint f(x) K(x, x') f(x') \, dx \, dx' \geq 0$$
> i.e., the kernel is **positive semi-definite**. The functions $\{\psi_k\}$ form an orthonormal basis of $\mathcal{F}$, and the *feature map* is $\phi(x) = (\sqrt{\lambda_1}\psi_1(x), \sqrt{\lambda_2}\psi_2(x), \ldots)$.
